128.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TcI is "also frequent in domestic cycles" and is the "dominant DTU responsible for the transmission of Chagas disease" in parts of the endemic area, contradicting the claim of absence from domestic cycles in Central America. Moreover, Central America is reported to have TcI identified. There is therefore no support for a host-specific barrier preventing TcI transmission to humans; the text points to TcI's broad presence in both sylvatic and domestic cycles and its role in human transmission.

128.Option: The exclusive association of TcV and TcVI with domestic cycles in Southern Cone countries, despite their absence in similar environments in North and Central America, suggests a region-specific ecological barrier limiting their spread
Evidence: These DTUs showed the most differential geographical distribution. Indeed, TcV was identified in North America in exceptional cases in Mexico (Veracruz) in T. dimidiata as well as above-mentioned TcII [29]. TcV and TcVI have never been identified in US in 148 determinations, nor in Central America in 120 cases. In contrast, in South America, these DTUs together have frequently been identified in several countries, Argentina (76.9%), Bolivia (44.6%), Chile (28.6%) and Paraguay (55.2%)—but very rarely in others such as in Colombia (1.1%) [24, 39–41], Ecuador (3.3%) [42], and Brazil (1.5%).
Both DTUs are clearly associated with domestic cycles since only 10.5% of them are identified in hosts from wild cycles.

128.Rationale: The passage documents that TcV and TcVI are largely confined to the Southern Cone and Bolivia and are "clearly associated with domestic cycles" (only 10.5% in wild cycles), while being essentially absent from the US and Central America despite sampling. This geographic and cycle-restricted pattern is consistent with a region-specific ecological barrier (environmental, vector-host network or anthropogenic factors) limiting their spread beyond certain ecological niches. The evidence supports the inference that the distribution reflects ecological/region-specific constraints rather than uniform dispersal, making this option the best-supported interpretation among those presented.

128.Option: The consistent detection of TcII in domestic cycles across South America, despite its absence in North and Central America, suggests a host immune barrier that prevents its establishment in human populations outside South America
Evidence: TcII was much more rarely identified (9.6% of overall DTUs identified). It was not identified in Central America out of 120 identifications, and only 13 identifications were reported from North America out of 459 (2.8%).
For now, its geographical distribution is more consistent with a South American origin, and further south than north of the Amazon basin where this DTU is more abundant.

This DTU presents a much lower genetic diversity than TcI. For example, the haplotype diversity of COII and CytB genes are 0.39 and 0.48, while for mtTcI they are 0.81 and 0.58 respectively [8]. In humans, it is relatively abundant, accounting for 20% of human strains, but it is highly abundant in Brazil (66% of human strains identifications) and rare in most other countries except Colombia (15%) and Chile (30%).
128.Rationale: While the passage documents that TcII is more common in South America and rare in North/Central America, it provides no evidence that this pattern is due to a "host immune barrier" preventing establishment in human populations outside South America. In fact, the passage notes TcII is found in humans ("accounting for 20% of human strains" overall and 66% in Brazil), showing it readily infects humans where present. The text instead suggests geographic origin, ecological distribution, and historical/biogeographic reasons for its pattern. 129.Option: The continued occurrence of neonatal tetanus is due to the ineffectiveness of the tetanus toxoid vaccine in preventing infection, as evidenced by the lack of significant association between maternal vaccination and survival

maternal tetanus toxoid vaccination was also not significantly associated with survival in this study unlike reports from some previous studies, this probably may be because none of the mothers in this study completed the two doses of vaccination in pregnancy.
129.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes TT vaccination as the easiest, cost-effective preventive measure and states three doses provide multi-year protection. The reported lack of association between maternal vaccination and survival in this study is attributed to incomplete dosing (none completed two doses), not to vaccine ineffectiveness. Therefore asserting that vaccine ineffectiveness explains persistence is not supported by the passage.

129.Option: Persistent transmission is driven by a convergence of structural inequities, cultural practices, and gaps in preventive healthcare access, despite overall declining incidence
Evidence: NNT results from unhygienic delivery practices and some harmful traditional cord care practices which include cutting the cord with non-sterile equipment such as razor blade; application of shea butter, mentholatum containing balms and animal dung to the cord [3].

Majority of the affected babies in Nigeria are delivered either at home, faith (mission) based homes, with untrained or poorly trained traditional birth attendants as only 43% of deliveries are attended by skilled birth attendant as revealed by the 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS) report [4].

About a third of the babies presented with inability to suck or spasms while about a quarter of them had fever at presentation.

129.Rationale: The passage repeatedly links persistent NNT to structural inequities (poverty, underserved populations), cultural/harmful cord-care practices (application of shea butter, mentholatum, nonsterile cutting), high rates of deliveries outside skilled care, low antenatal attendance and under-vaccination, and underreporting. This convergence of social, cultural, and healthcare access gaps is the most consistent explanation for ongoing transmission despite declining incidence.

129.Option: The persistence of neonatal tetanus is due to the high rate of home deliveries in rural areas, which are not adequately monitored by public health surveillance systems
Evidence: Majority of the affected babies in Nigeria are delivered either at home, faith (mission) based homes, with untrained or poorly trained traditional birth attendants as only 43% of deliveries are attended by skilled birth attendant as revealed by the 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS) report [4].
About 70% of the babies were delivered outside health facilities most likely without skilled birth attendants and under unhygienic conditions which agrees with the 2018 NDHS [4] report.
NNT is under reported in Nigeria with only about 5% of cases reporting to health facilities [13].

129.Rationale: The passage documents a high proportion of deliveries occurring outside formal health facilities (home, mission homes, TBAs) and explicitly states extreme underreporting of NNT cases. Those facts support that high home-delivery rates combined with inadequate surveillance/health-system capture contribute importantly to persistent occurrence.

129.Option: The persistence of neonatal tetanus is primarily due to the high prevalence of comorbidities such as anemia, hypoglycemia, and sepsis, which increase susceptibility to infection
Evidence: 5 [26.3%] of the babies had low blood sugar (hypoglycaemia) at presentation while 7 [36.8%] of them had anaemia at presentation.

129.Rationale: While comorbidities (anemia, hypoglycaemia, sepsis) are common among cases and worsen prognosis, the passage attributes the root cause of NNT persistence to delivery and cord-care practices, poor antenatal care/vaccination, and structural factors. Thus comorbidities explain severity and mortality but are not presented as the primary driver of ongoing transmission.

129.Option: Neonatal tetanus persists because of the widespread use of unapproved medications such as shea butter and mentholatum, which directly introduce Clostridium tetani into the umbilical stump
Evidence: NNT results from unhygienic delivery practices and some harmful traditional cord care practices which include cutting the cord with non-sterile equipment such as razor blade; application of shea butter, mentholatum containing balms and animal dung to the cord [3].

Of all the patients in this study, only 1 (5.2%) claimed to have used only methylated spirit for the care of her baby’s umbilical cord, about 50% used hot water compress as cord care method and about 20% each applied shea butter or mentholatum to the cord stump.

129.Rationale: The passage does identify application of shea butter and mentholatum as harmful cord-care practices that can encourage infection and notes the umbilicus as the portal of entry. However, claiming these substances directly and solely introduce C. tetani overstates the evidence; the text describes a range of unhygienic practices (non-sterile cutting, hot compresses, delivery outside skilled care) and structural issues that together explain persistence. Thus the option is too narrow and not fully supported as the primary explanation.

130.Option: Enhanced phagocytic activity of macrophages due to increased expression of FPR2 receptors on immune cells
Evidence: Previous studies have shown that the anti-inflammatory and pro-resolving properties of AT-RvD1 are primarily mediated by FPR2 [49].

In our model, FPR2 participates in regulating pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory cytokines (IFNγ and IL-1Β) and reducing cellular infiltrates because its absence weakens the effect produced by AT-RvD1.

130.Rationale: The passage supports that AT-RvD1 signals through FPR2 (and also possibly GPR32) and that effects are blunted in FPR2-/- mice, but it does not state or provide evidence that AT-RvD1 increases expression of FPR2 on immune cells. The option asserts an increase in receptor expression (enhanced FPR2 expression) as the mechanism; that specific claim is unsupported by the text. The passage indicates receptor-mediated action is required for some effects but does not describe upregulation of FPR2 as the mechanism for parasite clearance.

Evidence: Importantly, AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal activity.
However, we observed that AT-RvD1 alone or combined with Bz reduced the heart parasite load in infected mice.
130.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal activity, so a mechanism invoking direct inhibition of parasite replication (e.g., via mitochondrial suppression in T. cruzi) contradicts the reported properties of the agent. 130.Option: Resolution of chronic inflammation restores immune system function, enabling clearance of persistent parasites that otherwise evade detection due to an immunosuppressive environment
Evidence: Importantly, AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal activity. However, we observed that AT-RvD1 alone or combined with Bz reduced the heart parasite load in infected mice.

we cannot exclude the fact that the observed clearance is the result of resolvin-induced efferocytosis [72].

130.Option: Upregulation of parasite-specific antibodies that enhance opsonization and complement-mediated lysis
Evidence: After infection in mammals, T. cruzi induces robust innate and adaptive immune responses, which play a significant role during the acute and chronic phases of the disease.

we cannot exclude the fact that the observed clearance is the result of resolvin-induced efferocytosis [72].
130.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges adaptive responses and the participation of B cells, it does not present evidence that AT-RvD1 increases parasite-specific antibody production or that antibody-mediated opsonization/complement lysis explains the reduced cardiac parasite load. Instead, the authors attribute clearance to resolution of inflammation and possible efferocytosis; there is no data or statement supporting upregulation of parasite-specific antibodies by AT-RvD1.

130.Option: Increased production of reactive oxygen species by neutrophils leading to oxidative damage of parasite membranes
Evidence: AT-RvD1 is a pro-resolving lipid mediator effective against inflammation, dampening pathological inflammatory responses and restoring tissue homeostasis; importantly, it contributes to clearing adverse inflammatory environments and allowing more efficient parasite elimination, with the participation of FPR2, at least partially.
However, Bz could also increase reactive oxygen species production at the tissue level [60,61], contributing to the production and establishment of a pro-inflammatory environment.

130.Rationale: The passage associates increased reactive oxygen species (ROS) production with benznidazole (Bz), not with AT-RvD1. AT-RvD1 is described as anti-inflammatory and pro-resolving and is reported to reduce inflammatory infiltrates, making an ROS-mediated neutrophil killing mechanism unlikely and unsupported by the text. The authors instead favor resolution-mediated mechanisms (e.g., efferocytosis, restored immune function) rather than increased oxidative killing by neutrophils.

131.Option: The presence of anti-ZIKV IgG-NS1 antibodies in mothers is a stronger predictor of microcephaly than anti-DENV IgG-NS1 levels, as it reflects recent or active infection during pregnancy
Evidence: When we evaluated the relative levels of IgG-NS1, we observed significantly lower levels of DENV, but not ZIKV, antibodies in mothers of children with microcephaly relative to those without microcephaly (Fig 4B).
this comparison was not performed for ZIKV IgG-NS1 since positivity for this test was a study inclusion criterion.

131.Rationale: The passage reports that mothers of children with microcephaly had lower DENV (but not ZIKV) IgG-NS1 levels and explicitly notes that comparison for ZIKV IgG-NS1 was not performed because ZIKV IgG-NS1 positivity was an inclusion criterion. The study therefore identifies low DENV IgG-NS1 (or a lower DENV/ZIKV ratio) as associated with microcephaly, not that maternal anti-ZIKV IgG-NS1 is a stronger predictor.

Evidence: This analysis indicated that mothers of children with microcephaly had a lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in comparison with those without microcephaly (Fig 4C).

In conclusion, here we show that the development of microcephaly in children with CZS is associated with low plasma levels of placentally transferred, pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies.
Thus, pre-existing immunity directed to the NS1 protein of orthoflaviviruses seems to play a protective role against ZIKV infection and CZS-related microcephaly.
131.Rationale: The passage directly reports that a lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in mothers and children is associated with microcephaly and that higher pre-existing DENV NS1 IgG is associated with reduced risk. The authors interpret this as evidence that pre-existing dengue (DENV) NS1 immunity may be protective against severe congenital Zika syndrome.

131.Option: The use of hydroxychloroquine and doxycycline in dengue patients may alter anti-NS1 antibody levels, confounding the association between DENV immunity and microcephaly risk
Evidence: Our study has some limitations. We did not prospectively measure antibody responses in pregnant women early after ZIKV exposure, which could be better correlated with clinical outcomes in newborns.
In conclusion, here we show that the development of microcephaly in children with CZS is associated with low plasma levels of placentally transferred, pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies.
131.Rationale: The passage makes no mention of hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline, or use of these drugs in dengue patients; there is no evidence presented that such treatments altered anti-NS1 antibody levels or confounded the association. The study attributes associations to antibody levels and acknowledges other limitations (e.g., timing of sampling) but does not discuss drug-related confounding, so this option introduces an unsubstantiated factor not supported by the text.

131.Option: Higher levels of anti-DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies in mothers are associated with increased risk of microcephaly due to enhanced antibody-dependent enhancement of Zika virus infection
Evidence: When we evaluated the relative levels of IgG-NS1, we observed significantly lower levels of DENV, but not ZIKV, antibodies in mothers of children with microcephaly relative to those without microcephaly (Fig 4B).
In conclusion, here we show that the development of microcephaly in children with CZS is associated with low plasma levels of placentally transferred, pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies.

In addition, anti-ZIKV NS1 antibodies have no risk of antibody-dependent enhancement of infection [20,24], highlighting their role in antibody-mediated protection against disease and as an optimal target in vaccine development.
131.Rationale: The study finds the opposite association (low, not high, maternal DENV NS1 IgG levels associate with microcephaly). Furthermore, the passage specifically states that anti-NS1 antibodies do not pose a risk of antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE). Thus the option claiming higher anti-DENV NS1 leads to increased risk via ADE contradicts both the observed association and the described biology of NS1 antibodies.

131.Option: The lack of correlation between DENV and ZIKV IgG-NS1 levels in mothers suggests that pre-existing dengue immunity has no role in modulating Zika virus outcomes in pregnancy
Evidence: Indeed, we did not observe a correlation between ZIKV and DENV IgG-NS1 antibody levels in plasma from mothers (rho = −0.05; p = 0.45) or children (rho = 0.004; p = 0.95).
In conclusion, here we show that the development of microcephaly in children with CZS is associated with low plasma levels of placentally transferred, pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies.

131.Rationale: Although DENV and ZIKV IgG-NS1 levels were not correlated (i.e., they vary independently), the study nevertheless demonstrates an association between low maternal DENV NS1 IgG and increased microcephaly risk. Lack of correlation between the two antibody measures does not imply absence of an effect of DENV immunity; the authors interpret the data as evidence that pre-existing DENV NS1 immunity may be protective.

132.Option: The insertion of mCherry into the E3-E2 junction alters the cleavage efficiency of the furin protease, resulting in prolonged exposure of the antigen in its precursor form and enhancing antigen presentation to B cells
Evidence: Insertion of mCherry between E3 and E2 might slow down the furin cleavage of E3 because the parental CHIKV 37997 virus contains very little uncleaved E3-E2 (p62) precursor.

132.Rationale: The passage does report that insertion of mCherry "might slow down the furin cleavage" and identifies precursor bands, so the biochemical effect described in the option is supported. However, the authors do not attribute the sustained high-titer antibody response to prolonged precursor exposure; instead they explicitly point to the high-density, repetitive surface display of mCherry (and viral replication/innate activation) as explanations for strong immunogenicity. Therefore the specific mechanism proposed in this option (prolonged precursor exposure via altered furin cleavage) is not supported as the most likely cause of the observed durable antibody response.

132.Option: Repetitive, high-density display of the foreign antigen on the viral surface enhances B cell activation and drives durable, high-titer antibody responses after a single infection
Evidence: On each chimeric CHIKV 37997-mCherry virion, 240 copies of mCherry are presented in a repetitive pattern between viral spikes (Fig 2).

Consistently, a single infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced long-lasting and high titer antibody responses against mCherry that can only be reached after boosting in mice conventionally immunized with mCherry proteins (Fig 10).
132.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that 240 copies of mCherry are presented in a repetitive array on the virion surface and directly links that high-density display to efficient B cell recognition and activation, with the observed consequence of long-lasting, high-titer antibody responses after a single infection. This matches the option's mechanism and is presented as the primary explanation in the text.

132.Option: The presence of viral RNA replication intermediates during infection strongly activates innate immune responses, leading to enhanced T cell-mediated immunity and sustained antibody production against the foreign antigen
Evidence: Virus replication generates viral RNA replication intermediates and viral RNA that strongly activate host innate immune responses, which may explain the strong immunogenicity of our replication-competent chimeric virus[29].
Consistently, a single infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced long-lasting and high titer antibody responses against mCherry...
132.Rationale: The passage states that viral RNA replication intermediates strongly activate host innate immunity and that this may explain the strong immunogenicity of the replication-competent chimeric virus. While the text emphasizes B cell activation via surface antigen density as a key mechanism, it also explicitly cites innate immune activation by replicating viral RNA as a contributing explanation for the strong antibody responses. The option extends this to T cell–mediated help (plausible immunologically), but the core claim—that replication intermediates stimulate innate responses that enhance antibody production—is supported by the passage.

132.Option: The fusion of mCherry to the E2 glycoprotein enables the antigen to be incorporated into the viral nucleocapsid core, allowing for efficient delivery to antigen-presenting cells and robust B cell activation
Evidence: the resulting chimeric virus presents mCherry on the virion surface in a repetitive pattern and induces strong antibody responses against both CHIKV and mCherry.
mCherry proteins were well exposed, occupying the space between viral spikes on the particle surface (Fig 2D).
240 copies of C form an icosahedral nucleocapsid core enclosing viral genomic RNA within the lipid membrane
132.Rationale: The passage clearly describes mCherry as fused to E2 and presented on the virion surface ("well exposed, occupying the space between viral spikes") rather than being incorporated into the nucleocapsid core. The nucleocapsid core is described separately (240 copies of C forming the core enclosing genomic RNA). There is no support in the text for incorporation of mCherry into the nucleocapsid or for the mechanism stated in the option; instead antigen is surface-displayed, which is the mechanism linked to robust B cell activation.

132.Option: The chimeric virus's ability to persist in host tissues for extended periods provides continuous antigen exposure, which drives prolonged B cell stimulation and high-titer antibody production
Evidence: As we, and other researchers, have reported before [16, 20], CHIKV persists in both the ipsilateral and contralateral ankles after CHIKV 37997 infection. In contrast, viral RNA in the ankles on the contralateral side of CHIKV 37997-mCherry infected mice were not significantly higher than the assay limit of detection, and viral loads in ankles on the ipsilateral side were significantly lower than those in parental CHIKV 37997 infected mice (Fig 8C).
CHIKV 37997-mCherry was attenuated in vivo

The high density of mCherry can be easily recognized by B cells and, therefore, activate B cells efficiently. Consistently, a single infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced long-lasting and high titer antibody responses against mCherry ...
132.Rationale: The passage shows that the parental CHIKV 37997 can persist, but the chimeric CHIKV 37997-mCherry was actually attenuated and had lower/paucity of persistent viral RNA in tissues compared with parental CHIKV 37997. Despite this reduced persistence, CHIKV 37997-mCherry still induced strong, long-lasting antibody responses. The authors therefore attribute the durable antibody response to high-density surface display (and innate activation by replication), not to prolonged tissue persistence. Thus the option that persistence is the driver of sustained antibodies is not supported by the text.

134.Option: Fatigue is a key predictor of severe disease outcomes in individuals with a history of eye worm migration, even when microfilaremia is absent
Evidence: In comparison to LN, each of the two groups with history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) reported several signs and symptoms significantly more often. This was the case for Calabar swelling-like manifestations (adjusted prevalence ratio [95% confidence interval]: (1.40 [1.10–1.79] for EW and 2.05 [1.56–2.69] for EWMF), paresthesia (1.51 [1.19–1.92] and 1.73 [1.32–2.26]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.63 [1.86–3.72] and 1.78 [1.15–2.76]), and fatigue (1.81 [1.43–2.30] and 1.49 [1.12–2.01]).
individuals with high microfilarial densities are known to be at risk for spontaneous or treatment-related severe manifestations and excess mortality [1,7,12,13,18,19].

In summary, the infection states comprising participants with history of eye worm were significantly associated with the occurrence of clinical complaints. Contrarily, microfilaremic individuals reported complaints in a similar proportion as persons without signs of loiasis infection.
134.Rationale: While fatigue was reported more often among those with a history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) compared with LN, the paper does not identify fatigue as a specific predictor of severe outcomes. The study emphasizes that severe manifestations and excess mortality are linked to high microfilarial densities, not fatigue per se. The evidence shows fatigue is a common symptom in eye-worm–positive individuals, but not that it independently predicts severe disease, especially in the absence of microfilaremia.

134.Option: The presence of urticaria is strongly associated with severe disease outcomes in individuals with a history of eye worm migration, independent of microfilaremia
Evidence: Further, EW subjects reported several disease manifestations more often than the MF subjects. This was the case for transient swellings of any body part (other swellings 1.81 [1.05–3.10]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.84 [1.48–5.47]), urticaria (2.38 [1.23–4.59]) and fatigue (1.53 [1.02–2.30]).
individuals with high microfilarial densities are known to be at risk for spontaneous or treatment-related severe manifestations and excess mortality [1,7,12,13,18,19].
134.Rationale: Urticaria is reported more often in eye-worm–positive individuals than in some other groups, but the study does not link urticaria to severe disease outcomes or excess mortality. The paper specifically links severe manifestations and excess mortality to high microfilarial densities, not to urticaria. Thus, although urticaria is associated with the eye-worm infection state, it is not shown to be a strong independent predictor of severe outcomes in the absence of microfilaremia.

134.Option: Elevated absolute eosinophil counts are associated with high microfilarial density and predict severe disease outcomes, even in individuals without detectable microfilaremia
Evidence: Importantly, eosinophilia was higher in microfilaremic individuals than in amicrofilaremic loiasis.

Furthermore, the extent of eosinophilia seemed to be associated with L. loa microfilarial density and, conclusively all 56 individuals with ≥8,000mf/ml, had eosinophil counts above the normal range (≤0.5x103/μL).

The here described finding of an association between extent of eosinophilia and microfilaria counts, might therefore pathophysiologically explain the previously described excess mortality in highly microfilaremic individuals [13,19].
134.Rationale: The passage clearly links elevated eosinophil counts to microfilaremia and to higher microfilarial densities, and it proposes that eosinophilia may help explain excess mortality in highly microfilaremic individuals. However, the data show eosinophilia is greater in microfilaremic than in amicrofilaremic patients; the study does not demonstrate that elevated eosinophils predict severe outcomes in the absence of detectable microfilaremia. Therefore the statement that eosinophilia predicts severe disease even when microfilaremia is absent is not supported by the provided evidence.

134.Option: A history of eye worm migration is associated with neurological symptoms and fatigue, suggesting increased risk of severe disease even without detectable microfilaremia
Evidence: Comparing the loiasis infection states between each other showed that history of eye worm migration was associated with the presence of a wide range of signs and symptoms, independently of presence of microfilaremia.

This was the case for Calabar swelling-like manifestations ... paresthesia (1.51 [1.19–1.92] and 1.73 [1.32–2.26]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.63 [1.86–3.72] and 1.78 [1.15–2.76]), and fatigue (1.81 [1.43–2.30] and 1.49 [1.12–2.01]).

neurological symptoms, including paresthesia and transient paralysis of extremities, were strongly associated with loiasis, specifically with eye worm positive loiasis and not with microfilaremia itself.

Consequently, individuals having a high symptom load, often lack diagnostic features of infection in the blood which would facilitate definitive diagnosis, such as microfilaremia or extensive eosinophilia. This constellation may hamper diagnosis, and could cause inappropriate clinical management and treatment while these patients are indeed in need of appropriate clinical care to improve their symptoms.
134.Rationale: The study shows that history of eye worm (EW) is independently associated with a range of clinical manifestations, notably neurological signs (paresthesia, transient paralysis) and fatigue, and that these associations occur 'independently of presence of microfilaremia.' Neurological symptoms are emphasized as being strongly associated with eye-worm–positive loiasis but not with microfilaremia, and the authors note that patients with high symptom loads often lack blood-based diagnostic features. Together, these findings indicate that a history of eye worm with associated neurological symptoms and fatigue signals clinically important disease that may carry risk and requires attention even when microfilaremia is not detectable.

134.Option: A history of eye worm migration is associated with myalgia and severe headache, which are strong indicators of severe disease risk even without detectable microfilaremia
Evidence: In addition, individuals in the EW category reported myalgia and severe headache more often compared to LN (1.36 [1.01–1.82] and 1.33 [1.15–1.54], respectively).

Comparing the loiasis infection states between each other showed that history of eye worm migration was associated with the presence of a wide range of signs and symptoms, independently of presence of microfilaremia.
individuals with high microfilarial densities are known to be at risk for spontaneous or treatment-related severe manifestations and excess mortality [1,7,12,13,18,19].
134.Rationale: Although myalgia and severe headache were reported more often in the eye-worm (EW) group than in LN, the passage does not identify these symptoms as strong indicators of severe disease risk. The stronger links to severe manifestations and excess mortality described in the paper are with high microfilarial densities (and related eosinophilia), and the authors single out neurological findings (paresthesia, transient paralysis) rather than myalgia or headache as notable and frequent concerns in the EW group. Therefore the assertion that myalgia and severe headache are strong indicators of severe disease risk in the absence of microfilaremia is not supported by the presented evidence.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization with a moderate dose of the conjugate vaccine to promote both systemic memory B cells and mucosal IgA responses
Evidence: we were able to show that intradermal immunization of OSP:rTTHc induced OSP-specific IgA antibody secreting cells in intestinal lamina proprial tissue.
Following intramuscular and intradermal immunization, we detected OSP-specific IgG memory B cells in spleen of vaccinated animals at the time of sacrifice (Fig 6) in the various vaccine cohorts,
These data suggest that intradermal immunization of conjugate may not only induce mucosal responses, but may permit dose reduction [41].
135.Rationale: The study directly shows intradermal immunization induced mucosal (lamina propria) OSP-specific IgA-secreting cells and that OSP-specific IgG memory B cells were detected following intradermal immunization. The authors also note intradermal administration may permit dose reduction, consistent with a moderate dose strategy. Combining these findings, intradermal moderate-dose immunization is the most supported approach in the paper for promoting both mucosal immunity and systemic memory associated with durable protection.

135.Option: Intramuscular immunization with a high dose of the conjugate vaccine to maximize initial serum IgG antibody levels
Evidence: We found a significant increase in OSP (Fig 3A) and tetanus (Fig 3B)-specific IgG responses in serum of mice who received Inaba OSP-rTTHc vaccine intramuscularly or intradermally.

We detected OSP-specific IgG memory B cells in spleen of vaccinated animals at the time of sacrifice (Fig 6) in the various vaccine cohorts, although we did not detect this response in animals receiving the highest dose of vaccine (50 μg of OSP:rTTHc 5:1, intramuscularly).
135.Rationale: While intramuscular immunization generated serum IgG, the study specifically reports that the highest intramuscular dose (50 μg) did not show detectable OSP-specific IgG memory B cells. Thus a high intramuscular dose is not supported as the best strategy to enhance durable memory; the data argue against a simple 'higher dose = better long-term memory' interpretation.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization with a low dose of the conjugate vaccine to minimize adverse reactions and promote mucosal IgA responses
Evidence: we were able to show that intradermal immunization of OSP:rTTHc induced OSP-specific IgA antibody secreting cells in intestinal lamina proprial tissue.
These data suggest that intradermal immunization of conjugate may not only induce mucosal responses, but may permit dose reduction [41].
135.Rationale: The study supports that intradermal immunization induces mucosal IgA and that intradermal may allow dose reduction. However, the claim about 'minimize adverse reactions' is not addressed in the paper, and the study does not show that the lowest dose optimizes long-term memory (only that intradermal can permit dose reduction). Because the option couples an unsupported safety claim with an unproven assumption that a low dose is optimal for durable memory, it is not supported by the presented evidence.

135.Option: Intramuscular immunization with multiple doses to boost serum IgG and memory B cell responses
Evidence: Mice were immunized on days 0, 21, and 42.

immunization of mice induced prominent anti-OSP and anti-TT serum IgG responses, with significant increases occurring following a single immunization, and additional boosting of immune responses occurring after both second and third immunizations.
We assessed memory B cell responses after the third round of immunization as previously described [16,28].
We detected OSP-specific IgG memory B cells in spleen of vaccinated animals at the time of sacrifice (Fig 6) in the various vaccine cohorts,
135.Rationale: The study demonstrates that multiple (boosting) immunizations increased serum IgG and that memory B cell responses were assessed after the third immunization and were detectable. Therefore, an intramuscular multi-dose boosting schedule is supported by the data as a strategy to increase serum IgG and generate memory B cells that contribute to durable protection.

135.Option: Intramuscular immunization with alum adjuvant to enhance systemic IgG and vibriocidal responses
Evidence: co-administering the vaccine with alum did not significantly alter immune responses.
We detected low level vibriocidal responses in vaccinated animals (Fig 5).
135.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that co-administration with alum did not significantly alter immune responses, and that vibriocidal responses were low. Thus intramuscular immunization with alum is not supported by the study as a strategy that meaningfully enhances systemic IgG or vibriocidal responses for improved durable protection.

136.Option: Construct a phylogenetic tree using maximum likelihood methods on the seven MLST loci to infer geographic origin based on genetic relatedness
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates, sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].

It has been previously observed that B. pseudomallei STs from Australia and Southeast Asia are mutually exclusive as phylogenetic analyses show geographically correlated clusters of STs, although these analyses failed to group all samples from either region together [9] [10].

High relative rates of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11], meaning that allele frequency differences among populations is an appropriate method for determining population structure.
High relative rates of recombination to mutation complicate attempts to discern population structure for this species using strictly phylogenetic approaches.
136.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that using only the seven MLST loci yields insufficient phylogenetic discrimination because of low sequence diversity and high lateral gene transfer (recombination). Maximum likelihood phylogenetic trees built on these same seven loci would therefore be subject to the same limitations and risk misclassification; the authors argue phylogenetic approaches are complicated by high recombination and are not the preferred method for reliable population assignment in this organism.

136.Option: Use Bayesian clustering with a high probability threshold (≥95%) to assign isolates to populations based on allele frequency patterns rather than phylogenetic trees
Evidence: The program Structure [13] is a Bayesian-based clustering algorithm that has been used to infer population structure within genetically diverse bacteria such as Helicobacter pylori [14].
We used both BAPS and Structure results to assess population assignments [18].

As there is no empirical measure of determining the accuracy of population assignments, we further assessed Structure and BAPS assignments of B. pseudomallei using MLST data, by comparing individual ST assignments made by Structure and BAPS to the geographic information listed in the MLST database and to the likelihood of assignment into each population as calculated by Genetic Analysis in Excel (GenAlEx) v.6 [23].

To be conservative in our assignment of STs to a population, we suggest that a ST only be considered to be from a given population if Structure or BAPS assigned it to that population ≥95% of the time.

The relative rate of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11], meaning that allele frequency differences among populations is an appropriate method for determining population structure.
136.Rationale: The passage advocates using Bayesian clustering programs (Structure, BAPS) that assign isolates based on allele frequency patterns rather than relying on phylogenetic trees, specifically because high recombination undermines phylogenetic approaches. The authors explicitly recommend a conservative ≥95% assignment threshold to reduce misclassification. Therefore, this methodological choice most directly addresses misclassification risk from high recombination.

136.Option: Perform whole-genome sequencing on all isolates and use core genome MLST to identify single nucleotide polymorphisms that define geographic populations
Evidence: Whole genome sequencing of this ST will help resolve uncertainties regarding Australian and Southeast Asian population assignments as phylogenetic analyses can be expected to reflect population subdivisions as they have for the Australian and Southeast Asian populations [11].

Genotype interrogation at more loci or great numbers of STs will increase our knowledge of subpopulation dynamics, but in the meantime our current ability to differentiate between the two or three major populations is an important first step for epidemiological attribution.
single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) genotyping provides a streamlined way to characterize MLST populations even for recombining species [25], [26], [27].

Performing MLST on large bacterial collections is a time-consuming task; however, single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) genotyping provides a streamlined way to characterize MLST populations even for recombining species [25], [26], [27].
136.Rationale: The passage indicates that interrogating more loci (e.g., whole-genome data) or using SNP genotyping improves resolution and can resolve uncertainties that seven-locus MLST cannot, and explicitly states that whole-genome sequencing will help resolve ambiguous assignments. Whole-genome/core-genome approaches increase the number of independent markers and therefore better overcome the confounding effects of recombination, reducing misclassification risk.

136.Option: Use a neighbor-joining tree with bootstrapping to assess the robustness of genetic clusters and assign isolates based on cluster membership
Evidence: Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates, sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].

High relative rates of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11], meaning that allele frequency differences among populations is an appropriate method for determining population structure.
High relative rates of recombination to mutation complicate attempts to discern population structure for this species using strictly phylogenetic approaches.
136.Rationale: Neighbor-joining trees (even with bootstrapping) are phylogenetic approaches that rely on the same limited MLST loci. The passage explains that high recombination and limited diversity make phylogenetic clustering unreliable for B. pseudomallei. Thus this approach would not directly address misclassification risk and is contrary to the authors' recommended methods.

136.Option: Apply a principal component analysis (PCA) to the MLST allelic profiles to visualize genetic variation and assign isolates based on proximity to population centroids
Evidence: The program Structure [13] is a Bayesian-based clustering algorithm that has been used to infer population structure within genetically diverse bacteria such as Helicobacter pylori [14].
We used both BAPS and Structure results to assess population assignments [18].

The relative rate of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11], meaning that allele frequency differences among populations is an appropriate method for determining population structure.

Performing MLST on large bacterial collections is a time-consuming task; however, single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) genotyping provides a streamlined way to characterize MLST populations even for recombining species [25], [26], [27].
136.Rationale: PCA can be useful for visualization but does not explicitly implement the allele-frequency-based probabilistic assignment or provide the assignment probabilities and conservative thresholds (e.g., ≥95%) that the authors recommend to mitigate misclassification from high recombination. The passage endorses Bayesian clustering (Structure/BAPS) and SNP/WGS approaches for robust assignment; PCA on seven-locus MLST profiles would be less direct and less protective against recombination-driven misclassification.

137.Option: The genetic relatedness between isolates from Jimma and other sites, as shown by FST and ADMIXTURE, indicates that the high DBP copy number variants are maintained through inbreeding rather than gene flow, supporting localized adaptation
Evidence: The FST statistics showed low levels of genetic differentiation between Arbaminch and Halaba (FST value = -0.019), as well as between Arbaminch and Hawassa (FST value = 0.017; S6 Table). By contrast, higher differentiation was observed between Jimma and Arbaminch (FST value = 0.229) and between Jimma and Halaba (FST value = 0.327; S6 Table).

ADMIXTURE analysis showed that there were most likely two or three genetic clusters among all the Ethiopian samples based on the lowest cross validation scores of 0.75 and 0.78, respectively (Fig 7A). At K = 2, most of the samples from Badowacho, Hawassa, Arbaminch, and Halaba had predominantly the light-blue cluster, whereas samples from Jimma had the red cluster (Fig 7A).
P. vivax with high-order DBP copies evolved from isolates with a single-copy DBP multiple times independently at different geographic locations.
gene flow among geographical locations may allow further spread and a broad distribution of high-order PvDBP strains.
137.Rationale: While Jimma is genetically differentiated from other sites (FST, ADMIXTURE), the passage explicitly reports that high-order DBP copies evolved multiple times independently and that gene flow may spread high-order DBP strains. That pattern is inconsistent with the assertion that high DBP copy variants are maintained primarily through inbreeding (localized fixation). The text supports multiple independent duplications and potential spread via gene flow rather than sole maintenance by inbreeding/localized adaptation.

137.Option: The presence of multiple DBP gene copies in Ethiopian isolates indicates a higher rate of recombination events, which may explain the observed transmission network with Arbaminch as a source hub
Evidence: According to CNVnator, six gene regions showed copy number variation among our Ethiopian samples (Fig 5; S5 Table). ... duplications were observed in DBP1 on chromosome 6 and MSP3.11 on chromosome 10. DBP1 ranged from one to as high as five copies
expanded gene copies shared identical nucleotide sequences, suggesting rapid and recent duplications within an isolate [79, 88].
The transmission network indicated that Arbaminch was the major source of infections from which the infections in Jimma, Hawassa, Badowacho, and Halaba originated (Table 3; Fig 7B).
137.Rationale: The passage describes DBP expansion as tandem duplications and recent duplications (identical sequences), not as evidence of increased recombination rates. Recombination is discussed elsewhere (e.g., for MSP3), but the DBP copy-number changes are presented as duplications rather than recombination-driven diversity. Thus inferring a higher recombination rate from DBP copy-number variation is not supported by the text, even though Arbaminch is identified as a transmission source.

137.Option: The high copy number variation in DBP genes likely reflects adaptive evolution to diverse human Duffy phenotypes, and its spread from Arbaminch suggests active dissemination of these host-adapted variants across the region
Evidence: Given that Duffy phenotype is more diverse among the the human populations in Ethiopia than in Asia [87], it is possible that P. vivax expands DBP to multiple copies to enhance binding affinity and invasion to different host phenotype [79].
The transmission network indicated that Arbaminch was the major source of infections from which the infections in Jimma, Hawassa, Badowacho, and Halaba originated (Table 3; Fig 7B).

P. vivax with high-order DBP copies evolved from isolates with a single-copy DBP multiple times independently at different geographic locations. Isolates with high-order PvDBP copies may confer better fitness and favored by selection, resulting in a higher frequency in isolates of P. vivax from Ethiopia. Moreover, gene flow among geographical locations may allow further spread and a broad distribution of high-order PvDBP strains.
137.Rationale: The passage explicitly links DBP copy expansion to host Duffy-phenotype diversity as a plausible adaptive response to improve invasion across diverse human phenotypes. It also documents Arbaminch as a transmission source and notes that gene flow can spread high-order DBP variants. Together these statements support the interpretation that DBP copy-number variation reflects adaptation to Duffy diversity and that such host-adapted variants can be disseminated regionally (consistent with Arbaminch acting as a source).

137.Option: The low nucleotide diversity in TRAg genes across all study sites suggests that these genes are under strong purifying selection, limiting their role in host adaptation despite high copy number variation in other erythrocyte binding genes
Evidence: The TRAg gene family from the isolates from Ethiopia also showed a low level of nucleotide diversity when compared to the other EBP gene families with an average nucleotide diversity of 0.2% (Fig 3B).

The majority of these genes belong to the TRAg multigene family. The TRAg genes had an average dN/dS ratio of 2.75 across all branches and an average of 5.75 across all sites for the M2 model tested for selection (Table 2).
TRAg15 had more sites detected under positive selection, with 50 of the sites showing a posterior probability greater than 50% and 43 showing a posterior probability greater than 95%.
137.Rationale: Although TRAg genes show low nucleotide diversity, the study found evidence of positive selection (high dN/dS and many sites under selection) in multiple TRAg genes. Positive selection indicates adaptive change, not purifying selection. Therefore, the assertion that low diversity implies strong purifying selection and limits their role in host adaptation is contradicted by the passage's explicit detection of positive selection in TRAg genes.

137.Option: The expansion of DBP gene copies in Ethiopia is likely due to relaxed selection pressure from antimalarial drugs, allowing for the accumulation of non-functional variants that are then transmitted across regions via human movement
Evidence: The range of PvDBP copy variation may not be related to transmission intensity but host-parasite interaction during the invasion process.
expanded gene copies shared identical nucleotide sequences, suggesting rapid and recent duplications within an isolate [79, 88].

A number of antimalarial drug resistance genes including the chloroquine resistance transporter (CRT-O) gene, the dihydropteroate synthase (DHPS) gene, and dihydrofolate reductase–thymidylate synthase (DHFR) gene were previously reported with positive selection [20, 89], but we did not detect such in this study.
Sequences of PvCRT-O and PvMDR1 have been shown to be highly conserved in P. vivax isolates from Ethiopia [53].
137.Rationale: The passage argues that DBP copy expansion is more plausibly linked to host-parasite interaction (e.g., adaptation to Duffy phenotype diversity) rather than relaxed drug selection. Expanded DBP copies show identical sequences consistent with recent functional duplications rather than accumulation of non-functional variants. The study also reports no evidence in this dataset of selection in drug-resistance loci that would support drug-relaxation-driven accumulation of non-functional copies. Therefore the option's drug-relaxed-selection explanation is not supported by the text.

138.Option: The agreement in classifying drug efficacy into 'satisfactory', 'doubtful', or 'reduced' is sufficient to conclude that diagnostic methods are equivalent in measuring drug efficacy, even when sensitivity varies across species
Evidence: No single diagnostic method provides ERR that are equivalent to single Kato-Katz for all STH, but they agree in classifying drug efficacy according to the WHO guidelines

None of the evaluated tests provided equivalent results to those obtained by the currently recommended single Kato-Katz for all STH, but this might be due to the number of pairwise-comparisons and the strict bounds of equivalence.

However, despite the lack of equivalence, for most methods there was relatively good agreement in classifying ALB efficacy according to WHO guidelines. This suggests that each method holds promise for the assessment of drug efficacy in the context of assessing drug efficacy within STH control programs.
138.Rationale: The passage explicitly distinguishes equivalence of quantitative ERR estimates from agreement in categorical classification. It states that even though several methods agreed in classifying efficacy by WHO categories, none provided equivalent ERR results for all STH. Therefore, agreement in classification alone is not sufficient to conclude equivalence in measuring drug efficacy when sensitivities differ across species and settings.

138.Option: The risk of differential misclassification due to variable diagnostic sensitivity must be evaluated to avoid invalid conclusions about equivalence in drug efficacy
Evidence: The results of the present study highlight that the impact of the diagnostic sensitivity on ERR results is minimal (Tables 3–5 and Figs 2–4).

FECPAKG2 was previously evaluated as being less sensitive than Kato-Katz, while qPCR was found to have superior sensitivity for all STH [16, 26, 39]. This is also supported by the results of our study, where we noticed high numbers of false negative test results at baseline and follow-up for FECPAKG2.

When applying FECPAKG2, drug efficacy could not be measured in 438 (32.3%) of the 1,354 individuals with STH infection, because of false negative results at baseline (A. lumbricoides: 148/441, T. trichiura: 135/456 and hookworms: 155/457).
Diagnostic methods for assessing drug efficacy need to be validated for their intended-use

As illustrated in supplementary information (S1 Info), the absence of a significant difference does not imply equivalent ERR estimates nor does the presence of a significant difference rule out equivalent ERR results.
138.Rationale: The passage documents major differences in sensitivity between methods (e.g., FECPAKG2 vs qPCR) and large numbers of false negatives that prevented calculation of ERR. These issues constitute differential misclassification (different probabilities of detecting infection across methods, species, or settings) and can bias comparisons. The authors emphasize validating diagnostics for intended use and caution that lack of a significant difference does not imply equivalence. Thus evaluating and accounting for differential misclassification is the critical factor.

138.Option: Equivalence in drug efficacy can be concluded if the confidence intervals around the difference in egg reduction rates do not include zero, regardless of the diagnostic method's sensitivity
Evidence: We therefore applied a level of equivalence that was tailored to the different STH species. The level of equivalence for A. lumbricoides, hookworms and T. trichiura was set arbitrarily at -/+2.5, -/+5.0 and -/+10-point percentage respectively.

For A. lumbricoides, there is significant evidence that a diagnostic method results in equivalent drug efficacy results compared to a 1x KK when the 95% confidence intervals (95%CI) around the difference in drug efficacy does not include +/-2.5%.

As illustrated in supplementary information (S1 Info), the absence of a significant difference does not imply equivalent ERR estimates nor does the presence of a significant difference rule out equivalent ERR results.
138.Rationale: Equivalence testing is not based on whether the CI excludes zero (no difference); it is based on whether the CI lies entirely within predefined equivalence bounds (species-specific in this study: ±2.5%, ±5%, ±10%). The passage emphasizes tailored equivalence margins and warns that absence of a significant difference does not imply equivalence. Therefore the statement is incorrect.

138.Option: High sensitivity of a diagnostic method ensures that equivalence in drug efficacy can be reliably assessed, even in settings with high drug pressure
Evidence: FECPAKG2 was previously evaluated as being less sensitive than Kato-Katz, while qPCR was found to have superior sensitivity for all STH [16, 26, 39]. This is also supported by the results of our study, where we noticed high numbers of false negative test results at baseline and follow-up for FECPAKG2.

None of the evaluated tests provided equivalent results to those obtained by the currently recommended single Kato-Katz for all STH, but this might be due to the number of pairwise-comparisons and the strict bounds of equivalence.

it is also important to point out that our findings for qPCR do not necessarily apply for other qPCR assays, given that the plethora of described qPCR assays for STHs can differ substantially in performance.
138.Rationale: Although high sensitivity (e.g., qPCR) can reduce false negatives, the passage shows that superior sensitivity does not guarantee equivalence of ERR estimates across species or settings. Equivalence depended on species-specific margins, study site, and other factors; and qPCR performance may vary by assay. Thus high sensitivity alone does not ensure reliable equivalence conclusions.

138.Option: The magnitude of the difference in egg reduction rate between methods should be compared to a fixed threshold of 10% to determine equivalence, regardless of species or setting
Evidence: We therefore applied a level of equivalence that was tailored to the different STH species. The level of equivalence for A. lumbricoides, hookworms and T. trichiura was set arbitrarily at -/+2.5, -/+5.0 and -/+10-point percentage respectively.

Given the differences in drug efficacy of ALB across the STH species [8, 9] (A. lumbricoides: ~99%, hookworms: ~96%, T. trichiura: ~65%), a level of equivalence that is acceptable for T. trichiura may not be acceptable for A. lumbricoides.
The level of equivalence for A. lumbricoides, hookworms and T. trichiura was set arbitrarily at -/+2.5, -/+5.0 and -/+10-point percentage respectively.
138.Rationale: The passage explicitly states species-specific equivalence bounds were used and justifies different thresholds because baseline efficacies differ by species. Therefore using a single fixed 10% threshold regardless of species or setting is inappropriate.

139.Option: The proportion of the population that receives at least one dose of the drug during each annual round of MDA
Evidence: The Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis (GPELF) aims to use mass drug administration (MDA) to interrupt transmission and to reduce morbidity, with annual doses of a multi-drug regimen for at least five years (www.filariasis.org) [3].

The African Programme for Onchocerciasis Control aims to establish by 2010 community-based sustainable ivermectin treatments of 50 million people in 19 African countries having meso- and hyper-endemicity (www.who.int/apoc/en/).

Numerous technical challenges threaten the success of these eradication programs [2],[4], including incomplete efficacy of available drugs against adult filarial worms [5],[6], severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections [3], and the risk that filarial worms will develop resistance to the drugs available for MDA [7],[8].
139.Rationale: While programmatic coverage (numbers treated annually) is important and the passage describes goals for annual MDA and large-scale treatment targets, the passage explicitly highlights the risk of drug resistance as a central technical challenge threatening the success of MDA. Thus, although coverage matters, it is not presented as the most critical factor affecting the reliability of long-term treatment outcomes compared with emergence of resistance.

139.Option: The duration of drug exposure in individual patients relative to the parasite life cycle
Evidence: The Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis (GPELF) aims to use mass drug administration (MDA) to interrupt transmission and to reduce morbidity, with annual doses of a multi-drug regimen for at least five years (www.filariasis.org) [3].

Notably, antibiotic treatment targeting Wolbachia endosymbionts of filarial nematodes is emerging as an alternative drug treatment for filariasis. Doxycycline treatment has been shown to result in an almost complete loss of Mf and macrofilaricidal effects in LF and in sterilization of adult worms and macrofilaricidal effects in O. volvulus (reviewed in [16],[17]). However, the period of treatment with doxycycline at present (between 4–8 weeks) is not applicable to mass treatment strategies, due to both the logistical difficulties and contra-indication in children under eight and pregnant women [16],[17], as a result antibiotic treatment regimens require further refinement.

Numerous technical challenges threaten the success of these eradication programs [2],[4], including incomplete efficacy of available drugs against adult filarial worms [5],[6], severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections [3], and the risk that filarial worms will develop resistance to the drugs available for MDA [7],[8].
139.Rationale: Duration of individual drug exposure (and its mismatch with practical MDA regimens) is discussed (doxycycline 4–8 weeks is impractical for MDA). However, the passage emphasizes other program-threatening issues—especially the risk of drug resistance—as the critical factor jeopardizing long-term success. Therefore duration of individual exposure is important operationally but is not presented as the most critical determinant of the reliability of long-term transmission outcomes.

139.Option: The consistency of drug delivery schedules across different communities in the MDA program
Evidence: The Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis (GPELF) aims to use mass drug administration (MDA) to interrupt transmission and to reduce morbidity, with annual doses of a multi-drug regimen for at least five years (www.filariasis.org) [3].

139.Rationale: Consistency and sustainability of delivery are clearly relevant to MDA (the passage cites community-based sustainable treatments and annual regimens). Nevertheless, the passage singles out the emergence of drug resistance as a principal technical threat to program success. Thus consistency of schedules is important but not identified in the passage as the most critical factor affecting the reliability of long-term protection assessments.

139.Option: The presence of co-infections with other parasitic organisms that may alter drug metabolism
Evidence: Numerous technical challenges threaten the success of these eradication programs [2],[4], including incomplete efficacy of available drugs against adult filarial worms [5],[6], severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections [3], and the risk that filarial worms will develop resistance to the drugs available for MDA [7],[8].
139.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections as a threat to MDA safety and feasibility, which can affect program uptake and observed outcomes. However, the text gives greater emphasis to the risk and demonstrated occurrence of drug resistance as the central concern for long-term program reliability. Therefore co-infections (and related toxicity) are important but not portrayed as the single most critical influence on reliability of long-term transmission outcomes.

139.Option: The emergence and spread of drug-resistant parasite strains due to repeated exposure during mass drug administration
Evidence: Numerous technical challenges threaten the success of these eradication programs [2],[4], including incomplete efficacy of available drugs against adult filarial worms [5],[6], severe drug toxicity in people with heavy loiasis infections [3], and the risk that filarial worms will develop resistance to the drugs available for MDA [7],[8].

The emergence of drug resistant strains of the O. volvulus parasite was initially suggested by reports of patients with onchocerciasis who failed to respond to IVM treatment [10],[11] and a recent report from Ghana has provided the first direct proof of IVM resistance in O. volvulus populations [12].

A number of studies have associated IVM resistance with genetic markers (reviewed in [13]), while a recent, more extensive study using worms taken from individuals before and after repeated IVM treatments, has demonstrated that IVM causes genetic selection on O. volvulus [14].

139.Rationale: The passage repeatedly highlights emergence and spread of drug-resistant filarial parasites as a central, program-threatening problem: it explicitly cites the 'risk that filarial worms will develop resistance,' documents ivermectin resistance reports and genetic selection after repeated treatments, and notes the lack of alternative drugs. These statements make resistance the most critical factor influencing the reliability of observed long-term treatment outcomes when assessing protection against transmission.

140.Option: Use a time-to-event analysis to model the time from sample collection to deterioration, with biomarker levels as time-varying covariates
Evidence: The main limitation of our retrospective study is that our samples were collected from patients at the end of the four year period during which the extent of progression was analyzed as opposed to the beginning of the four year period, which would have been optimal for directly measuring their prognostic powers.
We expect that a prospective study in the future will reveal the answer to this question.
140.Rationale: A time-to-event (survival) analysis with biomarkers as time-varying covariates requires longitudinal follow-up and repeated or prospective measurement of biomarkers beginning before or at the start of the risk period. The passage explicitly states the study is retrospective with samples collected at the end of the follow-up period and that a prospective study would be needed to directly measure prognostic power. Therefore this analytical approach is not feasible or directly supported by the study design described.

140.Option: Compare biomarker levels between patients with long disease duration and those with short disease duration to assess whether progression rate is associated with biomarker levels
Evidence: To investigate the potential influence of disease duration as a secondary variable, a new test group was created containing only those patients for whom the disease onset date was 7–13 years prior to the sample collection day.

we confirmed that there was no significant difference in disease duration between these two groups. The results remained consistent with our previous findings: CSF CXCL10, CXCL9, and neopterin were all elevated in deteriorating patients with respect to stable patients (p<0.01, Figure 5).
140.Rationale: Directly comparing biomarker levels between long-duration and short-duration patients tests association with duration, not whether biomarkers predict progression independent of duration. The investigators instead controlled for duration by creating a subgroup with a similar disease duration (7–13 years) and then compared deteriorating vs stable patients within that subgroup. Simply comparing long vs short duration groups would not isolate prediction of progression independent of duration and could conflate effects of duration with progression rate.

140.Option: Stratify patients by disease duration and compare the odds of being deteriorating across strata, using biomarker levels as a predictor within each stratum
Evidence: To investigate the potential influence of disease duration as a secondary variable, a new test group was created containing only those patients for whom the disease onset date was 7–13 years prior to the sample collection day.

we confirmed that there was no significant difference in disease duration between these two groups. The results remained consistent with our previous findings: CSF CXCL10, CXCL9, and neopterin were all elevated in deteriorating patients with respect to stable patients (p<0.01, Figure 5).
140.Rationale: Stratification by disease duration is a valid epidemiologic approach to control for confounding by duration and could be used to examine whether biomarker–progression associations persist within duration strata. However, the question asks which strategy would most directly support the conclusion independent of duration; the study most directly addressed this by creating a subgroup with a restricted (matched) range of disease durations and comparing deteriorating vs stable patients within that subgroup. Stratifying into multiple duration strata and comparing odds across strata is a less direct approach than the exact matching/restricted-duration comparison the authors performed, so it is not the best answer given the passage.

140.Option: Compare biomarker levels between deteriorating and stable patients within a subgroup matched for disease duration to control for time-related confounding
Evidence: To investigate the potential influence of disease duration as a secondary variable, a new test group was created containing only those patients for whom the disease onset date was 7–13 years prior to the sample collection day.

Patients fitting this criterion were selected from the 53 total available from both the Training and Test sets: eight stable patients and ten deteriorating patients; we confirmed that there was no significant difference in disease duration between these two groups.
The results remained consistent with our previous findings: CSF CXCL10, CXCL9, and neopterin were all elevated in deteriorating patients with respect to stable patients (p<0.01, Figure 5).
140.Rationale: The passage describes exactly this analytical strategy: restricting the analysis to patients with similar disease duration (7–13 years) so that disease duration is not a confounder, then comparing biomarker levels between deteriorating and stable patients. This approach most directly demonstrates that biomarker associations with progression are independent of illness duration by holding duration constant and showing the biomarkers remain elevated in deteriorating patients.

141.Option: Chest pain is a key early indicator of severe pulmonary complications in paragonimiasis due to its direct association with pleural invasion by migrating larvae
Evidence: In this study, dyspnea was the most common symptom, followed by chest pain and cough. However, chest pain was identified in 57.9% of patients with pneumothorax associated with paragonimiasis, and simultaneous chest pain and dyspnea were reported in 37%.
According to Yoshida A, et al. chest pain occurs when the Paragonimus larvae invades and injures the pleura, causing pneumothorax [1].

A subsequent multivariate analysis suggested that asthma (OR 8.10, 95% CI 1.43–45.91), chest pain (OR 8.15, 95% CI 2.70–24.58), and intrapulmonary lesions (OR 8.94, 95% CI 1.12–71.36) were independent risk factors for pulmonary paragonimiasis-associated pneumothorax (Table 5).
Most of patients with pneumothorax (89.5%, 17/19) underwent chest tube insertion as a first treatment.
141.Rationale: The passage gives multiple lines of evidence that chest pain is not only common among paragonimiasis patients with pneumothorax (57.9%) but is mechanistically linked to pleural invasion by migrating Paragonimus larvae, which can cause pneumothorax. Chest pain was an independent predictor in multivariate analysis (OR 8.15) and, because pneumothorax frequently required chest tube insertion (an urgent intervention), chest pain is a key acute clinical feature that should prompt urgent evaluation for this severe complication in this population.

141.Option: Pleural effusion is the most significant clinical feature indicating severe pulmonary complications because it is highly prevalent in patients with pneumothorax and reflects an inflammatory response to larval invasion
Evidence: The pneumothorax group showed a significantly higher prevalence of pleural effusion (78.9% vs 28.7%, p<0.001).
This pneumothorax was observed as an early manifestation of radiologic findings of pulmonary paragonimiasis [15].
After a univariate analysis, multi-collinearity was evaluated among independent variables. Age, height, dyspnea, pleural effusion, eosinophil (1,000/mm3), and eosinophil (%) were removed.
141.Rationale: While pleural effusion is strongly associated with pneumothorax in this cohort (78.9% prevalence in the pneumothorax group) and plausibly reflects the inflammatory response to larval invasion, the study removed pleural effusion from the multivariable model (multi-collinearity) and did not list it as an independent predictor of pneumothorax. Therefore, although pleural effusion is an important associated finding, the passage supports chest pain and intrapulmonary lesions as the independent predictors that more directly suggest pneumothorax requiring urgent intervention.

141.Option: A history of asthma is the most significant clinical feature indicating severe pulmonary complications because it is an independent risk factor and increases susceptibility to pneumothorax in this population
Evidence: After a univariate analysis, multi-collinearity was evaluated among independent variables. Previous pneumothorax (odds ratio [OR] 9.29, 95% confidence interval [CI] 1.23–70.26), asthma (OR 5.81, 95% CI 1.27–26.61), chest pain (OR 6.77, 95% CI 2.49–18.42), and intrapulmonary lesions (OR 10.24, 95% CI 1.33–78.66) were statistically significant (Table 4).

A subsequent multivariate analysis suggested that asthma (OR 8.10, 95% CI 1.43–45.91), chest pain (OR 8.15, 95% CI 2.70–24.58), and intrapulmonary lesions (OR 8.94, 95% CI 1.12–71.36) were independent risk factors for pulmonary paragonimiasis-associated pneumothorax (Table 5).
In this study, asthma, rather than COPD, was identified as a significant risk factor for pneumothorax associated with pulmonary paragonimiasis.
141.Rationale: Asthma is indeed an independent risk factor for pneumothorax in this study (multivariate OR 8.10), so it increases susceptibility. However, the question asks which clinical feature most strongly suggests the presence of a severe pulmonary complication requiring urgent intervention. A history of asthma is a baseline risk factor (predisposing condition), not an acute presenting sign that would trigger urgent intervention on its own. The study emphasizes acute clinical clues (chest pain) and imaging findings (intrapulmonary lesions) as more immediate indicators of pneumothorax.

141.Option: Intrapulmonary lesions are the most significant predictor of severe pulmonary complications because they are strongly associated with pneumothorax and indicate active parasite-induced structural lung damage
Evidence: Intrapulmonary lesions were significantly higher in the pneumothorax group than in the non-pneumothorax group, but there was no difference between the two groups in detailed lesions.

A subsequent multivariate analysis suggested that asthma (OR 8.10, 95% CI 1.43–45.91), chest pain (OR 8.15, 95% CI 2.70–24.58), and intrapulmonary lesions (OR 8.94, 95% CI 1.12–71.36) were independent risk factors for pulmonary paragonimiasis-associated pneumothorax (Table 5).
Our study suggests that chest pain, asthma, and intrapulmonary lesion are clinically significant risk factors associated with pulmonary paragonimiasis.
141.Rationale: Intrapulmonary lesions are strongly associated with pneumothorax in this cohort (ipsilateral relationship in 94.7% of cases) and were an independent predictor in multivariate analysis (OR 8.94). These lesions indicate parasite-induced structural lung damage that predisposes to pleural disruption and pneumothorax, making them a strong predictor of a severe complication that may require urgent intervention. Thus intrapulmonary lesions, especially when seen ipsilateral to symptoms, are a clinically significant finding to prompt urgent evaluation and management.

141.Option: Dyspnea is the most significant clinical feature indicating severe pulmonary complications because it is more prevalent in patients with pneumothorax and reflects underlying lung dysfunction from parasite migration
Evidence: There was a significant difference between the pneumothorax and non-pneumothorax groups in the number of subjects with chest pain and dyspnea (57.9% [11/19] vs 16.9% [27/160], p<0.001; 68.4% [13/19] vs 28.1% [45/160], p<0.001).
In this study, dyspnea was the most common symptom, followed by chest pain and cough.
After a univariate analysis, multi-collinearity was evaluated among independent variables. Age, height, dyspnea, pleural effusion, eosinophil (1,000/mm3), and eosinophil (%) were removed.
141.Rationale: Dyspnea is indeed common and significantly more frequent in the pneumothorax group (68.4% vs 28.1%). However, the study removed dyspnea from the multivariable model (multi-collinearity) and emphasizes chest pain and intrapulmonary lesions as independent predictors. Dyspnea is a sensitive symptom of lung dysfunction but less specific for pneumothorax in this cohort; chest pain and imaging findings provide stronger, more actionable clues that should prompt urgent intervention for pneumothorax.

142.Option: The presence of a large glomerulus (MD1) that receives input from CO2-sensitive neurons suggests a dedicated neural pathway for detecting a key host odorant
Evidence: The largest glomerulus in both the female and male antennal lobe is MD1, with a mean volume of 5,222 μm3 and 4,090 μm3, which accounts for 4.26% and 4.81% of their total antennal lobe volumes, respectively.
