Closer examination of TL behavior by geographical region shows that the Amazon region exhibits the highest incidence rates for all observed periods.

The Andean region, which gathers over 50% of the country's population, shows great socioeconomic diversity and highly variable geography [27]. These features ... The opposite situation is seen in the Andean region, which is made up of the 11 departments that make up the majority of the country’s population, in which the large number of cases does not result in elevated incidences (Figs 1 and 5; S1 Table).
167.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that the Andean region contains a large proportion of the population (over 50%) and therefore can register large absolute numbers of cases without high incidence rates; conversely the Amazon has a relatively small population yet shows very high incidence per 100,000. This population-denominator effect directly explains why high case counts in a populous, high-altitude Andean area (analogous to central Peru) can coexist with stable or moderate incidence rates, while the Amazon's smaller population leads to higher incidence metrics despite fewer absolute cases. Thus this option best matches the spatiotemporal patterns described in the passage.

167.Option: The Andean region's higher prevalence of multiple Leishmania species leads to more severe cases and thus higher case counts, while the Amazon region's lower species diversity results in milder disease and fewer reported cases
Evidence: It is worth noting that Colombia is the country with the greatest number of circulating species in the world, 10 in total (L. panamensis, L. braziliensis, L. guyanensis, L. infantum chagasi, L. mexicana, L. lainsoni, L. amazonensis, L. colombiensis, L. equatoriensis, L. naiffi), and that the most prevalent species exhibit high genetic diversity [9–11].
Antioquia showed the highest number of vector species, 11 overall, followed by Meta, Caldas, Boyacá, and Amazonas, with 9 species each.

Likewise, the superposition of the vector species and leishmaniasis cases shows that departments with greater reported vector diversity tend to display an elevated number of cases, particularly in the departments belonging to the Andean and Orinoco regions (Fig 5).

Likewise, the great diversity of circulating Leishmania species in Colombia plays a fundamental role in the permanence and propagation of the disease, given that neither epidemiological surveillance, nor treatment, take this variable into account.
167.Rationale: Although species diversity and vector presence influence disease dynamics and may affect clinical severity and treatment response, the passage does not indicate that higher species diversity in the Andean region is the principal explanation for the specific discrepancy between high case counts and stable incidence. In fact, the Amazon is noted to have substantial vector/reservoir richness and high incidence. The passage emphasizes population denominator effects (large Andean population diluting incidence) and differences in reporting/surveillance as more direct explanations for observed patterns. Therefore the hypothesis that species diversity alone explains the discrepancy is not supported as the most likely factor in the passage.

168.Option: The co-expression of Tim-3 and Tax on CD4+ T cells in HTLV-1-infected individuals indicates that these cells are in a state of exhaustion, which contributes to disease progression
Evidence: We observed that Tax was expressed on PBMC from some subjects following 24 hours of culture and was detected on both Tim-3+ as well as Tim-3− CD4+ T cells (Figure 4A).
We further identified a unique subset of Tax expressing CD4+ T cells that were Tim-3hi and lacked PD-1 in most of the subjects expressing Tax (Fig. 4A).
In this study, we report that CD8+ and CD4+ T cells in HTLV-1 infection express lower levels of Tim-3, and this was more pronounced in patients with HAM/TSP.
168.Rationale: The option asserts that co-expression of Tim-3 and Tax on CD4+ T cells indicates exhaustion driving disease. The passage instead reports Tax is present on both Tim-3+ and Tim-3− CD4+ T cells and even describes a Tim-3hi Tax+ subset that lacks PD-1. Moreover, the overall finding in the study is lower Tim-3 expression on T cells in HTLV-1 infection. The data do not support interpreting Tim-3/Tax co-expression as the primary marker of exhaustion driving progression; rather, the authors emphasize reduced Tim-3 (not Tim-3/Tax co-expression) and link low Tim-3 to hyperfunctional, potentially pathogenic T cells.

168.Option: The absence of co-expression of Tim-3 and PD-1 on CD8+ T cells in HTLV-1-infected individuals indicates a lack of T cell exhaustion, which supports the hypothesis of persistent T cell activation driving disease progression
Evidence: Only a few T cells co-expressed both Tim-3 and PD-1, and no differences were observed between uninfected subjects and those with HTLV-1 asymptomatic infection or HAM/TSP patients.
In this study, we report that CD8+ and CD4+ T cells in HTLV-1 infection express lower levels of Tim-3, and this was more pronounced in patients with HAM/TSP.
We propose that this low expression of Tim-3 on HTLV-1 Tax-specific T cells may lead to a persistent and deleterious effector T cell pool leading to more inflammation.
168.Rationale: While the passage does state that only a few T cells co-express Tim-3 and PD-1, and overall Tim-3 expression is low in infected subjects, the strongest direct support for persistent activation comes from functional data (reduced Tim-3 correlating with increased IFN-γ). The mere absence of co-expression is a descriptive finding and does not by itself most strongly support the hypothesis. The authors emphasize low Tim-3 and the functional consequences (hyperfunction), not simply the lack of Tim-3/PD-1 co-expression, so this option is not the best-supported answer.

168.Option: The higher frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− CD8+ T cells in HTLV-1-infected individuals compared to controls would indicate a state of T cell exhaustion that contributes to disease progression
Evidence: We observed a significant decrease in the frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− expressing CD8+ and CD4+ T cells among HTLV-1 seropositive subjects (CD8+: median 8.01%, IQR 5.42–10.50; CD4+: median 4.3%, IQR 3.50–5.99) compared to HTLV-1 seronegative controls (CD8+ median 15.10%, IQR 10.50–17.60; CD4+: median 6.84%, IQR 5.74–7.85) (Figure 1A and B).

CD8+ T cell dysfunction and/or exhaustion are common features of many chronic viral infections, including HIV-1 and HCV infections ... In this study, we report that CD8+ and CD4+ T cells in HTLV-1 infection express lower levels of Tim-3...
168.Rationale: This option claims a higher frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− CD8+ T cells in infected individuals and interprets that as exhaustion. The passage actually documents a significant decrease in Tim-3+ PD-1− cells among HTLV-1 seropositive subjects compared with controls. Thus the factual premise of the option is incorrect relative to the study data, and the interpretation (that such a finding would indicate exhaustion) runs counter to the authors' emphasis that reduced Tim-3 marks hyperfunctional T cells in this setting.

168.Option: Lower Tim-3 expression on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells is associated with higher IFN-γ production, suggesting a hyperfunctional T cell state that may drive immunopathology
Evidence: Phenotypically, we observed that Tax HTLV-1-specific, HLA-A*02 -restricted CD8+ T cells consistently retain a lower frequency of Tim-3.

we observed a statistically significant inverse correlation between the frequency of Tim-3 on both Tax-specific as well as CMV-specific CD8+ T cells and the amount of IFN-γ secreted (r = −0.8982; P = 0.0046; r = 0.9710; P = 0.0028; Figure 3A).
We propose that this low expression of Tim-3 on HTLV-1 Tax-specific T cells may lead to a persistent and deleterious effector T cell pool leading to more inflammation.
168.Rationale: The passage provides direct functional evidence linking lower Tim-3 expression on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells to greater IFN-γ secretion (strong inverse correlation). The authors interpret low Tim-3 as marking hyperfunctional, potentially pathogenic T cells and propose this could lead to inflammation and disease. This finding most directly and strongly supports the hypothesis that persistent T cell activation contributes to disease progression.

168.Option: Elevated PD-1 expression on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells is associated with increased cytokine production, indicating a state of chronic T cell activation that contributes to disease progression
Evidence: The frequency of PD-1 expression on Tax-specific or CMV-specific CD8+ T cells also did not associate with the amount of IFN-γ secreted (r = 0.1317; P = 0.7520 and r = 0.2245; P = 0.594, respectively) (Figure 3B).
PD-1 appears to mark early T-cell activation and exhaustion, while Tim-3 represents a more terminal stage of impairment.
168.Rationale: The option claims elevated PD-1 is associated with increased cytokine production, but the study's data show no association between PD-1 frequency on antigen-specific CD8+ T cells and IFN-γ secretion. The authors further distinguish PD-1 (early activation/exhaustion marker) from Tim-3 (linked to terminal impairment), and the functional link to increased cytokine production in this cohort is made for low Tim-3 rather than elevated PD-1. Therefore this option is not supported by the data.

169.Option: Since ZIKV does not cause pathological damage in mosquito ovaries, it cannot be maintained in mosquito populations through vertical transmission
Evidence: No pathogenetic injury was found in the germarium, oocytes, trophocytes and follicular epithelium in the ZIKV-positive ovaries compared with the control ovaries.
Ae. albopictus of the Foshan strain can transmit ZIKV from G0 to the adult F1 generation, which is clear evidence that this vector mosquito can vertically transmit ZIKV.

Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.
vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.
169.Rationale: The option asserts that absence of pathological damage implies inability to be maintained by vertical transmission. The passage states explicitly that no pathogenetic injury was found in ovaries, but also provides direct evidence of vertical transmission to F1 adults and transmission from F1 females to mice, and concludes vertical transmission could help maintain ZIKV in vector populations. Thus the claim is contradicted by the experimental findings: lack of ovarian pathology does not preclude vertical transmission or maintenance; in fact vertical transmission with viable, infectious F1 adults was demonstrated.

169.Option: ZIKV vertical transmission in Ae. albopictus is limited to the first gonotrophic cycle and does not support long-term persistence in mosquito populations
Evidence: Starting from 4 dpi, the ovaries showed evidence of infection and remained positive throughout the remainder of the experiment (Fig 1A). During the FGC, the infection rate of the ovaries was 19.04% at 4 dpi, 30.43% at 7 dpi, 39.28% at 10 dpi and 75% at 14 dpi. During the SGC, the infection rates of the ovaries were similar and reached the maximum value (100%) at 33 dpi. The average infection rate of the ovaries in the SGC (93.75%) was significantly higher than that in the FGC (40.66%) (χ2 = 60.428, P <0.001).

In this study, we experimentally demonstrated that Ae. albopictus could transmit ZIKV vertically from a female parent to her F1 progeny (eggs and adults). Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.
169.Rationale: The option claims vertical transmission is limited to the first gonotrophic cycle; the passage shows the opposite—ovary infection increased and persisted into the second gonotrophic cycle (SGC), reaching 100% at 33 dpi, and F1 adults were infected and infectious. Therefore vertical transmission is not limited to the first cycle and the data support potential persistence across cycles.

169.Option: The absence of significant differences in hatching, pupation, and emergence rates between ZIKV-infected and control mosquitoes implies that vertical transmission has no ecological impact on mosquito populations
Evidence: In the control group, the rates of hatching, pupation and emergence were 61.01%, 93.08% and 95.15%, respectively, while the rates in the ZIKV group were 57.49%, 91.19% and 98.12%, respectively. No significant difference was found between them. These results suggested that ZIKV infection did not prevent the growth and development of the offspring.

However, at 33 dpi of the SGC, the number of eggs in the control groups was significantly higher than that in the ZIKV groups (Fig 3B) (t = 2.767, P = 0.008). As Fig 3C shows, oviposition rates were calculated, and no significant difference was detected, which suggested that ZIKV may negatively influence fecundity but not oviposition of the infected mosquitoes.
vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.
169.Rationale: The option overgeneralizes from some null differences (hatching/pupation/emergence) to conclude no ecological impact. The passage reports no significant differences in those life-stage rates but also documents a significant reduction in number of eggs at 33 dpi (negative influence on oogenesis/fecundity) and emphasizes that vertical transmission could influence maintenance/endemicity. Therefore it is incorrect to conclude vertical transmission has no ecological impact.

169.Option: Vertical transmission enables Zika virus to persist in mosquito populations across generations, allowing for re-initiation of human transmission cycles even without ongoing external exposure
Evidence: Ae. albopictus of the Foshan strain can transmit ZIKV from G0 to the adult F1 generation, which is clear evidence that this vector mosquito can vertically transmit ZIKV.

In this study, we experimentally demonstrated that Ae. albopictus could transmit ZIKV vertically from a female parent to her F1 progeny (eggs and adults). Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.

congenitally infected females are able to carry the virus for their entire life and transmit the virus horizontally by feeding without the requirement for an extrinsic incubation period (approximately 8 to 14 days) [31].

Considering this situation, the low filial infection rates indicated that vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.
169.Rationale: The experimental data demonstrate transovarial vertical transmission to F1 adults and show that vertically infected F1 females can infect mammalian hosts via bites. Congenitally infected females can be infectious for life without an extrinsic incubation period, and the authors explicitly state vertical transmission could help maintain ZIKV in vector populations and contribute to endemicity. These findings support the conclusion that vertical transmission can enable persistence across generations and re-initiation of human transmission without continuous external input.

169.Option: The low filial infection rate of 2.06% in F1 eggs indicates that vertical transmission is insufficient to sustain ZIKV in mosquito populations over time
Evidence: In this study, the minimal filial infection rates in eggs from two gonotrophic cycles were 2.06% and 0.69%, without statistical significance (p>0.05).
In adult mosquitoes, the effective population transmission rate (minimum of 1.87%) was calculated in this study.

Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.

Considering this situation, the low filial infection rates indicated that vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.
169.Rationale: While filial infection rates are low (e.g., 2.06%), the study nevertheless detected infected F1 adults capable of transmitting to mammals and calculated an effective population transmission rate (1.87%). The authors interpret vertical transmission as an alternate mechanism that could help maintain ZIKV in vector populations and contribute to endemicity. Therefore the presence of low but nonzero vertical transmission does not by itself prove insufficiency to sustain the virus; the experimental findings support potential persistence despite low rates, making the option incorrect.

170.Option: The higher proportion of rural health facilities in Tororo district compared to Wakiso district led to lower RHD service readiness due to geographic isolation and transportation barriers
Evidence: All four districts had a mix of urban and rural administrative units.
Wakiso district (population 2.0 million) is adjacent to Kampala in the Central region and is the second most populated district in the country.
We found that the Tororo district in the Eastern region generally had the lowest scores on all indicators in this study.
ongoing research by our group has begun to shed light on the significant transportation barriers and catastrophic out-of-pocket costs faced by Ugandans with RHD.[25]
170.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that all four districts had a mix of urban and rural administrative units, so asserting that Tororo simply has a higher proportion of rural facilities is not supported by the text. While transportation barriers for patients are discussed, the authors attribute Tororo's low readiness to lack of prior RHD research/clinical activities and being more representative of districts with limited RHD services, rather than to a higher rural facility proportion or geographic isolation relative to Wakiso. The passage also highlights Wakiso's proximity to Kampala and larger private sector as plausible explanations for better readiness, which is a different mechanism than the option proposes.

170.Option: The absence of prior RHD-specific research and clinical programs in Tororo district likely contributed to lower health system capacity and readiness compared to districts with such initiatives
Evidence: We found that the Tororo district in the Eastern region generally had the lowest scores on all indicators in this study.
Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.

In this way, Tororo is probably more like many of the other districts in Uganda that have limited or no RHD services, and therefore it is most likely to be representative of the national situation outside of Kampala, Lira, or Mbarara as well as other conditions in other countries in sub-Saharan Africa.

Though the overall level of health worker awareness was low in our sample, the proportion of health workers that had received training was higher in Lira and Mbarara districts compared to Wakiso and Tororo districts. Awareness may have been increased due to concurrent RHD research activities in those two districts.
170.Rationale: The authors explicitly link Tororo's low readiness scores to the fact that it had not been a site for prior RHD research or clinical activities, and they note that districts with research activities (Lira, Mbarara) had higher health worker training/awareness. This provides direct, text-supported evidence that absence of prior RHD-specific programs is a key, district-specific explanatory factor for the observed disparity in service readiness.

170.Option: The lower number of nurses per health facility in Tororo district compared to Wakiso district resulted in reduced RHD service readiness due to workforce shortages
Evidence: A higher number of nurses in a health facility was also associated with a significantly higher RHD readiness score.
We found that the Tororo district in the Eastern region generally had the lowest scores on all indicators in this study.
Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.
170.Rationale: While the passage states that facilities with more nurses had higher readiness scores (establishing a general association between nurse staffing and readiness), it does not provide district-level data showing Tororo had fewer nurses than Wakiso. The text instead points to the absence of RHD research/clinical programs (and proximity to capital/private facility mix) as explanations for readiness differences. Because the specific claim that Tororo has a lower number of nurses than Wakiso is not supported by verbatim evidence in the passage, this option cannot be taken as the most direct explanation.

170.Option: Differences in the availability of essential antibiotics like benzathine penicillin G and amoxicillin across districts explain the variation in RHD service readiness despite similar population-level risk factors
Evidence: Amoxicillin and benzathine penicillin G were widely available across all health facility levels.

When we compared service readiness across districts, Wakiso district facilities had the highest mean readiness score for primary prevention overall, while Tororo district had the lowest (Fig B in S1 Text).
These drugs were less likely to be available at primary healthcare facilities, including at health centers III and especially at health centers II.
170.Rationale: The passage reports that amoxicillin and benzathine penicillin G were 'widely available across all health facility levels,' which argues against large inter-district differences in basic antibiotic availability being the primary driver of the observed readiness gap. The authors point instead to lack of prior RHD research/clinical activities and other system-level factors as explanations for district differences. Although antibiotic availability varied by facility level (e.g., HC II versus HC IV/hospital), the text does not attribute the Tororo vs. Wakiso disparity to district-level differences in these antibiotics.

170.Option: The lack of national policy prioritization for RHD in Tororo district, unlike in Wakiso, led to lower service readiness due to insufficient government funding and political will
Evidence: Participants also expressed concern over lack of national policies concerning RHD. They felt that, because of insufficient policy prioritization, funding for RHD services in their districts has remained poor:

"..right now… [the government] are not even aware that [RHD is] there… I have always sat on meetings, [and] we have always done the budget together, but there has never been any funding for RHD." (HW 008 Lira)
The 71st World Health Assembly (WHA) adopted a Resolution on RHD in 2018, mandating Member States to prioritize RHD control programs.
Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.
170.Rationale: Although the passage documents a general lack of national policy prioritization for RHD (a country-wide issue affecting funding and attention), it does not present evidence that Tororo uniquely lacked national policy support while Wakiso did not. The authors point to the absence of local RHD research/clinical activities as the more direct, district-specific explanation for Tororo's particularly low readiness. Therefore, while lack of national policy is an important systemic problem, it is not the most direct district-level factor explaining the observed disparity according to the text.

171.Option: The detection of viral antigen and RNA in the brain and lungs of animals that reached endpoint criteria, but not in surviving animals, confirms that NiV replication was necessary for disease development
Evidence: The brains of animals that reached euthanasia criteria showed a consistently high viral load, regardless of the inoculation dose (Fig 2A).
Similarly, high virus titers were observed in the brains of these animals (Fig 2B).
Although viral RNA was detected in the brains of some surviving animals (Fig 2A), infectious virus could not be detected (Fig 2B).
Neither lesions nor viral antigen were detected in animals who survived the intracranial challenge or mock-inoculated animals (Fig 5J and 5K).
In all animals that reached endpoint criteria, small foci of viral antigen were detected in type I and type II pneumocytes, and occasionally within endothelial cells (Fig 3B and 3D).
Multifocal to regionally extensive virus antigen and viral RNA positivity was detected throughout the leptomeninges on IHC and ISH, respectively (Fig 5D and 5G).
171.Rationale: The presence of high viral RNA and infectious virus titers, and of viral antigen/RNA by IHC/ISH, specifically in animals that developed severe neurological disease and euthanasia criteria (and the absence of lesions/antigen in surviving/mock animals), is strong direct evidence that active NiV replication was associated with and necessary for the observed disease. The passage explicitly documents high viral loads and antigen in affected animals and lack of infectious virus/antigen in survivors, supporting the conclusion that virus replication — not procedural factors — caused the disease.

171.Option: The absence of neutrophilic infiltrates in mock-inoculated animals and the identification of a non-bacterial origin for the 16S rRNA signal rule out procedural trauma and bacterial contamination as causes of the neurological disease
Evidence: However, no bacteria were visible in tissue slides and neutrophils were not observed in intracranially inoculated mock animals.

To definitively prove that no bacterial contamination occurred during the inoculation procedure, RNA was extracted from brain tissues and 16S rRNA RT-PCR was performed. We detected a nonspecific amplified product of 650 bp (S4A Fig). Sanger sequencing showed the amplified product corresponds to a mitochondrial gene of the Syrian hamster (S4B Fig).
Thus, the neutrophilic infiltrate observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters was due to NiV infection.
171.Rationale: These quotes show the investigators checked for bacterial contamination and procedural artifacts and found none: mock-inoculated animals lacked neutrophilic infiltrates, no bacteria were seen in slides, and the 16S PCR product was hamster mitochondrial sequence rather than bacterial sequence. That evidence directly rules out bacterial contamination or a procedural contamination artifact as the cause of the neutrophil-rich lesions and supports that NiV infection induced the pathology.

171.Option: The rapid progression of disease within 2–6 days post-inoculation, consistent with direct CNS access, supports that NiV was the cause of neurological disease rather than procedural effects
Evidence: Animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M uniformly displayed severe neurological signs and reached endpoint criteria within 2–6 days post inoculation (dpi) (Fig 1).
Thus, direct inoculation of NiV into the cerebellomedullary cistern results in a rapid progression to severe neurological disease.
Direct inoculation of NiV into the cerebellomedullary cistern provides the virus an opportunity to easily breach these barriers, partially explaining the rapid development of disease.
171.Rationale: Rapid, dose-consistent onset of severe neurological signs shortly after CNS inoculation is consistent with an infectious cause acting via direct CNS access. While procedural injury can cause acute effects, the consistency of timing and the biological plausibility provided by immediate CNS access (explicitly described in the passage) support the interpretation that the virus — not nonspecific procedural effects — drove the rapid neurologic deterioration.

171.Option: The uniformity of neurological signs across all animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M, combined with the absence of signs in mock-inoculated animals, indicates that the disease was specifically caused by NiV
Evidence: Animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M uniformly displayed severe neurological signs and reached endpoint criteria within 2–6 days post inoculation (dpi) (Fig 1).
All mock-inoculated animals survived until study endpoint at 14 dpi (Fig 1).
Neither lesions nor viral antigen were detected in animals who survived the intracranial challenge or mock-inoculated animals (Fig 5J and 5K).
Animals were randomly assigned to groups by veterinary care staff.
171.Rationale: The combination of (a) consistent, uniform neurological disease among virus-inoculated animals, (b) survival and lack of lesions/antigen in mock-inoculated controls, and (c) random assignment of animals to groups (reducing systematic bias) most directly supports a causal link between NiV inoculation and the observed neurologic disease rather than a procedural artifact. The mock control rules out effects of the injection procedure itself; random assignment ensures comparability between groups.

171.Option: The dose-dependent development of neurological disease, with no signs at doses below 0.04 TCID50, supports a causal link to NiV rather than procedural or non-specific factors
Evidence: The time to euthanasia was dose-dependent, with the animals that received the highest dose reaching euthanasia criteria as early as 2 dpi, and animals that received lower doses reaching euthanasia criteria 1–2 days later.
Lowering the inoculation dose to 0.2 TCID50 resulted in only 50% of animals requiring euthanasia between 4–6 dpi; no signs of disease were observed in the surviving animals.
Doses of 0.04 TCID50 or below did not result in disease signs nor lethality.
171.Rationale: A dose-response relationship (more frequent/severe disease at higher inoculum and absence of disease below a threshold dose) is a classical indicator of causality in experimental studies. This pattern argues that the virus dose determines disease occurrence and severity, making it unlikely that non-specific procedural factors (which would not be expected to vary with viral dose) are responsible.

172.Option: Direct transmission from infected cat fleas to other arthropods via cofeeding on a shared vertebrate host can explain R. felis presence in non-flea vectors
Evidence: Thus, horizontal transmission of R. felis from infected to naïve fleas via cofeeding was determined to be sufficient to perpetuate the agent within populations of cat fleas [8,9].

To test the hypothesis that R. felis-infected cat fleas may contribute to dissemination of the agent to other hematophagous vectors, cofeeding bioassays were employed to examine rickettsial transmission from infected cat fleas (C. felis) to ticks (D. variabilis) and mosquitoes (Anopheles quadrimaculatus) on a vertebrate host.
The data suggest R. felis-infected cat fleas transmit rickettsiae to other hematophagous vectors via cofeeding on a vertebrate host.
R. felis was detected in 75% (15/20) D. variabilis nymphs that were forcibly removed from the host 24 hrs after cofeeding.
Naïve An. quadrimaculatus were able to acquire infection through cofeeding with R. felis-infected cat fleas.
172.Rationale: The passage directly demonstrates and states that cofeeding from infected cat fleas can transmit R. felis to other arthropods (both ticks and mosquitoes) feeding on the same vertebrate host. Experimental cofeeding bioassays and measured infection proportions in recipient ticks and mosquitoes provide direct evidence that flea-to-other-arthropod transmission via cofeeding is a plausible mechanism explaining detection of R. felis in non-flea vectors.

172.Option: Vertical transmission from infected female ticks to their offspring could account for R. felis presence in non-flea arthropods, even without vertebrate host involvement
Evidence: More recently, in sub-Saharan African countries where R. felis has been reported as an emerging cause of unknown fever, R. felis has been detected in mosquitoes [3,15,16]. Detection of R. felis in male mosquitoes in China and unfed adult mosquitoes in the United States suggests vertical transmission in nature [17,18].
laboratory studies have demonstrated R. felis was vertically transmitted by the American dog tick, Dermacentor variabilis, when adult ticks were exposed via capillary feeding [14].
Under laboratory conditions, adult D. variabilis are susceptible to infection and subsequent transovarial transmission of R. felis for at least one generation [14].
172.Rationale: The passage provides laboratory evidence that vertical (transovarial) transmission can occur in at least some non-flea arthropods (ticks) and notes patterns in mosquitoes consistent with vertical transmission in nature. Therefore vertical transmission in arthropods is a plausible mechanism that could maintain R. felis within non-flea populations without requiring vertebrate-mediated transmission. The passage also indicates limitations (e.g., variable vertical transmission in fleas and that some aspects such as maintenance in F1 larvae were not assessed), so while vertical transmission is plausible and supported by lab data, its sufficiency to explain widespread detection in nature may be limited and context-dependent.

172.Option: R. felis may be transmitted from infected vertebrate hosts to non-flea arthropods through blood-feeding, even if the pathogen is not detectable in the host's bloodstream
Evidence: cofeeding mechanism has been described for both flea- and tick- borne rickettsial pathogens, and occurs in the absence of a rickettsemic host [9,40].

There are several pieces of evidence supporting the potential for horizontal transmission of R. felis to vertebrate hosts, such as identification of R. felis in the salivary glands of cat fleas [41] and detection of R. felis in the blood of vertebrate hosts exposed to R. felis-infected fleas [5,42–44].
The detection of R. felis in molecular surveys of mosquitoes do not address the origin of rickettsial infection.
172.Rationale: The passage emphasizes cofeeding transmission that occurs without a rickettsemic (bloodstream) host, which specifically undermines the idea that vertebrate blood (even undetectable) is the primary conduit. Although R. felis has been detected in the blood of some vertebrates, the authors highlight that cofeeding can transmit infection in the absence of detectable rickettsemia and that the origin of infections detected in vectors is often unresolved. The specific claim that vertebrate-to-arthropod transmission can occur reliably even when the pathogen is undetectable in host blood is not supported by the passage and is contradicted by the focus on cofeeding as a mechanism that bypasses a rickettsemic host.

172.Option: R. felis may persist in the environment and be acquired by non-flea arthropods through contact with contaminated surfaces on the vertebrate host
Evidence: Fleas deposit rickettsiae in the dermis at the vector-host skin interface resulting in dissemination across the skin [9], presumably where other arthropods, including ticks that have mouthparts in the same tissue, can acquire the agent during feeding events;
cofeeding mechanism ... occurs in the absence of a rickettsemic host [9,40].
All flea, tick, and mosquito samples were washed with 10% bleach for 5 mins, 70% ethanol for 5 mins, and three times with sterile distilled H2O for 5 mins to remove environmental DNA.
172.Rationale: The passage describes dermal deposition at the vector–host skin interface and acquisition during feeding (cofeeding), not environmental persistence on surfaces as a route of transmission. The authors also took steps to remove environmental DNA from samples, indicating concern about contamination but not supporting environmental acquisition as a demonstrated transmission route. There is no experimental or observational evidence in the passage that R. felis persists as an environmentally stable agent on host surfaces and is acquired by other arthropods via contact; the data instead support direct transfer during feeding (cofeeding) or vertical transmission in vectors.

172.Option: Horizontal transmission of R. felis from infected mosquitoes to other arthropods via cofeeding on a vertebrate host could explain the presence of the pathogen in non-flea vectors
Evidence: To test the hypothesis that R. felis-infected cat fleas may contribute to dissemination of the agent to other hematophagous vectors, cofeeding bioassays were employed to examine rickettsial transmission from infected cat fleas (C. felis) to ticks (D. variabilis) and mosquitoes (Anopheles quadrimaculatus) on a vertebrate host.
Naïve An. quadrimaculatus were able to acquire infection through cofeeding with R. felis-infected cat fleas.
Thus, while field and laboratory studies support the transmission potential of R. felis by alternate vectors, conduits for introduction of the agent into arthropod populations remain undefined.
172.Rationale: The passage provides experimental evidence for flea-to-tick and flea-to-mosquito transmission via cofeeding, but it does not present data showing mosquitoes acting as donors that transmit R. felis to other arthropods via cofeeding. The authors state that conduits for introduction into arthropod populations remain undefined beyond the demonstrated flea-to-other transmission. Therefore, mosquito-to-other-arthropod horizontal transmission via cofeeding is speculative and not supported by the experiments or observations in the passage.

173.Option: Delayed sample collection may lead to underestimation of acute infections, particularly for pathogens with short-lived biomarkers like NS1 antigen or rickettsial DNA, and reliance on serology without paired samples may misclassify past exposure as current infection
Evidence: convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.
Of 76 (30%) patients with dengue as a single disease, 71/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion;
Our PCR positive R. typhi patient had 8 days of fever before admission to hospital.
Murai et al. [58] found that Orientia tsutsugamushi DNA was detectable in patient’s blood until day 3–8 after the start of chemotherapy with minocycline or doxycycline.

Forty percent of pregnant women in this study did not have confirmed diagnoses; of these 18% received antibiotics before collecting samples for cultures, which may have reduced the frequency of positive culture results [63].
caution should be used when mixed diseases are based just on serology [48].
173.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that missing convalescent samples likely led to missed seroconversions and potential under-diagnosis (first quote). Acute markers (NS1/PCR) identified most dengue cases when present (second quote), but PCR positivity for rickettsial infections is time-limited and can be lost within days (third and fourth quotes). Prior antibiotic use can reduce culture yield (fifth quote), and the authors warn that serology-only diagnoses (without paired samples) require caution (sixth quote). Together these statements support the conclusion that delayed sampling will underestimate some acute infections and that relying on single serologic results risks misclassifying past exposure as current infection.

173.Option: The use of PCR and antigen detection methods at admission provides the most reliable evidence of acute infection, minimizing the risk of misclassification due to timing of sample collection
Evidence: Of 76 (30%) patients with dengue as a single disease, 71/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion;
Our PCR positive R. typhi patient had 8 days of fever before admission to hospital.
Murai et al. [58] found that Orientia tsutsugamushi DNA was detectable in patient’s blood until day 3–8 after the start of chemotherapy with minocycline or doxycycline.

convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.
173.Rationale: While PCR and antigen (NS1) detection were valuable and detected most dengue cases when positive (first quote), the passage also shows that nucleic-acid detection can be transient or dependent on timing and/or treatment (second and third quotes). The authors additionally emphasize missing convalescent samples and missed seroconversions (fourth quote). Therefore, PCR/antigen at admission is useful but not universally sufficient to minimize misclassification caused by delayed sampling; the option overstates reliability.

173.Option: The absence of convalescent samples limits the ability to confirm acute infection for all pathogens, but this is not a major concern because most infections are self-limiting and severity is determined by clinical presentation alone
Evidence: convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.

Of 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).

These data suggest that dengue control, optimization of dengue-related fluid balance management during pregnancy and optimization of early diagnosis and treatment of rickettsial diseases and typhoid in pregnancy are needed.
173.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the absence of convalescent samples likely led to missed seroconversions (first quote). The claim that this is not a major concern is contradicted by documented severe pregnancy outcomes from infections such as dengue, including maternal death, miscarriage, stillbirth and congenital abnormality (second quote). The authors conclude that improved diagnosis and early treatment are needed (third quote), indicating that diagnostic limitations are an important concern, not a trivial one. Thus the option's minimizing statement is not supported.

173.Option: Testing for all pathogens simultaneously at admission ensures accurate assessment of disease severity, as it captures the full spectrum of infections regardless of the timing of symptom onset
Evidence: We focused on specific diseases that local evidence suggested might be important, including septicemia, pyelonephritis, rickettsioses (scrub typhus, murine typhus and spotted fever group rickettsia), leptospirosis, malaria, dengue fever, Japanese encephalitis virus (JEV) infection and HEV infection (S1).
we were not able to investigate diverse other important viral infections in pregnancy [64] or other potential pathogens such as Coxiella burnetii.

Forty percent of pregnant women in this study did not have confirmed diagnoses; of these 18% received antibiotics before collecting samples for cultures, which may have reduced the frequency of positive culture results [63].

convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.
173.Rationale: The passage describes that the investigators tested a focused panel of pathogens and explicitly were unable to investigate other important viral infections or pathogens (first and second quotes). It also documents practical reasons why admission testing alone may miss infections (prior antibiotics reducing culture yield and missing convalescent samples leading to missed seroconversions) (third and fourth quotes). Therefore simultaneous admission testing does not guarantee accurate assessment regardless of timing; timing and the breadth of testing both limit detection and interpretation.

174.Option: The 2011–2012 cluster of genotype 1.1 isolates represents a new, locally evolved variant of S. Typhi that emerged independently in the modern era due to selective pressure from widespread antibiotic use
Evidence: The twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.
A potential explanation to account for this cluster is that it was an outbreak caused by bacteria shed from a long-term carrier.
Amongst the 151 non-H58 isolates, few antimicrobial resistance mechanisms were detected.
174.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that the 2011–2012 cluster's low SNP distances make independent, rapid local evolution during acute transmission unlikely and instead proposes a chronic carrier outbreak as the likely explanation. Moreover, the text states few antimicrobial resistance mechanisms were detected among non-H58 isolates, so there is no evidence of widespread antibiotic-driven selection producing a new resistant/variant lineage in the modern era. Therefore the option claiming a new locally evolved variant due to antibiotic selective pressure is not supported by the evidence.

174.Option: Sporadic typhoid cases in Santiago during the 2010s are primarily due to re-emergence of environmental transmission from untreated sewage irrigation, as evidenced by the persistence of ancient S. Typhi genotypes in the modern population
Evidence: In April 1991, the government of Chile abruptly prohibited the practice of irrigating crops with untreated sewage wastewater in Santiago.

Following imposition and strict enforcement of the prohibition of use of untreated sewage water for irrigation, the Santiago cholera epidemic quickly came to an end, and the incidence of typhoid dropped precipitously in subsequent years.

The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.

The observation that the 1980s and 2010s collections of S. Typhi isolates do not cluster independently within our commonly found genotypes ... suggests that many of the sporadic cases detected in the 2010s share common ancestry with S. Typhi cases from the 1980s. These may, in turn, be linked to shedding of S. Typhi from chronic typhoid carriers.
174.Rationale: The passage documents the 1991 ban on using untreated sewage for irrigation and links that policy to the precipitous decline in typhoid incidence, arguing against re-emergent environmental irrigation as the primary driver. Instead, the authors identify travellers, immigrants, and especially chronic carriers (infected pre-1991) as sources of current sporadic cases, and genomic evidence of shared ancestry is interpreted as consistent with shedding from long-term human carriers. Thus environmental re-emergence from irrigation is not the primary inference supported by the data.

174.Option: Sporadic typhoid cases in Santiago during the 2010s are caused by imported strains from high-endemic regions, as indicated by the presence of H58 genotypes in the modern isolates
Evidence: we also detected 11 additional distinct genotypes across the two time periods. These include three previously described importations of isolates belonging to H58 genotypes 4.3.1 and 4.3.1.2.

Although there have been several detected introductions of the globally dominant H58 S. Typhi clade (4.3.1) into Santiago, as occurs with most industrialized countries in North America, Europe, and Australia, this was not a frequent genotype found in the sporadic modern Chilean cases.
174.Rationale: While the passage notes occasional introductions of H58 (imported) isolates, it explicitly states H58 was not a frequent genotype among sporadic modern cases. The genomic and epidemiologic conclusions emphasize persistence of local, ancient genotypes and likely contribution from chronic local carriers rather than widespread importation as the primary cause. Therefore the blanket statement that sporadic 2010s cases are caused by imported H58 strains is not supported.

174.Option: The 2011–2012 cluster of genotype 1.1 isolates likely resulted from a single environmental exposure event, such as a contaminated food source, given the high genetic similarity among isolates
Evidence: The twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.
A potential explanation to account for this cluster is that it was an outbreak caused by bacteria shed from a long-term carrier.

Thus, the closely related nature of these isolates occurring mainly over a single year and during a period when typhoid was no longer hyperendemic in Santiago could be explained by an outbreak instigated by a chronic carrier who was shedding multiple highly related strains.

If this hypothetical carrier was working as a food handler, intermittently contaminating food or water consumed by subjects during 2011–12, this could result in the cluster of closely related isolates we observe.
174.Rationale: The authors favor an explanation of a chronic carrier intermittently shedding closely related strains (possibly contaminating food intermittently if a food handler), rather than a single one-time environmental exposure. The passage explicitly suggests a long-term carrier outbreak producing multiple related isolates over the period. Thus the specific claim of a single environmental exposure event is not the inference supported by the genomic and epidemiologic data.

174.Option: Sporadic typhoid cases in Santiago during the 2010s are likely sustained by long-term human carriers who harbor and intermittently shed genetically stable S. Typhi lineages originating from the hyperendemic era
Evidence: The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.

The observation that the 1980s and 2010s collections of S. Typhi isolates do not cluster independently within our commonly found genotypes (2, 3.5 and 1.2.1) suggests that many of the sporadic cases detected in the 2010s share common ancestry with S. Typhi cases from the 1980s. These may, in turn, be linked to shedding of S. Typhi from chronic typhoid carriers.

As such, it is unlikely that these isolates originated from a clonal source and sequentially acquired this genetic variation in <2 years. Conversely, these strains are clearly highly related and were all collected during a short time span. A potential explanation to account for this cluster is that it was an outbreak caused by bacteria shed from a long-term carrier.
This genomics analysis suggests that asymptomatic typhoid carriers (or other unknown reservoirs) are likely responsible for continuing sporadic typhoid cases in Chile.
174.Rationale: The passage repeatedly links the persistence of historical genotypes in the 2010s to chronic human carriers as the most parsimonious source: genomic similarity across decades implies shared ancestry with 1980s strains, the authors propose chronic carriers shedding intermittently (including a specific discussion of the 2011–2012 cluster as likely carrier-related), and the conclusion explicitly states asymptomatic carriers are likely responsible for ongoing sporadic cases. Therefore this option best matches the authors' inference about long-term transmission dynamics.

175.Option: The dose-dependent and reversible nature of PZQ-induced phenotypes suggests a shared mechanism involving modulation of excitable cell function, likely through disruption of bioaminergic signaling pathways.
Evidence: Exposure of regenerating planarian (D. japonica) trunk fragments to praziquantel (PZQ) yielded two-headed worms (Fig 1A, [17]), an effect never observed in the absence of drug exposure. This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].

Acute incubation of intact worms with PZQ caused worms to adopt a spastic, curled morphology with inhibitory effects on worm motion (Fig 4A). This effect was dose-dependent (Fig 4B), with a concentration-dependence similar to that observed for the (longer term) polarity effect (EC50 = 38±3.6μM for bipolarity in trunk fragments versus EC50 = 23±2.4μM for mobility in intact worms, Figs 1B and 4B) and reversible following drug removal (Fig 4C).

Strikingly, the bipolarizing action of PZQ was blocked by co-incubation with 5-HT, or the analogue O-methylserotonin (O-MT), but not by co-incubation with other bioaminergic neurotransmitters (Fig 1C).
These data show that PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.

What is the molecular basis of this phenology between planarian regenerative polarity and schistosome motility? An appealing explanation relates to the recent identification of muscle cells as the coordinating nexus of positional signaling during planarian regeneration ... drugs which miscue regenerative patterning all possess a shared ability to modulate excitable cell physiology and perturb muscle function.

PZQ action in planarians has previously been shown to depend on Cav1A and tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) functionality (red, [18,20]). Activity of this pathway is opposed by Cav1B and tryptophan hydroxylase functionality (blue, [18,20]).
175.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes dose-dependent, concentration-matched effects on both polarity and motility that are reversible and antagonized by serotonergic agonists (O-MT/5-HT). It places these drug-evoked phenotypes in the context of excitable cell (muscle) physiology and bioaminergic (dopamine/serotonin) signaling and links PZQ action to Cav channel–bioaminergic pathways. Together these observations support interpretation that the concentration–response relationship reflects modulation of excitable-cell/bioaminergic signaling rather than nonspecific toxicity, making this option correct.

175.Option: The concentration-dependent induction of bipolarity indicates that PZQ acts as a direct agonist of serotonergic receptors, leading to aberrant head specification during regeneration.
Evidence: Strikingly, the bipolarizing action of PZQ was blocked by co-incubation with 5-HT, or the analogue O-methylserotonin (O-MT), but not by co-incubation with other bioaminergic neurotransmitters (Fig 1C).
These data show that PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.

While diverse classes of serotonergic blockers caused regenerative bipolarity, the penetrance was typically much lower than seen with PZQ (Table A in S1 Text). Results with ergot alkaloids were however of interest.
175.Rationale: The passage states that 5-HT (a serotonergic agonist) blocks PZQ-evoked bipolarity and that PZQ mimics serotonergic blockers. That pattern is inconsistent with PZQ being a direct serotonergic agonist; instead PZQ behaves like a serotonergic antagonist (or otherwise opposes serotonergic activation). Therefore Option 1 is incorrect.

175.Option: The similar EC50 values for regenerative polarity and motility defects suggest that PZQ exerts its effects through a non-specific cytotoxic mechanism that damages cellular integrity at high concentrations.
Evidence: This effect was dose-dependent (Fig 4B), with a concentration-dependence similar to that observed for the (longer term) polarity effect (EC50 = 38±3.6μM for bipolarity in trunk fragments versus EC50 = 23±2.4μM for mobility in intact worms, Figs 1B and 4B) and reversible following drug removal (Fig 4C).

Antagonism of acute PZQ-evoked mobility defects in intact worms (75μM) by co-incubation with O-MT but not other neurotransmitters (all at 100μM). *, p < 0.01 relative to PZQ (dashed line). PZQ-evoked mobility defects are reversible on solution exchange (‘wash’).
drugs which miscue regenerative patterning all possess a shared ability to modulate excitable cell physiology and perturb muscle function.
175.Rationale: A non-specific cytotoxic mechanism would be expected to produce irreversible damage and not be antagonizable by neurotransmitter agonists. The passage emphasizes reversibility on wash and pharmacologic antagonism by 5-HT/O-MT, and ties effects to modulation of excitable cell/muscle physiology rather than generic cellular destruction. Thus the similar EC50s are better explained by a shared, specific target in excitable cells, not nonspecific cytotoxicity; Option 2 is incorrect.

175.Option: The concentration-dependent induction of both polarity and motility defects implies that PZQ acts as a broad-spectrum neuromodulator that disrupts multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously.
Evidence: These data show that PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.

We have previously suggested that PZQ engages dopaminergic pathways to subvert regeneration and it is noteworthy that both dopamine and serotonin regulate cAMP turnover with opposing effects on flatworm musculature [34–36].

PZQ action in planarians has previously been shown to depend on Cav1A and tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) functionality (red, [18,20]). Activity of this pathway is opposed by Cav1B and tryptophan hydroxylase functionality (blue, [18,20]).

This ‘functional antagonism’ model (Fig 6, [20]) envisages opposing Ca2+ entry pathways (Cav1A versus Cav1B) coupling to discrete bioaminergic neurotransmitters that differentially couple to cAMP within the excitable cell niche.
175.Rationale: The authors present a model of specific, opposing bioaminergic pathways (dopaminergic vs serotonergic) coupled to Cav channels and cAMP rather than indiscriminate disruption of many neurotransmitter systems. While some uncertainty remains (they do not absolutely exclude direct interactions with bioaminergic receptors), the evidence supports targeted modulation of specific excitable-cell signaling axes rather than a broad-spectrum, nonspecific neuromodulation. Therefore this option overstates and is not supported by the text.

175.Option: The dose-response relationship supports the hypothesis that PZQ induces bipolarity by directly activating Wnt signaling pathways, which are known to regulate body axis formation in planarians.
Evidence: dopamine and serotonin also are known regulators of Wnt signaling. D2Rs selectively associate with both β-catenin (to inhibit Wnt signals [40]) and Cav channels (to regulate their expression [41]). 5-HT is a well-established wounding signal [42], long range messenger involved in regenerative proliferation [43,44] and a reciprocally permissive cue for Wnt signaling [45,46].

Such associations provide precedence for coupling bioaminergic activity to the more established players of planarian regenerative signaling that localize with a myocyte population likely regulated by bioaminergic cues.

This is most conservatively explained by functional antagonism of the opposing Cav1A/dopaminergic pathway, however the possibility that PZQ directly targets flatworm bioaminergic receptors as an ergomimetic cannot be excluded until heterologous expression of platyhelminth GpCRs is optimized.
175.Rationale: Although Wnt signaling is implicated downstream of bioaminergic modulation, the passage does not claim PZQ directly activates Wnt pathways. Instead, it argues PZQ perturbs excitable-cell/bioaminergic signaling that can influence Wnt-regulated patterning. The authors favor a model of Cav channel–bioaminergic interaction rather than direct Wnt activation by PZQ, so Option 4 is not supported by the text.

176.Option: Use of a decision tree model that incorporates both clinical symptoms and laboratory parameters to improve diagnostic accuracy
Evidence: To address this goal, we show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.

Furthermore, many dengue endemic places lack the necessary laboratory infrastructure or support [7] and thus a scheme for reliable clinical diagnosis, using data that can be obtained routinely, would be useful for early recognition of dengue fever

decision algorithms are in principle simple to understand and are able to handle both nominal and categorical data. Importantly, they are also able to handle missing values, which are commonly encountered in clinical studies.
176.Rationale: This option describes a modeling choice (including clinical and laboratory inputs) that can improve diagnostic performance but does not itself constitute a study design feature that most directly supports causal inference in a retrospective outcome-selected study. The passage shows decision trees use clinical and haematological parameters, but also highlights limited laboratory infrastructure in many endemic settings. While inclusion of lab parameters can increase predictive accuracy, causal inference in a retrospective case-control–style design requires temporally prior, objective measurement and design choices that address bias and temporality rather than simply the model inputs.

176.Option: Application of k-fold cross-validation with k=10 to assess model performance and reduce overfitting in the decision tree
Evidence: The calculated algorithms were validated using the k-fold cross-validation approach. This is considered to be a powerful methodology to overcome data over-fitting [15].
The fold value was set to k = 10.
176.Rationale: k-fold cross-validation (k=10) is an important model-validation technique that the authors used to reduce overfitting and assess predictive performance, but it is a method for internal model validation rather than a study design feature that strengthens causal inference. Cross-validation improves reliability of predictive accuracy estimates, but it does not establish temporality, reduce recall or selection bias inherent in retrospective outcome-selected designs, nor does it by itself improve causal interpretation of predictor–outcome associations.

176.Option: Inclusion of both dengue and non-dengue cases from multiple geographic sites to ensure representation of diverse clinical presentations
Evidence: We constructed a decision tree for dengue diagnosis with 1,200 patients with acute febrile illness. Of these, 1,012 were recruited from the EDEN study and 188 from Vietnam.

Since the course of disease in all but three of the Singaporean adult cases were consistent with DF instead of DHF, we have included 188 DHF/DSS paediatric and adult cases from Vietnam in order to ensure that the diagnostic algorithm developed here is robust across a spectrum of dengue presentations.

The decision algorithm for the diagnosis of dengue within the first three days of illness made use of a combination of platelet count, total white cell count, body temperature, absolute lymphocyte and neutrophil counts, in sequential order (Figure 1A).
176.Rationale: Including cases from multiple sites and both dengue and non-dengue presentations improves representativeness and external validity and helps the algorithm generalize across clinical spectra. However, the question asks which design feature most directly supports validity of causal inference in a retrospective, outcome-selected study; multi-site inclusion aids generalizability and may reduce some selection concerns, but it is less directly tied to causal validity than features that ensure temporality and objective, pre-outcome measurement of predictors.

176.Option: Use of temporally prior, objective biomarkers collected within 72 hours of illness onset in a multi-site cohort with validation across subgroups
Evidence: Adult patients (age >18 years) presenting at selected primary care polyclinics within 72 hours of onset of acute febrile illness and without rhinitis or clinically obvious alternative diagnoses for fever were eligible for study inclusion.
Venous blood was also collected for haematological, virological and serological analyses at every visit.

The best performing decision algorithm made used of platelet count, the crossover value (Ct) of the real-time RT-PCR for dengue viral RNA ... and the presence of anti-dengue IgG antibodies, as the first, second and third splitting parameters, respectively (Figure 3). All 3 parameters were obtained from the first visit.

Since the course of disease in all but three of the Singaporean adult cases were consistent with DF instead of DHF, we have included 188 DHF/DSS paediatric and adult cases from Vietnam in order to ensure that the diagnostic algorithm developed here is robust across a spectrum of dengue presentations.
176.Rationale: Causal inference in retrospective outcome-selected designs is most threatened by reverse causation and exposure misclassification. Collecting objective biomarkers and clinical measurements that were recorded prior to or very early in the disease course (within 72 hours/at the first visit) preserves temporality (predictors measured before severe outcome), reduces recall and measurement bias, and strengthens the plausibility of causal interpretation. Multi-site sampling and subgroup validation support robustness across populations. The passage explicitly emphasizes early (first-visit/within-72-hours) collection of objective laboratory markers (platelet count, RT-PCR Ct, IgG) and multi-site data, which aligns with the design feature that most directly supports validity of causal claims.

176.Option: Development of a decision tree using data from hospitalized patients only to ensure accurate outcome ascertainment
Evidence: The use of data obtained from the 89 hospitalised cases alone resulted in a very similar decision algorithm, although the AUCs were substantially lower than the above analysis due largely to the smaller dataset.

Since the incidence of DHF is low in Singapore [20] and hospitalisation of the dengue cases is subject to variation arising from physician-to-physician differences in decision-making, we have used platelet count nadir of 50,000/mm3 or less at 5 to 7 days after onset of illness as an objective end-point for our analysis.
176.Rationale: Restricting the analysis to hospitalized patients improves certainty of outcome measurement but can introduce selection bias (hospitalization depends on care-seeking and clinician decisions) and reduce sample size and generalizability. The passage notes that using hospitalized-only data reduced AUCs because of smaller sample size and that hospitalisation is subject to variation in physician decision-making, arguing against hospitalized-only sampling as the best design choice to support causal inference in this retrospective outcome-selected context.

177.Option: Use mosquito surveillance as an adjunct to human testing to detect current infection and validate findings from human-based surveillance
Evidence: By contrast, mosquito surveillance gives a snapshot indication of current infection, and could serve as a useful adjunct to human surveillance methods[27, 47].

There were also examples where human testing did not detect significant transmission but xenomonitoring did. For example, the study by Ramaiah et al. reported a mosquito infection rate of 4.7% of mosquitoes when a community survey performed concurrently found no evidence of human infection on ICT testing.
Xenomonitoring surveys also appear to have utility in identifying hotspots, as in the case of Rao et al. (2018) who detected a MIR of 5.2% in an area which had recently passed TAS[38].
177.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that mosquito surveillance (xenomonitoring) provides a snapshot of current infection and can act as a useful adjunct to human surveillance. It also gives concrete examples where mosquito testing detected infection when human testing did not, demonstrating that combining mosquito surveillance with human testing reduces the chance of missing residual transmission hotspots due to measurement error in human-based data alone.

177.Option: Replace antigen-based testing with antibody-based testing in all surveillance activities to increase sensitivity and detect historical infections
Evidence: Compared to Binax Now or Alere ICT (the most commonly used tests at the time of most of these surveys) as the index test, Table 3 shows that antibody tests produce a higher proportion of positive results. Bm14Ab and Wb123Ab values are, on average, 5.1 and 6.7 times higher respectively than the corresponding BinaxNOW values, based on the median value of this ratio across the selected studies.

Antibody tests are a measure of the host response to infection which can persist for some time after all antigenic material from the original infection has been eliminated. This means that antibody tests are associated with an increased false-positive rate and the detection of more historical cases, meaning there would be financial and logistical implications to switching to widespread antibody testing[13].

All methods of human surveillance are affected by the persistence of the marker (antibody or antigen) in circulation. This is of variable duration for different test types, meaning that their results are not directly comparable. It also means that results are not truly indicative of current infectivity and will therefore include cases of historical disease.
177.Rationale: While antibody tests increase the proportion of positive results (greater sensitivity), the passage warns they persist after infection and lead to increased false positives and detection of historical infections. Replacing antigen-based testing with antibody testing across all surveillance would therefore reduce the risk of false negatives but would increase false positives and misclassify historical infections as current transmission, which does not effectively reduce the risk of missing true residual hotspots and could introduce other measurement errors and programmatic problems.

177.Option: Conduct repeated Transmission Assessment Surveys (TAS) at shorter intervals to improve detection of transmission hotspots despite low sensitivity
Evidence: It is acknowledged that the TAS methodology is resource-intensive and may also lack sensitivity in low-prevalence settings[5, 7].

This review has identified that the sensitivity of LF surveillance in selected low prevalence populations can be increased by changes to the diagnostic test and/or study population. TAS is an important programmatic tool to guide decisions on when to stop MDA but several studies report that it lacks sensitivity when used in low prevalence settings, such as a post-validation context[13, 17, 23, 38], and may not accurately describe the spatial distribution of LF at community-level[14].
The WHO recommend integrating post-MDA surveillance strategies with other ongoing surveillance activities[4]. Only three papers reported on efforts to integrate LF surveillance with other activities.
177.Rationale: The passage notes that TAS is resource-intensive and may lack sensitivity in low-prevalence settings; repeating TAS more frequently would increase resource use without addressing its intrinsic sensitivity limitations or its limited spatial resolution. The review instead highlights alternative diagnostic tests, targeted sampling, and integration with other surveillance (including mosquito surveillance) as more promising approaches. Therefore repeated TAS at shorter intervals is unlikely to be the most effective strategy to reduce the risk of missing residual hotspots due to measurement error.

177.Option: Use a single diagnostic test with the highest reported sensitivity across all studies to standardize surveillance and minimize measurement error
Evidence: This included variation in study design, baseline endemicity, population sampled, use of diagnostic tests and reporting metrics. It was agreed that this variation precluded formal meta-analysis and instead required a narrative review structured according to the core themes identified.

Compared to Binax Now or Alere ICT (the most commonly used tests at the time of most of these surveys) as the index test, Table 3 shows that antibody tests produce a higher proportion of positive results.

Antibody tests are a measure of the host response to infection which can persist for some time after all antigenic material from the original infection has been eliminated. This means that antibody tests are associated with an increased false-positive rate and the detection of more historical cases, meaning there would be financial and logistical implications to switching to widespread antibody testing[13].

All methods of human surveillance are affected by the persistence of the marker (antibody or antigen) in circulation. This is of variable duration for different test types, meaning that their results are not directly comparable.
177.Rationale: Although standardization is desirable, the passage emphasizes heterogeneity in tests and that the most sensitive test (e.g., some antibody tests) may detect historical, not current, infection and increase false positives. Selecting a single highest-sensitivity test without considering specificity, marker persistence, and epidemiologic context could introduce measurement bias and misclassification. The review instead recommends choosing diagnostic strategies appropriate to objectives (e.g., detecting ongoing transmission vs historical exposure) and combining methods, rather than a blanket single-test approach.

177.Option: Target surveillance exclusively to adult males, as they are more likely to harbor infection and show higher prevalence rates
Evidence: Studies reported that LF tests typically report higher prevalence of infection in adults than in children[14, 31] and it is thought that adults (particularly adult men) may represent the majority of the reservoir of infection for LF[37].
As prevalence reduces it may therefore also be appropriate to target surveillance to focus on these high-risk populations.
Post-validation surveillance in Togo found positive cases in low-risk areas, highlighting the importance of developing surveillance systems with nationwide coverage[40, 42].
177.Rationale: While adults, especially men, may disproportionately contribute to the reservoir and targeting them can improve sensitivity, the passage cautions that post-validation surveillance has found positive cases in low-risk areas and stresses the need for nationwide coverage. Exclusively targeting adult males risks missing hotspots in other groups or locations; thus exclusive targeting would increase the chance of missing residual transmission rather than most effectively reducing that risk.

178.Option: The inclusion of baseline microfilaria prevalence data in the model calibration process
Evidence: The available mf prevalence data from each site were arranged into four different temporal data streams to imitate the current WHO guidelines ... scenario 1—baseline mf prevalence data only

data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2)

Note that this was also irrespective of the two types of longitudinal data assimilation procedures (sequential vs. simultaneous) used by the different models in this study. Thus, for all models and sites, model-only predictions made in the absence of data (scenario 0) showed the highest prediction uncertainty, highlighting the need for data to improve the predictive performance of the present models.
178.Rationale: Although baseline mf prevalence (scenario 1) is one component of calibration, the passage explicitly states that baseline-only data were not the most informative. The authors report that data streams that include post-MDA 5 survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty most, indicating baseline data alone are insufficient to make forecasts reliably. The quoted sentences show that baseline-only (scenario 1) provided less reduction in uncertainty than scenarios including post-MDA 5.

178.Option: The use of a sequential data assimilation method rather than a batch estimation approach in model calibration
Evidence: EPIFIL used a sequential model updating procedure to iteratively modify the parameters with the introduction of each subsequent follow up data point ... LYMFASIM and TRANSFIL, by contrast, included all the data in each investigated stream together for selecting the best-fitting models for each time series–i.e. model selection for each data series was based on using all relevant observations simultaneously in the fitting process
Note that this was also irrespective of the two types of longitudinal data assimilation procedures (sequential vs. simultaneous) used by the different models in this study.
178.Rationale: The passage describes both sequential (EPIFIL) and batch/simultaneous (LYMFASIM, TRANSFIL) approaches but emphasizes that the key findings (reduced uncertainty when models are constrained by appropriate data streams) held irrespective of which assimilation strategy was used. Therefore the choice between sequential vs batch is not presented as the most critical determinant of forecast reliability; instead, the availability of the informative data streams (e.g., post-MDA5) drives the reliability.

178.Option: The use of a deterministic model structure over a stochastic one in simulating intervention outcomes
Evidence: The three existing LF models employed for this study included EPIFIL, a deterministic Monte Carlo population-based model, and LYMFASIM and TRANSFIL, which are both stochastic, individual-based models.
This gain in precision, and thus the information gained using these data streams, is, as expected, greater for the stochastic LYMFASIM and TRANSFIL models compared to the deterministic EPIFIL model.
