203.Rationale: This option closely matches the authors' interpretation. The experimental data show an early hypercoagulable signal (increased thrombin generation) followed by different later patterns: B. atrox shows delayed hypocoagulability (consumption, thrombocytopenia, reduced ROTEM MCF) consistent with bleeding, while B. lanceolatus shows fibrinogen recovery, increased fibrin generation and an inflammatory response that can promote hypercoagulability and thrombosis. The passage explicitly frames these as two possible trajectories following an early coagulopathic phase.

203.Option: The lack of change in platelet count with B. lanceolatus venom indicates that thrombocytopenia is not a factor in the pathogenesis of thrombosis in this envenomation.
Evidence: Significant thrombocytopenia was observed with B. atrox venom at H3 (p = 0.04), H6 (p = 0.02), and H24 (p < 0.001) while B. lanceolatus venom had no effect on platelet count (Fig 2C).

A non-significant trend to MCF increase at H24 and a slight hypercoagulable profile were observed with B. lanceolatus venom in our rat model, nevertheless of little contribution to explain the mechanisms of thrombosis in this envenomation.
we observed a systemic inflammatory response involving IL-6, TNF-α, and MCP-1 after B. lanceolatus venom injection.
Venom-induced thrombotic complications could be explained, at least in part, by quantitative changes in fibrinogen.
203.Rationale: The experimental results show no platelet count decrease with B. lanceolatus venom while thrombocytopenia is a clear feature of B. atrox envenomation linked to bleeding. The authors therefore emphasize alternate mechanisms for B. lanceolatus-associated thrombosis (inflammation, fibrinogen changes, fibrin generation, PAI-1 elevation) rather than platelet depletion. Thus, the absence of thrombocytopenia in the B. lanceolatus model supports the interpretation that thrombocytopenia is not a key driver of thrombosis in that setting.

203.Option: The recovery of fibrinogen at 24 hours with B. lanceolatus venom is sufficient to explain the onset of thrombosis, regardless of inflammatory or coagulation factors.
Evidence: Plasma fibrinogen was significantly lowered with the two venoms at H3 (p = 0.009 for B. atrox and p = 0.005 for B. lanceolatus) and at H6 (p = 0.03 for B. atrox and B. lanceolatus). At the opposite, plasma fibrinogen was significantly increased at H24 with B. lanceolatus venom (p < 0.001; Fig 2D).

Venom-induced thrombotic complications could be explained, at least in part, by quantitative changes in fibrinogen. An increase in plasma fibrinogen at H24 was remarkable in our B. lanceolatus envenomation model.
The increase in PAI-1 at H6 and H24 suggests an inhibition of the fibrinolytic pathway but ROTEM analysis may not show fibrinolysis shutdown.
we observed a systemic inflammatory response involving IL-6, TNF-α, and MCP-1 after B. lanceolatus venom injection.

This increase in PAI-1 ... suggests an inhibition of the fibrinolytic pathway ... The procoagulant effect of B. atrox venom depends on various factors, notably the geographical origin of the specimens collected [58].
203.Rationale: Although fibrinogen recovery/hyperfibrinogenemia at H24 is highlighted as a contributor to thrombotic risk, the authors explicitly point to multiple cooperating mechanisms (inflammation with IL-6/TNF-α/MCP-1, increased PAI-1 and decreased fibrinolysis, increased fibrin generation) that together explain thrombosis. The passage does not claim fibrinogen recovery alone is sufficient; it frames fibrinogen changes as one component of an inflammation- and coagulation-driven process.

203.Option: The similar proteomic composition of B. atrox and B. lanceolatus venoms explains why both species cause early coagulopathy, but differences in venom dose account for the divergent clinical outcomes.
Evidence: Surprisingly, venom composition of these two Bothrops species are relatively similar with comparable activities on coagulation in vitro in the whole blood and plasma [48].
TGA showed a decrease in lag-time and time to peak and an increase in thrombin peak at H3 with the two venoms while ETP was only increased with B. atrox venom.

Since preliminary in vitro experiments conducted in our laboratory using ROTEM in the rat whole blood showed that the procoagulant effect of B. atrox venom was 2-fold that of B. lanceolatus venom, we used 220% of the estimated intraperitoneal LD50 in mice for B. lanceolatus venom (i.e., 4 mg per rat), and half of this value for B. atrox venom (i.e., 2 mg per rat) to target equipotent doses.

Our global investigation of hemostasis suggest that mechanisms involved in Bothrops envenomation-induced bleeding and thrombosis are not distinct but represent the two sides of the same coin ... An initial step of venom-induced hypercoagulability ... followed by a secondary step of hypocoagulability with B. atrox venom ... or persistent hypercoagulability with B. lanceolatus venom ...

203.Rationale: While the two venoms have similar proteomic profiles and both produce early procoagulant signals in vitro, the authors do not attribute divergent clinical outcomes primarily to dose differences. In the experiment they adjusted doses to achieve approximate equipotency, and the text emphasizes dynamic time-course effects, species-specific enzymatic activities, inflammatory responses, and recovery kinetics (e.g., fibrinogen restoration, PAI-1 changes) as explanations for different clinical trajectories. Thus, attributing divergence mainly to dose is not supported by the passage.

204.Option: The number of microfilariae present in the specific blood spot sample used for DNA extraction
Evidence: Three false negative results were obtained with blood spot samples where mf burden was very low (20–200 mf/mL, or up to 4 organisms in a 20 µL blood spot).

204.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes some false negatives to sampling error (i.e., no organisms present in the processed blood spot) and documents that very low numbers of organisms in the aliquot (few organisms in a 20 µL spot) led to negative results. Thus, the actual number of microfilariae in the specific filter-paper blood spot being processed is a critical determinant of parasitological accuracy when using minimally invasive dried blood samples.

204.Option: The efficiency and consistency of DNA extraction methods used on dried blood spots
Evidence: Microfilaremia estimation was most accurate and most consistent with samples subjected to easyMAG extraction. This automated extraction process will therefore be preferred in laboratory settings where such equipment is available.
These false negatives were most likely due either to sampling error (no organisms present in the processed blood spot) or to issues with the DNA extraction process.

204.Rationale: The study reports variation in quantitative estimates and false negatives that are attributable to differences in DNA extraction. 204.Option: The presence of cross-reactive filarial antigens in the blood sample that interfere with PCR amplification

204.Rationale: While the passage notes serologic cross-reactivity affecting antibody assays, it explicitly states that the qPCR targets are species-specific due to primer/probe sequence design and that the assays do not detect DNA from other filariae. There is no indication that cross-reactive antigens in blood samples interfere with PCR amplification; thus this option is not supported.

204.Option: The timing of blood collection relative to the peak microfilaremia period in the daily cycle
Evidence: Similar to microscopy, optimum PCR performance requires proper timing of blood collection at mid-day when microfilaremia is greatest; however, a microfilaremia kinetics study by Kamgno et al. suggests that smaller numbers of organisms may be detectable outside the window of peak microfilaremia [23], and a nomogram may be used to extrapolate qPCR results to predict true parasite burden.

204.Rationale: The authors explicitly state that optimal PCR performance requires mid-day collection because microfilaremia is diurnally periodic and peaks at mid-day. Evidence: These extrapolated values were adjusted by the ratio of the percentage of blood spot material used as template (1–10%) compared to the percentage of spiked blood sample used as template (1%) to estimate the number of mf present in each blood spot. 205.Option: The significant association between flower and/or fruit presence and Ae. albopictus abundance in village habitats suggests that general floral availability is the main factor influencing vector distribution

206.Option: Higher frequency of household-level clustering of infections, indicating shared environmental exposure and increased transmission risk
Evidence: HCC is considered a “clustered” disease; some studies have pointed out the importance of the presence of tapeworm carriers in a household triggering HCC in endemic and non-endemic communities[82;83].

206.Rationale: Household clustering (presence of tapeworm carriers in a household) indicates increased local exposure/transmission rather than increased host susceptibility. The question asks which factor indicates heightened risk for developing active infection despite similar exposure levels; clustering reflects differential exposure (shared environmental contamination), not that a subgroup is more likely to progress from exposure to active infection. The cited text describes clustering as a source of transmission (increased exposure), so this option does not match the 'despite similar exposure' condition.

Considering that the time required for seroconversion from recent infections is at least seven days, all selected samples from the patients had more than seven days of symptoms.

It is essential to perform studies involving molecular detection of C. burnetii in the early phase of the disease (10 days or less of symptoms), which would characterize an active infection before seroconversion

208.Option: The development of a mixed Th1/Th2/Th17 immune response in the cecum at 35 d.p.i.

Immunological signals resulting from infection start and finish in the cecum. Initially, there is a predominance of Th1/Th2 cytokines in the first days of the acute phase, which then transitions to the chronic phase characterized by a mixture of Th1/Th2/Th17 responses.
The damage related to the infection, including bacterial translocation, impacted the mesenteric lymph nodes, starting at 17 d.p.i., and expanded to the spleen and intraperitoneal cells [63].
208.Rationale: While a mixed Th1/Th2/Th17 profile in the cecum is a marker of chronic local immune modulation, the question asks which host factor most strongly correlates with progression to chronic infection and systemic immune activation. The mixed cecal response describes local tissue immune status at chronic timepoints (35 d.p.i.) but is not the clearest correlate of systemic immune activation. The passage links systemic activation to spread beyond the cecum (mesenteric nodes, spleen, intraperitoneal cells) and to peritoneal macrophage and peripheral blood changes rather than to the local mixed cytokine profile alone.

208.Option: The increase in spleen weight and size observed at 22 d.p.i. and 35 d.p.i.
Evidence: At the end of the acute phase and during the chronic phase, on the 22nd day, we observed a greater spleen weight in the infected group than in the noninfected group. Additionally, at 22 and 35 days after infection, the spleens of infected mice were longer than those of noninfected mice.
Morphometric analysis of the white pulp revealed a larger area in infected mice at 17, 22, and 35 days of infection.
These changes suggest that the lesions derived from the infection and bacterial translocation activated the lymphoid tissues of the spleen following sensitization of the mesenteric lymph nodes.
208.Rationale: Spleen enlargement is a clear indicator of systemic immune activation and is temporally associated with progression (noted at 22 and 35 d.p.i.). However, it is a downstream organ-level consequence rather than the host factor that most strongly correlates with the transition mechanism itself. The passage describes spleen changes as resulting from earlier events (e.g., bacterial translocation, mesenteric node sensitization, activated peritoneal macrophages and blood leukocyte changes). Thus spleen enlargement is an important marker of systemic activation but not the proximate host factor most strongly correlating with progression.

208.Option: A sustained pro-inflammatory and regulatory cytokine profile in peritoneal macrophages coupled with neutrophilia and lymphopenia in peripheral blood
Evidence: At 17 d.p.i., there was an increase in IL-6 and TNF production in peritoneal macrophages, which persisted until 22 d.p.i., along with an increase in IL-10 levels.
In the analysis of cytokines produced by peritoneal macrophages, we observed increases in TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-10 after 22 days (Fig 10).
Additionally, there was an increase in nitric oxide produced by these macrophages on the 35th day of infection when stimulated by LPS, indicating their activation (Fig 11).
we observed alterations in blood cells during the acute phase of infection, with a decrease in the number of circulating monocytes and an increase in the number of eosinophils at 10 d.p.i..

By 22 d.p.i., there was a significant decrease in the number of lymphocytes and an increase in the number of basophils and neutrophils in the blood, indicating a response against L4 larvae and secondary infection by invasive bacteria.
The damage related to the infection, including bacterial translocation, impacted the mesenteric lymph nodes, starting at 17 d.p.i., and expanded to the spleen and intraperitoneal cells [63].
208.Rationale: The passage directly links progression of infection (bacterial translocation beginning ~17 d.p.i. and worsening at 22 d.p.i.) with activation of peritoneal macrophages (increased IL-6, TNF, IL-10, and NO) and with peripheral blood changes including neutrophilia and lymphopenia. These macrophage cytokine changes and leukocyte shifts are described as occurring during the acute-to-chronic transition and are implicated in systemic immune activation and downstream splenic changes. Therefore, a sustained mixed pro‑inflammatory and regulatory macrophage cytokine profile together with blood leukocyte alterations is the host factor most strongly correlated with progression and systemic activation.

208.Option: The presence of eosinophils in the intestinal mucosa and submucosa throughout the acute phase of infection
Evidence: This increase in eosinophils was maintained at other time points measured during infection evolution (17th, 22nd, and 35th days) (S1 Table).
In infections with low parasitic loads, we observed an increase in eosinophils in the bloodstream from 10 d.p.i. onward.

Our results revealed eosinophilia in the mucosa and submucosa of the infected intestine at all time points of infection (10, 17, 22 and 35 d.p.i.), indicating that this infection promotes the recruitment of eosinophils to the parasite niche even at the L2 larval stage.
208.Rationale: Eosinophil recruitment is a consistent and early local feature of helminth infection in this model, but the passage frames eosinophilia as a local anti-helminthic/inflammatory response rather than the primary correlate of progression to chronic infection or systemic immune activation. Systemic activation is more clearly associated with peritoneal macrophage cytokine changes, leukocyte shifts (neutrophilia/lymphopenia), and splenic involvement than with mucosal eosinophilia alone.

208.Option: The increase in goblet cell numbers in the intestinal mucosa beginning at 17 d.p.i.
Evidence: we observed an increase in enterocytes at 10 d.p.i. and goblet cells at 17 d.p.i., highlighting the role of enterocytes in the intestine’s absorption mechanism [51] and goblet cells in mucin secretion, which act as a chemical and mechanical barrier against intestinal pathogens [7,52].
we observed a significant increase in the number of goblet cells and enterocytes (pink) in the mucosa (Mu), submucosa (Sm) and muscular layer (M).
208.Rationale: Goblet cell hyperplasia (starting around 17 d.p.i.) is an important mucosal defense and a marker of local tissue remodeling, but it reflects local epithelial responses (mucin secretion and barrier functions) rather than systemic immune activation. The passage links systemic activation to bacterial translocation, peritoneal macrophage activation, leukocyte changes, and splenic responses, making goblet cell increase a less direct correlate of progression to chronic systemic involvement.

209.Option: The bacterium's ability to be genetically modified and produce effector molecules that inhibit Leishmania development
Evidence: Paratransgenesis has been suggested as a feasible strategy for controlling the transmission of pathogens by arthropod vectors. This approach consists of the use of genetically altered symbiotic bacteria that secrete effector molecules that kill the infectious agents.
To that end, a strain of the non-pathogenic Bacillus species (Bacillus subtilis), isolated from P. papatasi, is proposed as a possible candidate for paratransgenic approach.

even if this bacterium possesses the main advantages of being non-pathogenic, easy to cultivate and to perform genetic manipulation, its use for paratransgenic control of Leishmania can be challenged by its capacity to establish long-term colonies in the gut of various sand fly species.
209.Rationale: The passage defines paratransgenesis as using "genetically altered symbiotic bacteria that secrete effector molecules that kill the infectious agents," making the ability to be genetically modified and to express effective anti-Leishmania molecules an essential requirement for any candidate. The authors explicitly note B. subtilis as attractive because it is amenable to genetic manipulation, supporting that modifiability and effector production are necessary selection criteria.

209.Option: The bacterium's non-pathogenicity and ease of cultivation in laboratory conditions
Evidence: To that end, a strain of the non-pathogenic Bacillus species (Bacillus subtilis), isolated from P. papatasi, is proposed as a possible candidate for paratransgenic approach.

even if this bacterium possesses the main advantages of being non-pathogenic, easy to cultivate and to perform genetic manipulation, its use for paratransgenic control of Leishmania can be challenged by its capacity to establish long-term colonies in the gut of various sand fly species.
209.Rationale: Non-pathogenicity and ease of cultivation are clearly advantageous and mentioned as 'main advantages' for candidate selection. However, the passage frames these as benefits rather than the single most critical factor: the authors immediately caution that even with these advantages, the candidate's capacity to establish and persist in the sand fly gut may challenge its utility. Therefore, while important, non-pathogenicity and cultivability alone are not the most critical deciding factor according to the text.

209.Option: The ability of the candidate bacterium to establish and persist in the midgut of the target sand fly species over time
Evidence: Since these bacteria should co-localize with the pathogen and be transmitted vertically to the next generation, they are introduced into vectors to block pathogen transmission [19–20].
its use for paratransgenic control of Leishmania can be challenged by its capacity to establish long-term colonies in the gut of various sand fly species.

In particular, if a paratransgenic approach is developed using B. subtilis as a host, it will be essential to probe its capacity to efficiently colonize the gut of Lutzomyia species and of other Old World sand fly species in which this bacterium has yet not been found in the gut.
209.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that paratransgenic bacteria must co-localize with the pathogen and be transmitted to subsequent generations, implying the need for stable colonization. It explicitly states that the main challenge for B. subtilis (despite other advantages) is its capacity to 'establish long-term colonies in the gut' and that testing colonization/persistence across target vector species is 'essential.' This makes persistence in the midgut the critical selection factor.

209.Option: The frequency of the candidate bacterium in the midgut of field-collected sand flies compared to laboratory-reared ones
Evidence: Among possible bacterial species that could be considered as candidates for the development of a paratransgenic approach, Bacillus pumilus and Bacillus flexus were identified as the most frequent cultivable bacteria identified in the midgut of P. papatasi field-collected from Tunisia, Turkey, and India [21].
Our results suggest that lab-reared P. perniciosus display a lower bacterial richness in their midgut than in field-collected sand flies.

Therefore, the nature of the feeding regimen leads to a striking contrast between field-collected and laboratory-reared sand flies, which might explain the lower bacterial richness observed in colonized sand flies.
209.Rationale: Frequency in field versus lab flies is informative — the authors note certain bacteria are 'most frequent' in field-collected midguts and that lab-reared flies have lower richness. However, frequency alone does not guarantee suitability for paratransgenesis; the text stresses colonization/persistence, co-localization with pathogen, vertical transmission, and genetic manipulability as critical properties. Thus relative frequency is useful data but not identified as the most critical selection factor.

209.Option: The presence of the candidate bacterium in the midgut of multiple sand fly species across different geographic regions
Evidence: Bacteria belonging to the Bacillus genus seem to display a host-specific distribution, with only B. subtilis being isolated in more than one sand fly species (P. halepensis, P. papatasi, and P. perniciosus).

In particular, if a paratransgenic approach is developed using B. subtilis as a host, it will be essential to probe its capacity to efficiently colonize the gut of Lutzomyia species and of other Old World sand fly species in which this bacterium has yet not been found in the gut. Thus, it will be of major epidemiological importance to develop a regional strategy for each endemic area with different bacterial isolates.
209.Rationale: Broad geographic and host presence is advantageous because it may allow a single candidate to be used across multiple vectors and regions. However, the passage highlights host-specific distributions and cautions that even B. subtilis is not universal; the authors recommend regional strategies and emphasize colonization ability in each target species. Therefore, cross-species presence is desirable but not the single most critical factor compared with persistent colonization and functional suitability.

210.Option: The presence of riparian forests along the Mouhoun River, which serve as refugia for tsetse populations and facilitate local re-colonization
Evidence: These species inhabit the riparian forests that form habitat galleries along the rivers and the flies' relative abundance is determined by forest ecotype and its level of fragmentation and destruction [2], [5].
riverine tsetse populations are mainly confined to the riverbeds of the various river systems which are organised in river basins
210.Rationale: While the passage clearly states that riparian forests are the primary habitat for riverine tsetse and therefore can act as local refugia and support local re-colonization (evidence above), the study emphasizes that the dominant factor determining re-invasion risk after eradication is connectivity and gene flow between basins across the savannah, not only local refugia. The authors conclude that populations in adjacent basins "are exchanging genetic material" and recommend buffer zones or extending campaigns to neighbouring basins to prevent reinvasion, indicating that cross-basin dispersal (connectivity) is the stronger determinant of re-invasion risk than the mere presence of riparian forest refugia.

210.Option: The use of biconical traps in multiple river basins, which may have introduced tsetse flies from adjacent basins into the Mouhoun River Basin during sampling
Evidence: From November 2007 to March 2008 each site was sampled using 5–10 unbaited biconical traps [29].

The population genetics data presented here suggest that the savannah area of the watershed divide between two adjacent river basins does not seem to represent a significant barrier to gene flow for the two riverine tsetse species studied.
riverine tsetse populations from adjacent river basins are exchanging genetic material, and can therefore not be considered as biologically isolated.
210.Rationale: There is no statement in the passage suggesting that sampling with biconical traps introduced tsetse flies or caused movement between basins. The traps are described only as a sampling method. The passage attributes observed genetic connectivity and the risk of re-invasion to natural dispersal and gene flow across the landscape (quotes above), not to artifacts of sampling. Therefore this option is not supported by the text.

210.Option: Gene flow across watershed divides due to dispersal through fragmented savannah habitats
Evidence: The population genetics data presented here suggest that the savannah area of the watershed divide between two adjacent river basins does not seem to represent a significant barrier to gene flow for the two riverine tsetse species studied.
riverine tsetse populations from adjacent river basins are exchanging genetic material, and can therefore not be considered as biologically isolated.

During the rainy season, riverine tsetse fly species disperse in the savannah areas neighbouring the river [7], probably in search of suitable hosts, like cattle, that during that time of the year do not have to enter the riparian forests to find drinking water.

It is conceivable that after some days without rain, remaining flies in the savannah areas are quickly forced to find resting sites before facing desiccation and are therefore stimulated to disperse at a higher rate. Following environmental cues such as humidity or temperature gradients, these flies will need to venture back to the closest gallery forest, that might well belong to another river basin system.
210.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes the lack of biological isolation and the risk of re-invasion to gene flow across watershed divides via dispersal through savannah areas. The authors state that the watershed savannah "does not seem to represent a significant barrier to gene flow" and that populations "are exchanging genetic material," and they describe behavioural/ecological mechanisms (seasonal savannah dispersal driven by host-seeking and environmental cues) that facilitate crossing between basins. The authors consequently recommend buffer zones or expanding eradication to adjacent basins to prevent reinvasion, directly tying re-invasion risk to cross-basin dispersal and gene flow.

210.Option: The higher effective population density of G. p. gambiensis compared to G. tachinoides, which increases the probability of successful re-invasion from neighboring basins
Evidence: For G. tachinoides ... resulted in an effective population density of De = Ne/S ≈1000 flies per km2.

For G. p. gambiensis densities were computed ... leads to an effective density of G. p. gambiensis De = Ne/S ≈8000 (for Ne = 80) or De = 15000 (for Ne = 150) G. p. gambiensis per km2 in the study area.

The analysis presented here showed that dispersal of G. tachinoides across river basins was ∼3 times higher than G. p. gambiensis, which suggests that G. tachinoides flies have the ability to disperse with ease despite the severe fragmentation of the riparian gallery forests in the study area.
210.Rationale: Although G. p. gambiensis has a higher effective density in sampled areas (evidence above), the passage indicates that cross-basin dispersal (and thus re-invasion potential) is greater for G. tachinoides (dispersal ∼3 times higher). The paper emphasizes connectivity and dispersal across watershed savannah as the critical determinant of re-invasion and concludes that adjacent basins exchange genetic material; it does not claim that higher local density of G. p. gambiensis is the dominant factor driving re-invasion. Therefore this option overstates the role of density relative to dispersal-driven gene flow.

210.Option: The genetic structure of G. tachinoides populations, which shows a higher degree of local inbreeding and reduced gene flow compared to G. p. gambiensis
Evidence: G. tachinoides occurs as a panmictic population along its riverine habitat in the same area, due to its more xerophylous nature allowing it to disperse more easily between suitable habitat patches [23]–[25].

Defining the subpopulation units: HierFstat analysis only found one significant hierarchical level of population structure in the G. tachinoides samples, i.e. subdivision by sites FSite/Basin = 0.026 (P-value = 0.001). Traps (P-value = 0.179) and river basin (P-value = 0.707) did not significantly contribute to the genetic structure of G. tachinoides.

The analysis presented here showed that dispersal of G. tachinoides across river basins was ∼3 times higher than G. p. gambiensis, which suggests that G. tachinoides flies have the ability to disperse with ease despite the severe fragmentation of the riparian gallery forests in the study area.
the higher level of genetic structuring observed for G. p. gambiensis populations at the micro-scale [22] corroborate these observations.
210.Rationale: This option asserts that G. tachinoides has higher local inbreeding and reduced gene flow compared to G. p. gambiensis, but the passage states the opposite: G. tachinoides is described as more panmictic with greater dispersal and less structuring by basin, whereas G. p. gambiensis shows higher micro-scale genetic structuring. The evidence therefore contradicts the option's premise, making it incorrect.

211.Option: Declining seroprevalence in young children signals a growing pool of susceptible individuals, which precedes and predicts the onset of cyclical EV-A71 epidemics
Evidence: We hypothesized that falls in EV-A71 seroprevalence rates were associated with new epidemics.
This shows that every 10% fall in monthly seroprevalence compared to the preceding month is associated with 16% higher odds of an epidemic.
younger children aged 1–6 years old had lower seroprevalence in non-epidemic years, indicating greater susceptibility, which may explain the higher HFMD incidence in this age group (Fig 2).
falls in seroprevalence in children aged 1–12 years old are the major driving force of the cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics seen in Malaysia over 18 years.
211.Rationale: The passage explicitly hypothesizes and demonstrates that decreases in seroprevalence (especially among young children) are temporally associated with increased odds of an epidemic. Lower seroprevalence indicates more susceptibles; the authors show a quantified association (10% fall → 16% higher odds), supporting this option.

211.Option: The observed increase in seroprevalence during epidemic years reflects a direct effect of the virus on immune system activation, rather than prior exposure or infection
Evidence: The higher seropositive rates and GMT levels seen during epidemic years are likely to reflect recent infection.
These results showed that EV-A71 seroprevalence spiking during reported EV-A71 epidemic years, and seroprevalence falling between epidemics (Fig 4A).
211.Rationale: The passage attributes higher seroprevalence in epidemic years to recent infection (i.e., prior exposure causing antibody rises), not to some abstract direct activation separate from exposure. Therefore the option's contrast ('rather than prior exposure or infection') contradicts the authors' interpretation.

211.Option: The cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics is best explained by seasonal climatic variation, as temperature and rain days were found to influence epidemic likelihood
Evidence: We found clear support for our hypothesis, showing that statistically significant decreases in population seroprevalence (as a proxy for immunity) are temporally associated with subsequent epidemics, after adjustment for age, temperature, rainfall, rain days, and ultraviolet radiance.
the overall likelihood of an epidemic was influenced by temperature and rain days, but not rainfall or ultraviolet radiance.
211.Rationale: While climatic factors (temperature and rain days) were associated with epidemic likelihood, the authors conclude that changes in population seroprevalence (immunity) are the major driving force of cyclical epidemics. Thus climate may contribute but does not 'best explain' the cyclical pattern as this option claims.

211.Option: The cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics is primarily driven by antigenic drift in the VP1 protein, as evidenced by the presence of positively selected codons at positions 98 and 145
Evidence: Evidence of strong negative selection and 2 codons with positive selection may explain the emergence of immune escape though the role in cyclical patterns of EV-A71 epidemics remains unclear.

While the increased diversity may simply reflect a larger number of infections, we cannot exclude that new variants with antigenic changes may escape population immunity and contribute to cyclical epidemics.
falls in seroprevalence in children aged 1–12 years old are the major driving force of the cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics seen in Malaysia over 18 years.
211.Rationale: The passage reports positively selected sites and genetic diversity, but explicitly states the role of antigenic change in cyclical patterns is unclear and emphasizes that declines in population immunity are the primary driving force. Therefore asserting antigenic drift as the primary driver is not supported.

211.Option: A sustained increase in seroprevalence over multiple years indicates that herd immunity is being maintained, reducing the likelihood of future epidemics
Evidence: This shows that every 10% fall in monthly seroprevalence compared to the preceding month is associated with 16% higher odds of an epidemic.
For such a transmissible virus, the epidemic size is mainly dependent on the size of the susceptible population. Following a viral epidemic, most of the population at risk would become immune.

As most children in Malaysia and other Asian countries [9,31] are seropositive by 5 years, an effective vaccine could prevent EV-A71 HFMD, as well as the severe associated neurological complications that mainly affect the very young [47].
211.Rationale: The passage links declines in seroprevalence to increased epidemic risk; conversely, maintained or increased seroprevalence implies fewer susceptibles and lower epidemic likelihood. The authors describe how post-epidemic immunity reduces susceptibility and imply that sustained population immunity (herd immunity) would reduce future epidemics.

212.Option: The absence of vascular leakage and paracellular barrier disruption allows for isolation of vaccine-induced immune effects from non-specific inflammatory responses
Evidence: In summary, we have established the ligated ileal loop model of V. cholerae-induced diarrhea in adult mice. Pathogenesis of diarrhea in this cholera model results from CFTR-mediated transepithelial Cl− secretion with no involvement of disrupted intestinal barrier function or vascular leakage.

To determine the contribution of vascular leakage to V. cholerae-induced intestinal fluid accumulation in this model, the amount of the plasma protein albumin was determined in fecal fluids of both control (no V. cholerae) and V. cholerae-inoculated mice. It was found that levels of albumin in fecal fluid of both control and V. cholerae-inoculated intestinal loops were under detection limit (<0.3 g/dL). This result indicated that vascular leakage did not contribute to the development of diarrhea in this experimental model of cholera.
212.Rationale: While the passage documents that vascular leakage and paracellular barrier disruption are not involved in this mouse model, that feature addresses mechanistic clarity of the intestinal disease phenotype rather than directly supporting use of a primate model to evaluate durability of vaccine protection over time. Durability of protection is most directly supported by the ability to follow mature, long-lived adaptive immune responses longitudinally; the absence of vascular leakage helps with interpretability of acute pathophysiology but does not itself enable long-term assessment of waning immunity in a primate model.

212.Option: The mature immune system in adult mice allows for longitudinal assessment of immune responses following vaccination and repeated challenge
Evidence: Importantly, due to the maturation of immune system in the adult mice, this cholera model could be used for evaluating preventive efficacy of cholera vaccine
212.Rationale: The key feature that most directly supports evaluating durability of protection over time is a mature, functional immune system that can generate and maintain adaptive immune responses and be monitored longitudinally. The passage explicitly states that maturation of the immune system in adult animals makes the model suitable for vaccine evaluation. Although the option refers to mice rather than primates, the same principle (mature immune system permitting longitudinal assessment of vaccine-induced immunity and waning over time) is the relevant attribute of a primate model with high similarity to humans.

212.Option: The use of outbred ICR mice ensures genetic diversity that mimics human population variation and supports long-term immune monitoring after vaccination
Evidence: Six-week-old ICR outbred mice (weight 30–35 g) were acquired from the National Laboratory Animal Center, Bangkok, Thailand.

Being small and with shorter lifespan, mice are relatively inexpensive, easy to handle and require smaller quantities of compounds/vaccines for therapeutic/preventive evaluation. Transgenic mice are also available for studying cholera pathophysiology.
212.Rationale: While outbred animals provide some genetic diversity, the passage does not claim that outbred ICR mice specifically 'mimic human population variation' in a way that directly supports durability studies in primates. Moreover, the question asks about a primate species with close similarity to humans; citing outbred mouse strain characteristics does not address that. Genetic diversity can be relevant to generalizability, but it is not the most direct feature for assessing durability of protection over time—having a mature, long-lived immune system and capacity for longitudinal follow-up is more directly relevant.

212.Option: The consistent induction of intestinal fluid accumulation within 12 hours post-challenge enables repeated challenge experiments to assess waning immunity over time
Evidence: intestinal fluid secretion increased over time and reached its peak at 12 h after V. cholerae inoculation (loop weight/length ratio = 0.203±0.037 g/cm). This level of intestinal fluid secretion was sustained until 18 h post-inoculation (loop weight/length ratio = 0.192±0.042 g/cm).

Diarrhea success rate, which indicated model reproducibility, and survival rate of mice were 90% and 80% at 12 h post-inoculation, and 68% and 50% at 18 h post-inoculation, respectively (Fig. 1B). Therefore, an optimal incubation period for induction of diarrhea by V. cholerae was 12 h.
212.Rationale: The model's consistent and rapid induction of intestinal fluid accumulation is valuable for studying acute pathophysiology and for assessing short-term efficacy after challenge, but a 12-hour acute readout is not the feature that most directly supports evaluating long-term durability of vaccine protection in a primate model. Durability requires ability to follow immune responses and protection over extended periods (months to years) and to perform longitudinal sampling; rapid, reproducible acute challenge is helpful for standardized efficacy assays but does not by itself enable or guarantee long-term assessment of waning immunity.

213.Option: The presence of helminth infection may reduce the sensitivity of T. cruzi PCR by suppressing the host immune response
Evidence: Helminth infection in humans may modulate the host immune system, changing the Th1/Th2 polarization. This immunological disturbance could modify the immune response to other infections or the antibody production after vaccination [15, 16].

Strongyloides spp infection in the murine model induces a Th2 response and regulatory cytokine induction (IL-10), leading to a suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines and diminishing Th1 response [26].
The percentage of patients with positive T. cruzi RT-PCR was higher in patients with helminth infection compared with those without helminth infection (75% versus 35.2%, p = 0.021).
Helminth infection could increase the probability of having a positive T. cruzi PCR.
213.Rationale: The passage indicates helminth infection modulates immunity toward Th2 and suppresses Th1/pro-inflammatory responses, and documents an association of helminth infection with a higher frequency of positive T. cruzi PCR (75% vs 35.2%). These data point toward increased PCR detectability (or increased parasitaemia) in co-infected patients rather than reduced PCR sensitivity. Therefore the statement that helminth infection reduces PCR sensitivity is not supported by the passage and is incorrect.

213.Option: The variability in T. cruzi PCR positivity across settings is primarily due to differences in helminth infection prevalence
Evidence: The percentage of positive T. cruzi PCR in peripheral blood in patients with Chagas disease in the chronic phase highly varies depending on the study: it ranges from 80% to 90% in studies performed in endemic countries, and is lower in non-endemic countries, ranging from 28% to 66% [5–10].
Helminth infection could increase the probability of having a positive T. cruzi PCR.

The geographical distribution of the different T. cruzi discrete typing units (DTUs) differs from country to country, which may have impact in the clinical presentation or in the proportion of patients with positive T. cruzi PCR in peripheral blood [29].
213.Rationale: While the passage reports that helminth infection could increase the probability of a positive T. cruzi PCR, it also documents wide variability in PCR positivity between endemic and non-endemic settings and explicitly notes other contributors (for example, geographic differences in T. cruzi discrete typing units). The passage therefore does not support the stronger claim that differences in helminth prevalence are the primary cause of PCR variability across settings; other factors are identified. Thus this option is incorrect.

213.Option: Helminth co-infection may increase PCR detectability of T. cruzi DNA without indicating higher parasite burden or disease progression
Evidence: The percentage of patients with positive T. cruzi RT-PCR was higher in patients with helminth infection compared with those without helminth infection (75% versus 35.2%, p = 0.021).
Helminth infection could increase the probability of having a positive T. cruzi PCR.

no differences in the parasitaemia were found between non co-infected and early co-infected mice (the T. cruzi infection took place 2–4 weeks after Taenia crassiceps infection), however, late co-infected mice (the T. cruzi infection took place 8–12 weeks after Taenia crassiceps infection, when a predominant Th2-type cytokine response is expected) showed significantly higher parasitaemia compared with non co-infected and early co-infected mice [25].
213.Rationale: The option asserts that increased PCR detectability in co-infected patients does not reflect higher parasite burden. However, the passage provides animal model evidence that late helminth co-infection was associated with significantly higher parasitaemia, and the authors interpret helminth co-infection as possibly increasing the probability of a positive PCR. Therefore the claim that increased detectability occurs without indicating higher parasite burden is not supported; the data suggest it may reflect higher parasitaemia. Accordingly this option is incorrect.

213.Option: Helminth co-infection should be treated before performing T. cruzi PCR to avoid false-positive results
Evidence: Helminth infection could increase the probability of having a positive T. cruzi PCR.
Further studies are needed to evaluate the impact of treating the helminth infection on the positivity of T. cruzi PCR.
213.Rationale: The passage raises the possibility that helminth infection affects PCR positivity and explicitly calls for further studies to evaluate the impact of treating helminth infection on T. cruzi PCR results. It does not recommend treating helminths before PCR nor does it describe PCR positives in co-infected patients as 'false-positive' results; rather, the concern is about interpretation (increased positivity) and potential effects on treatment-efficacy trials. Therefore the prescriptive statement that helminth infection should be treated before PCR to avoid false-positives is not supported by the passage and is incorrect.

213.Option: A positive T. cruzi PCR in co-infected patients may indicate a higher risk of treatment failure due to increased parasite load
Evidence: The percentage of patients with positive T. cruzi RT-PCR was higher in patients with helminth infection compared with those without helminth infection (75% versus 35.2%, p = 0.021).
Helminth infection could increase the probability of having a positive T. cruzi PCR.

no differences in the parasitaemia were found between non co-infected and early co-infected mice (the T. cruzi infection took place 2–4 weeks after Taenia crassiceps infection), however, late co-infected mice (the T. cruzi infection took place 8–12 weeks after Taenia crassiceps infection, when a predominant Th2-type cytokine response is expected) showed significantly higher parasitaemia compared with non co-infected and early co-infected mice [25].

T. cruzi PCR is not routinely performed in the management of chronic Chagas disease patients, but is becoming very useful in specific situations, such as follow-up in immunosuppressed patients in order to detect reactivation or in clinical trials to detect treatment failures [11–13].
Given that current clinical trials that evaluate treatment efficacy in Chagas disease are based on the positivity of T. cruzi PCR, this fact may be relevant.
213.Rationale: The passage documents an association between helminth co-infection and higher frequency of positive T. cruzi PCR, and animal data showing increased parasitaemia in co-infected hosts. PCR positivity is used in trials to detect treatment failures. Therefore, in co-infected patients a positive PCR could reflect higher parasite burden and could plausibly signal an increased likelihood of treatment failure or impact trial endpoints. The passage highlights this as a relevant consideration and calls for further study, supporting this option as a valid factor to consider when interpreting positive PCR results.

214.Option: The case-fatality ratio trend over time is best interpreted by comparing countries with similar outbreak histories and surveillance systems, regardless of vaccination status
Evidence: The correlation of JE vaccination with reduced JE CFR would be consistent with vaccination which not only reduced infection, but also moderated the disease severity [45].

However, it is also possible that the relationship we observe with vaccination is also due to changes in surveillance which were brought with the JE vaccination program [38] or other changes in healthcare that occur concurrently with vaccination, though we did attempt to account for these in the analysis, we could not completely.
The presence of JE immunization program, under-5 mortality, and the age of infection were important predictors to understand spatial-temporal variations in JE CFRs.
214.Rationale: This option is incorrect because it proposes to ignore vaccination status. The passage explicitly describes a correlation between expansion of JE immunization and reduced CFR and identifies immunization program presence as an important predictor of CFR. While matching on surveillance and outbreak history is important, vaccination status materially affects CFR and must be considered when interpreting trends, so interpretation cannot be validly made "regardless of vaccination status." The quoted passages show vaccination both plausibly reduces CFR and is an identified predictor in the analysis.

214.Option: The decline in case-fatality ratios after 2000 is primarily attributable to the expansion of Japanese Encephalitis immunization programs in high-burden countries
Evidence: JE immunization coverage expanded rapidly after 2000. 67% (10/15) countries and areas introduced the immunization programs nationally or sub-nationally after 2000[42], including China and India where most analyzed JE patients came from.
The correlation of JE vaccination with reduced JE CFR would be consistent with vaccination which not only reduced infection, but also moderated the disease severity [45].

However, it is also possible that the relationship we observe with vaccination is also due to changes in surveillance which were brought with the JE vaccination program [38] or other changes in healthcare that occur concurrently with vaccination, though we did attempt to account for these in the analysis, we could not completely.
214.Rationale: This option is not fully supported. The passage shows a temporal association between expanded immunization after 2000 and lower CFRs and notes that vaccination could plausibly reduce severity. However, the authors explicitly caution that the observed association may be confounded by concurrent changes in surveillance, case confirmation, and healthcare improvements, and that they could not entirely separate these effects. Therefore it is not correct to state that the decline is primarily attributable to immunization alone.

214.Option: The observed trend in case-fatality ratios over time is best understood by focusing on countries with the highest vaccination coverage, as they provide the most reliable data on program effectiveness
Evidence: Although 13 countries and areas were included in the modelling, 91% of analyzed JE patients came from China and India.

There were unexplained variations after addressing the current factors and indicated the presence of unobserved features, for instance, improved critical JE case management over time, differences in genotype, differences in healthcare seeking, and heterogenous case-reporting rate which were also crucial in understanding JE CFR variations across time and space.
The proportion of JE endemic countries that conducted JE surveillance and routinely tested JE cases has increased from 21% (5/24) in 2012 to 92% (22/24) in 2016[38, 39].
214.Rationale: This option is incorrect because the passage does not claim that countries with highest vaccination coverage necessarily provide the most reliable data. The authors note that data are dominated by a few countries (China and India), that there are unexplained heterogeneities (case management, genotype, care-seeking), and that surveillance and diagnostic methods vary widely. High vaccination coverage does not automatically eliminate biases from surveillance differences or reporting heterogeneity; thus focusing only on high-coverage countries would not by itself ensure the most reliable interpretation.

214.Option: The decline in case-fatality ratios after 2000 is likely due to improved case confirmation methods and reduced underreporting, independent of vaccination or outbreak frequency
Evidence: The proportion of JE endemic countries that conducted JE surveillance and routinely tested JE cases has increased from 21% (5/24) in 2012 to 92% (22/24) in 2016[38, 39].
For the laboratory-confirmed cases, most observations (146/192) used the WHO case definition: JE IgM antibody in CSF or serum as confirmed by MAC-ELISA on patients with acute encephalitis syndrome.
The correlation of JE vaccination with reduced JE CFR would be consistent with vaccination which not only reduced infection, but also moderated the disease severity [45].

However, it is also possible that the relationship we observe with vaccination is also due to changes in surveillance which were brought with the JE vaccination program [38] or other changes in healthcare that occur concurrently with vaccination, though we did attempt to account for these in the analysis, we could not completely.
214.Rationale: This option overstates the evidence. The passage supports that improved case confirmation and expanded surveillance likely influence observed CFR trends, but it does not support that these factors act independently of vaccination. The authors explicitly state that vaccination may reduce severity and that surveillance changes were often concurrent with vaccination programs, making it incorrect to attribute the decline solely to improved confirmation and underreporting reduction without considering vaccination or other concurrent changes.

214.Option: The observed decline in case-fatality ratios after 2000 may reflect reduced outbreak frequency and improved surveillance more than vaccination alone
Evidence: JE immunization coverage expanded rapidly after 2000. 67% (10/15) countries and areas introduced the immunization programs nationally or sub-nationally after 2000[42], including China and India where most analyzed JE patients came from.
The correlation of JE vaccination with reduced JE CFR would be consistent with vaccination which not only reduced infection, but also moderated the disease severity [45].

However, it is also possible that the relationship we observe with vaccination is also due to changes in surveillance which were brought with the JE vaccination program [38] or other changes in healthcare that occur concurrently with vaccination, though we did attempt to account for these in the analysis, we could not completely.
The proportion of JE endemic countries that conducted JE surveillance and routinely tested JE cases has increased from 21% (5/24) in 2012 to 92% (22/24) in 2016[38, 39].

there are unexplained variations after addressing the current factors and indicated the presence of unobserved features, for instance, improved critical JE case management over time, differences in genotype, differences in healthcare seeking, and heterogenous case-reporting rate which were also crucial in understanding JE CFR variations across time and space.
214.Rationale: This option best matches the nuance in the passage. The authors report a temporal decline in CFR coincident with expanded immunization, but they explicitly caution that the observed decline may be at least partly driven by concurrent improvements in surveillance, diagnostic confirmation, and healthcare/case management and by changing outbreak dynamics. The marked increase in routine surveillance and testing and the acknowledgment of unobserved features that affect CFR support the interpretation that improved surveillance and reduced outbreak frequency (or other concurrent changes) could explain much of the decline rather than vaccination alone.

215.Option: The significantly higher parasite load in VK247-infected individuals compared to those with VK210 or mixed infections provides direct evidence of increased disease severity due to a more virulent variant
Evidence: The parasite loads were higher in individuals infected by VK247 (median of 3.35 log parasites/μL; IQR 3.1–3.8 log parasites/μL) compared to patients infected by VK210 (median of 2.5 log parasites/μL; IQR 1.8–3.1 log parasites/μL) (p = 0.05) and patients with mixed infection (median of 1.8 log parasites/μL; IQR 0.52–3.1 log parasites/μL) (p = 0.01) (Fig 3A).

These findings demonstrate that VK247 may cause a sustained and stronger inflammatory response than VK210 since this response was not followed by IL-10. Due to this absence of association, we suggest that the necessary equilibrium to control the infection is not present in the VK247, since there is a high inflammatory response and parasite load, without association to the counter-regulatory effect of IL-10 cytokine. Based on this context we suggest that VK247 may induce a more severe infection pattern.
215.Rationale: Higher parasite load in VK247-infected individuals is evidence that VK247 is associated with greater parasitaemia and is consistent with greater virulence or severity. However, the question asks specifically which finding most strongly supports the hypothesis that the parasite contributes to a more severe clinical course by disrupting immune regulation. Parasitaemia alone is an indicator of greater pathogen burden and potential severity, but it does not by itself demonstrate disruption of immune regulatory mechanisms. The passage provides more direct evidence of disrupted regulation (loss of the IL-6/IL-10 correlation) separate from parasite load. Therefore, while this finding supports increased severity, it is not the strongest evidence for disruption of immune regulation.

215.Option: The lack of correlation between IL-6 and IL-10 in VK247-infected individuals, despite high inflammatory response and parasite load, indicates disrupted immune regulation
Evidence: The same fact was observed regarding IL-6 concentrations, which levels were higher in patients with VK247 (median of 16.7 pg/mL; IQR 13.9–82.39 pg/mL) compared to patients with VK210 (median of 1.08 pg/mL; IQR 0.0001–25.54 pg/mL) (p = 0.04) and to those with mixed infections (median of 0.08 pg/mL; IQR 0.0001–11.25 pg/mL) (p = 0.05) (Fig 3B).

The variants did not influence the IL-10 concentrations (Fig 3C), but in patients infected with VK210, the IL-10 levels correlated with IL-6 concentrations (r = 0.92 and p<0.0001) (Fig 3E). However, in patients with VK247, this was not observed and no correlation between the two cytokines was found (r = 0.3 and p = 0.68) (Fig 3D), which indicates that the increase in inflammatory cytokines was not followed by the regulatory effect of IL-10 in these patients.
The parasite loads were higher in individuals infected by VK247 (median of 3.35 log parasites/μL; IQR 3.1–3.8 log parasites/μL) compared to patients infected by VK210 ... (p = 0.01) (Fig 3A).
215.Rationale: The hypothesis centers on the parasite causing more severe disease by disrupting immune regulation. The most direct support in the passage is the discordance in VK247 infections: high IL-6 (inflammatory) levels and high parasite load occur without the usual accompanying regulatory IL-10 response (no IL-6/IL-10 correlation), whereas VK210 infections show a strong IL-6/IL-10 correlation. This absence of the compensatory IL-10 response in VK247-infected patients best demonstrates disrupted immune regulation linked to a variant associated with higher parasite burden and inflammatory signaling, and the authors explicitly interpret this as evidence that VK247 may induce a more severe infection pattern.

215.Option: The absence of detectable IFN-γ in plasma of infected individuals suggests that the immune response is suppressed, which may contribute to uncontrolled parasite growth and severe disease
Evidence: Only eight patients had detectable IFN-γ concentrations with a median of approximately zero; however, there was no significant difference compared to the healthy group (Fig 1D).
215.Rationale: While low detectable IFN-γ could reflect a limited Th1-type response in some patients, the passage notes this finding across the infected group and explicitly states there was no significant difference compared to healthy controls. The hypothesis in question concerns variant-specific disruption of immune regulation linked to severity; the stronger, variant-specific evidence in the study is the lack of IL-10 regulation in VK247 despite high IL-6 and parasite load. The IFN-γ observation is neither variant-specific nor tied to the disrupted regulatory pattern the authors emphasize, so it does not most strongly support the stated hypothesis.

215.Option: The observation that IL-10 concentrations were higher in plasma than in PBMC culture supernatants suggests that systemic immune regulation is active in vivo, which may explain the higher parasite load in VK247-infected individuals
Evidence: Interestingly, a different cytokine profile was observed between plasma and the PBMC cultures supernatants. The median IL-10 concentration was 367 times higher in the plasma from patients when compared to that of the culture supernatant.
The concentration of the inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 were higher in the culture supernatant than in the plasma, and IL-6 reached 111 times higher concentrations in the supernatant (Fig 4D).

The variants did not influence the IL-10 concentrations (Fig 3C), but in patients infected with VK210, the IL-10 levels correlated with IL-6 concentrations ... However, in patients with VK247, this was not observed and no correlation between the two cytokines was found (r = 0.3 and p = 0.68) (Fig 3D), which indicates that the increase in inflammatory cytokines was not followed by the regulatory effect of IL-10 in these patients.
215.Rationale: The observation that plasma IL-10 exceeds PBMC supernatant IL-10 indicates systemic (in vivo) regulatory signals exist, but this is a general finding for all patients and does not explain the variant-specific pattern. Crucially, the passage reports that VK247 infections show no IL-6/IL-10 correlation (i.e., lack of regulatory response) despite high IL-10 overall being present across the cohort. Therefore, the plasma-versus-supernatant IL-10 difference does not provide the strongest evidence that VK247 disrupts immune regulation; in fact, it underscores that systemic IL-10 can be present but is not appropriately linked to inflammatory signals in VK247 cases.

216.Option: High viremia levels in the first few days of illness, which exceed the threshold for mosquito infection
Evidence: Nguyet et al. [11] and Whitehorn et al. [12] have identified the plasma DENV viremia levels in Vietnamese dengue patients needed to successfully infect 50% of exposed Ae. aegypti or Ae. albopictus mosquitoes (50% mosquito infectious dose; MID50).

In addition, most ambulatory dengue cases were revealed to have viremia levels that exceed the MID50 in the first few days of their illness, indicating that most are still infectious to Ae. aegypti when they return to their homes.
216.Rationale: The passage provides direct biological evidence that the plasma viremia level relative to the MID50 is the key determinant of whether mosquitoes become infected. It explicitly states that most ambulatory (symptomatic outpatient) cases have viremia exceeding the MID50 in the first few days, making them infectious to Aedes. Therefore high viremia in early illness is the strongest influence on transmission to mosquitoes.

216.Option: The use of DEET-based repellent, which provides longer protection in febrile patients than in healthy individuals
Evidence: we found no difference in the duration of protection from the DEET-based repellent between dengue patients and healthy control participants.
For the 13% DEET-based repellent arms, there was no significant difference in elapsed time to landing between patients versus controls (Hazard ratio 0.71; 95% CI 0.42–1.19; p = 0.20)
216.Rationale: The passage reports that the DEET-based repellent provided similar durations of protection in febrile dengue patients and healthy controls and explicitly found no significant difference. Thus the claim that DEET provides longer protection in febrile patients is not supported by the presented evidence.

216.Option: The presence of symptomatic illness, which correlates with higher viral load and increased mosquito biting frequency
Evidence: Symptomatic dengue patients had been found to transmit DENV to Aedes spp. mosquitoes from ~1.5–2 days prior to fever onset until day 3–6 of illness [8–10].

In addition, most ambulatory dengue cases were revealed to have viremia levels that exceed the MID50 in the first few days of their illness, indicating that most are still infectious to Ae. aegypti when they return to their homes.

The capacity of asymptomatically-infected individuals to transmit to Ae. aegypti has been recently highlighted, but more work is needed to understand their prevalence and importance to the overall epidemiology of dengue [10].
216.Rationale: While symptomatic patients are often infectious during early illness, the passage emphasizes that viral load (viremia relative to the MID50) determines infectivity. It also notes that asymptomatic individuals can transmit, so symptomatic status per se is not the sole or necessarily strongest correlate of transmission. The evidence supports viremia level as the primary driver rather than symptom presence alone.

216.Option: Increased mosquito attraction due to higher body temperature and elevated carbon dioxide production during fever
Evidence: Dengue patients, like other febrile patients, may be more attractive to mosquitoes compared to non-febrile individuals due to their higher body temperature, and therefore higher production of carbon dioxide and possibly other volatile compounds [30–32].

Ae. aegypti appeared to be similarly attracted to both febrile dengue patients and healthy controls when no repellent was worn, with a median time to landing of 18s (10s–40s) in the control group versus 28s (17s–44s) in dengue patients (Hazard ratio of dengue patients vs. control: 0.64; 95% CI 0.36–1.14; p = 0.13).
216.Rationale: Although the passage proposes a biological mechanism by which fever could increase attractiveness to mosquitoes (higher temperature and CO2), the empirical data from the study did not demonstrate a clear increase in mosquito attraction to febrile patients (no significant difference in time to landing). Therefore this factor is plausible but not supported as the strongest influence by the presented evidence—viremia remains the primary determinant.

216.Option: The duration of the febrile phase, which extends from day 1 to day 6 of illness and increases the window of exposure
Evidence: Symptomatic dengue patients had been found to transmit DENV to Aedes spp. mosquitoes from ~1.5–2 days prior to fever onset until day 3–6 of illness [8–10].

In addition, most ambulatory dengue cases were revealed to have viremia levels that exceed the MID50 in the first few days of their illness, indicating that most are still infectious to Ae. aegypti when they return to their homes.
216.Rationale: The duration of the infectious window (roughly spanning the days around fever onset and early illness) does influence transmission opportunity, but the passage indicates that infectivity depends on viremia levels within that window. Thus while a longer febrile phase increases potential exposure time, it is not presented as the most influential factor compared with the magnitude of viremia early in illness.

217.Option: Widespread detection of antifilarial antibodies across multiple villages with no significant association to environmental or occupational factors
Evidence: The highest overall crude prevalence was observed for Bm33 Ab (45.6%, 95% CI 43.7–47.5%), followed by Wb123 Ab (25.6%, 95% CI 24.0–27.3%), Bm14 Ab (13.1%, 95% CI 11.8–14.4%) and Ag (5.1, 95% CI 4.2–5.9%).
Bm33 Ab were more widespread across the territory, while the distribution of villages with high prevalence of Ag, Wb123 and Bm14 Ab was more confined to the north-west of the territory.
There was a consistent positive association between all Abs and tuna cannery workers. Also, a positive association between Bm14 and Bm33 Abs and individuals working in outdoor locations.
The spatial models for all infection makers indicated that there was a positive association between the prevalence of LF and the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.
217.Rationale: The option asserts widespread antibodies with no associations to environment/occupation. The passage does note that Bm33 Ab is widespread, but it also documents that other markers (Ag, Wb123, Bm14) are geographically confined and that antibody positivity shows significant associations with occupational (tuna cannery/outdoor work) and environmental (tree coverage) factors. Thus the pattern described in the option (widespread Ab with no associations) does not match the evidence, so this option is incorrect.

217.Option: Uniform distribution of infection markers across all villages with no evidence of clustering or environmental gradients
Evidence: The results showed that the predicted probability of Ag, Wb123, Bm14 and Bm33 Abs differed geographically across the territory. Areas around Fagali’i and Fagamalo villages in the north-west had the highest predicted probability of all infection markers.

The maps confirm that villages with high prevalence of Bm33 Ab were more widespread across the territory, while the distribution of villages with high prevalence of Ag, Wb123 and Bm14 Ab was more confined to the north-west of the territory.
The high-risk area in the far north-west was also previously identified as potential hotspot of residual infection in cross-sectional surveys conducted in American Samoa in 2010, 2014 and 2016.
Small-scale spatial variation in LF prevalence was observed which indicates that there is scope for further spatial analyses to help inform spatially-targeted interventions in American Samoa.
217.Rationale: The option claims a uniform distribution with no clustering. The passage repeatedly describes geographic heterogeneity, identifies specific high-risk villages (clusters/hotspots), and emphasizes small-scale spatial variation and environmental gradients. Therefore the option is inconsistent with the passage and is incorrect.

217.Option: High prevalence of antigenemia in areas with low tree cover and minimal outdoor occupational activity
Evidence: The spatial models for all infection makers indicated that there was a positive association between the prevalence of LF and the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.

Tree coverage was also positively associated with Ag-positivity, with an estimated increase of 0.4% (95% CrI: 0.1–0.7%) in the odds of Ag-positivity for each 1% increase in the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.
There was a consistent positive association between all Abs and tuna cannery workers. Also, a positive association between Bm14 and Bm33 Abs and individuals working in outdoor locations.
217.Rationale: The option posits high antigen prevalence in low-tree, low-outdoor-activity areas; however, the passage reports a positive association between tree coverage and Ag positivity and also associations with outdoor/tuna cannery occupations. Thus the option contradicts the documented environmental and occupational drivers and is incorrect.

217.Option: Infection risk is primarily driven by age and sex, with no significant role for environmental or occupational factors
Evidence: In the spatial model, females had a 26.8% (95% CrI: 11.0–39.8%) lower risk of being Ag-positive than males. There was a 2.4% (95% CrI: 1.8–3.0%) decrease in the odds of Ag positivity for every year of age.

There were consistent associations between the infection markers and the sociodemographic variables included in the models. The observed differences among females and males and the positive association with age is most likely to be exposure-related.
There was a consistent positive association between all Abs and tuna cannery workers. Also, a positive association between Bm14 and Bm33 Abs and individuals working in outdoor locations.
The spatial models for all infection makers indicated that there was a positive association between the prevalence of LF and the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.
217.Rationale: While age and sex are indeed significant predictors (as the option states), the passage also shows clear, significant roles for environmental (tree coverage) and occupational (tuna cannery, outdoor work) factors. Because environmental and occupational factors are important contributors, the claim that there is no significant role for them is false; therefore this option is incorrect.

217.Option: High infection risk concentrated in specific villages with strong associations to tree cover and outdoor occupational exposure
Evidence: The results showed that the predicted probability of Ag, Wb123, Bm14 and Bm33 Abs differed geographically across the territory. Areas around Fagali’i and Fagamalo villages in the north-west had the highest predicted probability of all infection markers.
The high-risk area in the far north-west was also previously identified as potential hotspot of residual infection in cross-sectional surveys conducted in American Samoa in 2010, 2014 and 2016.
There was a consistent positive association between all Abs and tuna cannery workers. Also, a positive association between Bm14 and Bm33 Abs and individuals working in outdoor locations.
The spatial models for all infection makers indicated that there was a positive association between the prevalence of LF and the extent of tree coverage in the 20 m buffers.

These findings support recent evidence that the home environment may be also an important area for exposure in LF-endemic regions where W. bancrofti is transmitted by the day-biting mosquito, Ae. polynesiensis.
217.Rationale: The passage documents concentrated high predicted probability (clusters/hotspots) in specific villages (e.g., Fagali’i and Fagamalo) and reports statistically significant associations of infection markers with tree cover and with occupational exposures (tuna cannery and outdoor work). These findings directly support the interpretation that residual transmission is driven by localized environmental (tree coverage) and behavioral/occupational patterns rather than uniform community-wide exposure, making this option correct.

218.Option: Misdiagnosis by healthcare providers is a systemic failure that reflects inadequate training and contributes to prolonged health system delay, thereby indirectly affecting patient delay
Evidence: Reported misdiagnosis remained statistically significantly associated with a longer health-system delay in multivariate analysis (Table 5). The likelihood of misdiagnosed patients receiving a later diagnosis was nearly three times higher than those who were not misdiagnosed (those who were either correctly diagnosed or not given any diagnosis on their first visit) (OR 2.867, 95% CI 1.288–6.384, p = 0.01).
The principal association with longer health system delays was found to be patient reported misdiagnosis.
218.Rationale: Although misdiagnosis is clearly a systemic problem and is associated with longer delays, the passage links misdiagnosis specifically to health-system delay (the time between first consulting a medical doctor and receiving a diagnosis). The question asks about factors that influence the timing of medical consultation (i.e., when patients seek care), independent of clinical presentation. Misdiagnosis affects what happens after patients present (health-system delay), not the decision to consult a clinician in the first place, so it does not most directly reflect the social/structural influences on timing of consultation.

218.Option: Fear of social isolation due to leprosy-related stigma is a key structural barrier to timely medical consultation
Evidence: For participants who thought their symptoms were due to leprosy but feared they would be isolated from their community, the odds of them waiting longer before consulting was ten times higher than those who did not fear isolation (OR 10.37, CI 2.18–49.45, p = 0.003).

These findings indicate that stigma towards leprosy sufferers remains despite the availability of a cure, even in endemic regions [12,13]. Fear of isolation was a strong predictor of longer patient delay, implying that community isolation still exists in Brazil, despite compulsory confinement ending in 1962.
218.Rationale: The passage directly identifies fear of community isolation (stigma) as a strong predictor of longer patient delay with a large effect size (OR ~10). This is a social/structural factor acting independently of clinical signs: it changes whether and when people seek care regardless of their symptom severity, and thus most directly reflects how social conditions influence timing of medical consultation.

218.Option: The presence of insensitive skin lesions is a clinical marker that is more likely to prompt early diagnosis, indicating that structural factors such as clinician training influence diagnostic timing
Evidence: The only presenting symptom associated with health system delay was hypo-pigmented, insensitive skin lesions. Patients presenting with insensitive skin lesions had a 55% higher chance of being diagnosed quicker than those who did not present with this symptom (OR 0.463, CI 0.222–0.964, p = 0.039).
218.Rationale: This option describes a clinical feature (insensitive skin lesions) that increases the likelihood of earlier diagnosis; it is a patient clinical presentation factor, not a social or structural determinant of when patients decide to seek care. While clinician training (a structural factor) may influence diagnostic timing after presentation, the question asks about factors influencing the timing of medical consultation independent of clinical presentation — thus a clinical marker is not the correct choice.

218.Option: Perception that symptoms are not serious reflects a lack of health literacy, which is shaped by socioeconomic conditions and educational access
Evidence: Fifty-five participants, (45.1%), said they waited before consulting because they did not believe their symptoms were serious. The odds of them waiting longer was three times higher compared to those who did consider their symptoms serious (OR 3.114, 95% CI 1.235–7.856, p = 0.016).

Many patients appear to still be ignorant regarding leprosy symptoms. Nearly half of the participants in this study (45.1%) waited before consulting a doctor because they did not believe their symptoms were serious.

As leprosy is a disease of the poor and literacy rates are low among this population in Brazil, it could be useful to incorporate graphics or animations into patient education tools from primary care clinics or even in public areas such as bus stops, where educational posters could be displayed [3,36].
218.Rationale: The passage shows that believing symptoms are not serious was a common and significant predictor of delayed consultation (OR ~3). The authors link this ignorance about symptoms to low literacy and poverty in the affected population, tying the perception to socioeconomic and educational structures. Therefore this perception is a social/structural influence on timing of consultation (via health literacy) and is a correct factor.

218.Option: Use of traditional healers is a culturally embedded practice that reflects limited access to formal healthcare systems and systemic inequities in health service availability
Evidence: Participants who visited a traditional healer before a qualified medical doctor had 10 times higher odds of waiting longer before consulting a trained medical doctor than those who did not (OR 10.386, CI 1.092–98.784, p = 0.042).
