Together these statements provide a coherent mechanism in which continuous secretion of parasite glycoproteins produces broad, sustained suppression of DC activation despite ongoing exposure to PAMPs.

240.Option: Gradual degradation of pathogen-associated molecular patterns by parasite-derived enzymes, reducing immune recognition over time
Evidence: Among the 55 McES proteins identified were a selection of enzymes (i.e. proteases, phosphatases, enolases, lipases, aldolases, hydroxylases, fucosidases, isomerases, hydrolases, dehydrogenases and kinases), ion-binding proteins, protein transporters, fatty acid-binding proteins, conserved structural proteins ...
Biochemical analyses of M. corti tetrathyridia ES products (McES) narrowed down the immunosuppressive activity to glycoproteins.
McES broadly impair BMDC responsiveness to different stimuli

To rule out the possibility that McES bind to TLR-4 receptors to prevent the subsequent binding of LPS by steric hindrance, we assessed how the timing of exposure of host DCs to McES might influence the inhibitory effect on IL-12p70 production.
240.Rationale: Although parasite ES contain enzymes (including proteases), the passage attributes immunosuppression specifically to glycoprotein components and demonstrates broad impairment of DC responses across multiple stimuli and timings. The authors also tested and argued against simple receptor-blocking mechanisms and show suppression even when McES are added after activation. There is no direct evidence presented that PAMPs are degraded by parasite enzymes in a manner that reduces recognition; instead the data indicate active immunomodulatory glycoproteins mediate suppression.

241.Option: A negative result for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense may indicate the absence of the target subspecies, but only if the fly was captured during the peak transmission season
Evidence: Identification of T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense is reliant on the detection of specific single-copy genes (TgsGP [18] and SRA [19,20] respectively) for each subspecies. For detection of these low copy genes, the presence of sufficient genetic material is crucial. A negative result may indicate the absence of the target species or simply that insufficient DNA is present.

247.Option: Perform logistic regression analysis of reaction occurrence at 12 months post-treatment initiation, adjusting for clinical risk factors, to evaluate genetic marker effects on reaction timing

The passage shows the investigators chose survival analysis and Cox regression to address time-to-event and adjustment concerns rather than a fixed-time logistic approach.

Then, to estimate the associations between genetic markers and leprosy reaction (outcome), crude and adjusted hazard ratios (HR and aHR, respectively) at 95% confidence interval and p-value were calculated using the Cox proportional-hazards regression model. All variables were adequate in the model, except for the clinical forms variable, which was added as strata in the model to avoid the effect of non-proportionality over time in the analyses.

Then, to estimate the associations between genetic markers and leprosy reaction (outcome), crude and adjusted hazard ratios (HR and aHR, respectively) at 95% confidence interval and p-value were calculated using the Cox proportional-hazards regression model.
247.Rationale: ANOVA is not an appropriate method to compare time-to-event outcomes because it does not handle right-censoring or non-normal time-to-event distributions. 248.Option: Sub-neutralizing antibodies from a primary infection can enhance viral entry during a secondary heterotypic infection via FcγR binding, which can be mitigated by using an antibody that lacks FcγR binding capability
Evidence: Heterotypic secondary infections are the cause of 90% of the DHF/DSS cases reported [8].

One working hypothesis explaining the severity of dengue pathogenesis observed during secondary infection is antibody dependent enhancement (ADE) [7]. These antibody-virus complexes then bind to Fcγ receptors (FcγR) on macrophages and dendritic cells via the Fc portion of the antibody [9]. The result of ADE is a higher number of infected immune cells, leading to heightened immune response to the infection [9].

Balsitis et al. verified using aglycosylated and F(ab’)2 DENV-specific IgG that the interaction between the Fc portion of the serotype cross-reactive antibodies and the FcγR is required to induce ADE. In this study, they demonstrated that the genetically modified DENV-specific antibodies did not enhance DENV2 infection and were able to neutralize DENV infection as well as the non-modified variants.

248.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes ADE as mediated by sub-neutralizing antibodies that form complexes which bind Fcγ receptors on macrophages and dendritic cells, increasing infected immune cells and disease severity. It also provides direct experimental and conceptual evidence that antibodies engineered or selected to avoid FcγR binding (aglycosylated/F(ab')2 forms or avian IgY that 'naturally fails to bind FcγR') do not enhance infection. Therefore the statement that sub-neutralizing antibodies can enhance heterotypic infection via FcγR and that this can be mitigated by antibodies lacking FcγR binding is supported by the passage.

All groups receiving CHIKV antigens generated neutralizing antibody titers that are significantly different (P<0.01) from both control groups (PBS and Ac-GFP) but not significantly different from one another (Fig. 5A). The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).
The 0.1 µg VLP dose showed a further ∼10–20 fold reduction in antibody titers compared to 10 µg inactivated CHIKV (IgG1 P<0.01, IgG2c P<0.01) (Fig. 5B, C).
a single vaccination with 1 µg of unadjuvanted CHIKV VLPs was able to completely protect mice from CHIKV-induced viraemia and arthritis
252.Rationale: Dose clearly affects the magnitude of the initial antibody response and protection in this study (1 µg gave complete protection; 0.1 µg gave reduced titres and partial protection). Dose is therefore an important determinant of how robust the induced immunity is, and higher doses often yield stronger responses. However, when specifically interpreting long-term protection in the absence of boosters, the critical factor is not dose per se but the durability of the immune response that dose elicits (i.e., whether neutralizing antibodies persist at protective levels). Dose influences that durability, but the passage emphasizes neutralizing antibody magnitude/persistence as the direct correlate of lasting protection.

252.Option: The durability of the humoral immune response, particularly the magnitude and persistence of neutralizing antibodies, is the most critical factor in interpreting long-term protection after a single dose
Evidence: antibodies are believed to be required for protection against CHIKV infections [12], [27].

All groups receiving CHIKV antigens generated neutralizing antibody titers that are significantly different (P<0.01) from both control groups (PBS and Ac-GFP) but not significantly different from one another (Fig. 5A). The 1 µg VLP dose induced neutralizing antibody titers comparable with those observed after vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated CHIKV (Fig. 5A).
All animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia (Fig. 6A).
a single vaccination with 1 µg of unadjuvanted CHIKV VLPs was able to completely protect mice from CHIKV-induced viraemia and arthritis in an adult wild-type model of CHIKV arthritis
The VLPs induced a balanced IgG1/IgG2c response, in contrast with CHIKV infection where IgG2c responses dominate [12].
252.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that antibodies are believed to be required for protection and demonstrates a clear relationship between neutralizing antibody titers and sterilizing protection (1 µg VLPs induced neutralizing titers and complete protection against viraemia). Therefore, when assessing long-term protection after a single (non-boosted) immunization with a repetitive, multivalent nanoparticle (VLP) platform, the most critical factor to interpret whether protection will persist is the durability (magnitude and persistence) of the humoral neutralizing antibody response elicited by that single dose. Other factors (dose, cross-reactivity, storage stability, viral clearance kinetics) can modulate protection, but the direct correlate for long-term protection in this context is sustained neutralizing antibody levels.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through asymptomatic genital colonization, which allows the pathogen to evade systemic azithromycin and re-infect skin after minor trauma
Evidence: Asymptomatic carriage of H. ducreyi was demonstrated by PCR in the genital mucosa of about 2% of sex workers in The Gambia [14], but no studies address whether either H. ducreyi or T. p. pertenue potentially colonize non-genital skin.
Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
Our findings suggest that H. ducreyi survives on healthy non-genital skin where even minor trauma could initiate infection.
If azithromycin does not reach the outer surface of skin, it may not interrupt colonization, and these sources may perpetuate the infection in the community.
253.Rationale: While genital mucosal carriage has been documented elsewhere (first quote), the passage's findings and emphasis point to asymptomatic carriage on non-genital (skin) surfaces and environmental reservoirs as the explanations for persistence after MDA. The study directly documents H. ducreyi DNA on healthy skin and discusses skin-colonization escaping systemic azithromycin (second–fourth quotes). The passage does not provide evidence that genital colonization is the main driver of re-infection in these communities, so this option is not supported by the study's observations.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through a reservoir in the gut of infected individuals, which is not affected by systemic azithromycin and allows for re-infection via fecal-oral transmission
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
253.Rationale: The passage describes transmission related to skin contact and presents evidence of DNA on skin, flies, and linens (second–fourth quotes). There is no mention or evidence of gut reservoirs or fecal-oral transmission. The primary hypothesized reservoirs in the study are asymptomatic skin carriage and environmental fomites/insects, not the gastrointestinal tract (first quote). Therefore a gut reservoir/fecal-oral route is unsupported by the passage.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through a reservoir in the respiratory tract of asymptomatic individuals, which is not targeted by systemic azithromycin and allows for airborne transmission
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).
253.Rationale: The passage states transmission is thought to occur via non-intact skin contact and focuses on skin and environmental reservoirs (first and second quotes). It provides no evidence for respiratory tract carriage or airborne spread; instead, the environmental findings implicate flies and fomites (third quote). Thus a respiratory reservoir/airborne transmission hypothesis is not supported by the study.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through direct transmission from symptomatic cases via skin-to-skin contact, which remains frequent despite mass drug administration
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
people from households without an ulcer case had positivity rates that were similar to people with an H. ducreyi ulcer case in the household.
Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
Our findings suggest that H. ducreyi survives on healthy non-genital skin where even minor trauma could initiate infection.
253.Rationale: While direct transmission from symptomatic lesions is a known route (first quote), the data show similar skin-DNA positivity even in households without an index case (second quote), and the authors highlight asymptomatic skin carriage and environmental contamination as plausible reservoirs (third and fourth quotes). These findings indicate that ongoing transmission cannot be fully explained by symptomatic case-to-case skin contact alone; asymptomatic and environmental reservoirs better explain persistence after MDA.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through asymptomatic skin carriage and environmental reservoirs such as flies and contaminated linens, which are not effectively targeted by systemic azithromycin
Evidence: Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
Our findings suggest that H. ducreyi survives on healthy non-genital skin where even minor trauma could initiate infection.
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin...
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
If azithromycin does not reach the outer surface of skin, it may not interrupt colonization, and these sources may perpetuate the infection in the community.
253.Rationale: The passage directly documents H. ducreyi DNA on intact skin of asymptomatic individuals, on flies, and on bed linens (third–fifth quotes) and explicitly states that skin colonization and environmental contamination could allow persistence and transmission despite systemic azithromycin (first, second, and sixth quotes). The authors note azithromycin might not reach the skin surface to eradicate colonization, providing a biologically plausible mechanism for continued presence after MDA. This option matches the study's findings and interpretation.

254.Option: Exposure to bat bodily fluids through contact with surfaces where bats have roosted
Evidence: bats can harbor zoonotic pathogens that may be shed in excreta and bodily fluids (eg., feces, urine, saliva, blood, etc.) [19–20].
Buildings, like family homes, places of worship, and schools, can be highly permeable to bats, and with ongoing habitat loss bats are increasingly using these structures as roosts [26–27].

The presence of small mammals in these spaces can create opportunities for human and domestic animal contact with wildlife and their excreta, potentially exposing them to wildlife-borne pathogens [18].

Indirect contacts were frequently reported in our study and are also common pathways for zoonotic pathogen spillover [39]. Bat excreta reported in these indirect interactions, mostly feces, can contain pathogens shed by bats, including coronaviruses, rotaviruses, and paramyxoviruses that are viral families of human health concern [20].
254.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that bats shed pathogens in excreta and bodily fluids and that buildings used as roosts create opportunities for human contact with those materials on surfaces. Contact with contaminated surfaces (bodily fluids/excreta) in shared built environments is a direct pathway for indirect environmental transmission and is therefore supported by the text.

254.Option: Consumption of fruit pulp contaminated by bat saliva in shared indoor environments
Evidence: Many frugivorous bat species chew and eject saliva-covered fruit pulp below their roosts, which domestic animals may then consume and become exposed to shed pathogens [44].
Indeed, it is thought that Nipah and Hendra virus, both paramyxoviruses with high human mortality, emerged in pigs and horses in this fashion, respectively [25].
Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).
254.Rationale: While the passage describes transmission via saliva-contaminated fruit pulp, it specifically describes this route in relation to domestic animals consuming pulp below roosts and the emergence of viruses through animal intermediates (pigs/horses). The passage emphasizes feces/urine as the dominant indirect contact in buildings. It does not describe people consuming saliva-contaminated fruit pulp in shared indoor environments as a primary or documented pathway in this study, so this option is not supported as the most direct indoor environmental contamination route by the passage.

254.Option: Contact with bat feces and urine in shared indoor environments
Evidence: Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).

Indirect contacts were frequently reported in our study and are also common pathways for zoonotic pathogen spillover [39]. Bat excreta reported in these indirect interactions, mostly feces, can contain pathogens shed by bats, including coronaviruses, rotaviruses, and paramyxoviruses that are viral families of human health concern [20].
Most properties (88%) had bat presence year-round (n = 90).
254.Rationale: The passage directly identifies contact with bat feces and urine as the predominant form of indirect contact in buildings and specifies that bat excreta can contain viruses of human health concern. Thus, contact with feces/urine in shared indoor environments most directly increases the likelihood of indirect environmental pathogen transmission according to the study findings.

254.Option: Inhalation of bat fecal dust from contaminated surfaces in shared indoor environments
Evidence: Fungal pathogens, like Histoplasma capsulatum, the causative agent of histoplasmosis, may also be inhaled from bat fecal dust and have infected people living in buildings with bat roosts in Africa [40].
Buildings, like family homes, places of worship, and schools, can be highly permeable to bats, and with ongoing habitat loss bats are increasingly using these structures as roosts [26–27].
Indirect contacts were frequently reported in our study and are also common pathways for zoonotic pathogen spillover [39].
254.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that inhalation of bat fecal dust is a documented route of infection (e.g., histoplasmosis) for people living in buildings with bat roosts. Inhalation of contaminated dust is an indirect environmental transmission pathway and is therefore supported as a key mechanism by the text.

254.Option: Direct contact with live bats during attempts to remove them from buildings
Evidence: Close to half of participants (39%) reported direct contact with bats, including people touching bats (n = 40) and one report of being bitten.
Attempts to remove bats from buildings were reported by almost 80% of participants (n = 81). Of those reporting removal efforts, almost half reported direct contact with bats (n = 34).
Direct contacts can expose humans to lethal viruses hosted by bats, with various lyssaviruses (including rabies virus) being the most well-known bat-borne pathogens transmitted in this manner [38].
254.Rationale: This option describes direct contact, which the passage treats separately from indirect environmental transmission. The question asks specifically about indirect pathogen transmission via environmental contamination. While direct contact during removal increases risk of direct transmission (e.g., bites, lyssaviruses), it is not an indirect environmental contamination route and thus does not answer the question as posed.

255.Option: The combined model uses QMRA to estimate exposure and SIR to predict the number of new infections per day, allowing for direct inference of the proportion of diarrhea cases attributable to waterborne transmission without considering the probability of symptomatic illness
Evidence: To estimate the risk of infection for an individual child over the course of a single day (individual risk), accounting for boiling and other factors, we produced risk plots for each pathogen and tubewell type and each year using the output from the QMRA model (i.e. probability of infection from drinking tubewell water for one day, see Fig 1).

If P(i) = 1, the probability that the infection resulted in a diarrheic symptom (P(s)) was modeled based on published studies of the diarrhea morbidity ratio for children diagnosed with Cryptosporidium [45, 47] or Giardia [9, 46] in developing countries.

To estimate the waterborne child diarrhea disease burden in the study population attributable to Cryptosporidium and Giardia protozoal contamination of tubewell drinking water, we used an SIR model coupled with the QMRA model, above, to longitudinally track the daily infection status of each individual child in our study population over time (Fig 2)...
255.Rationale: The option is incorrect because, although the combined approach uses QMRA to estimate exposure and the SIR to project infections over time, the study explicitly models the probability that an infection becomes symptomatic (P(s)) and uses that to estimate diarrheic cases. Thus the inference of attributable diarrhea does consider the probability of symptomatic illness rather than ignoring it.

255.Option: The combined model uses QMRA to estimate pathogen concentration and SIR to simulate the spread of infection, allowing for direct comparison of model outputs to observed diarrhea rates without adjusting for household-level water treatment behaviors
Evidence: To account for the effects of boiling water (the only point of use treatment method used by the study population) on parasite viability, we used WHO reported [39] 6 log10 reduction values for Cryptosporidium and Giardia from boiling (i.e. 6 log10 = 99.9999% reduction) and used household survey data collected from study households (n = 355) to estimate the fraction of households that boiled their drinking water (9% and 16% of deep and shallow tubewell users, respectively, reported boiling; see Table 1, B, boiling rates) (Appendix B.5 in Supporting Information).
SIR model ... assigning each to a tubewell type (TW) and to boiling (B) before drinking as a function of the household fractions for each practice in the population (see TW and B in Table 1).

Using results from the SIR model, we estimated the diarrheal disease burden associated with drinking tubewell water during each monsoon season (mid-June to mid-September 2012 and 2013) for the population of < 5 children in the Sanitation Trial (population burden) and compared modeling estimates to observed levels of child diarrhea measured in the Sanitation Trial over the same periods.
255.Rationale: This option is incorrect because the combined QMRA–SIR modeling explicitly accounts for household-level water treatment (boiling) by incorporating household survey estimates of boiling rates and the boiling reduction factor into the exposure and SIR assignments. The model outputs are compared to observed surveillance with household treatment accounted for.

255.Option: The combined model simulates how daily exposure leads to cumulative illness, recovery, and immunity, enabling estimation of time-varying diarrhea prevalence that can be compared to observed surveillance data
Evidence: To estimate the waterborne child diarrhea disease burden in the study population attributable to Cryptosporidium and Giardia protozoal contamination of tubewell drinking water, we used an SIR model coupled with the QMRA model, above, to longitudinally track the daily infection status of each individual child in our study population over time (Fig 2)...

In our SIR model, S means a child is susceptible ... I means a child is infected ... and R is a child who has recovered from a diarrheic infection from Cryptosporidium or Giardia and is immune to infection for the moment.
Once the illness event ended a child was assumed to transition into the recovered state and be temporarily immune to reinfection for a duration of seven days post-infection [17].

To align with the three-month monsoon period when tubewells were sampled in 2012 and 2013, the SIR model was run on a daily time step for 98 days each year (allowing for eight days of model warm-up to establish baseline distributions of SIR states)...

Using results from the SIR model, we estimated the diarrheal disease burden associated with drinking tubewell water during each monsoon season ... and compared modeling estimates to observed levels of child diarrhea measured in the Sanitation Trial over the same periods.
255.Rationale: The option correctly describes the added value of integrating QMRA and SIR: QMRA provides daily infection probabilities from exposure, and the SIR framework converts those daily infections into time-varying counts of symptomatic infections, recovery, and temporary immunity on a daily time step. This yields modeled diarrhea prevalence over time that can be sampled and directly compared to surveillance data.

255.Option: The combined model applies QMRA to estimate daily infection risk and SIR to calculate the average duration of illness, enabling estimation of the total number of diarrhea episodes per child over the study period
Evidence: For children drinking protozoa-contaminated tubewell water in our study population, we estimated the risk of infection for an individual child over the course of a single day (individual risk), accounting for boiling and other factors, and compared the expected risk for different households (i.e., deep vs. shallow tubewell users), years (i.e. 2012–2013) and protozoa (i.e. Cryptosporidium vs. Giardia)...

To model this variability, we used a duration of illness parameter (D) (see Table 1) fit from published sources. For Cryptosporidium, we identified four studies ... From these studies, the mean duration of cryptosporidiosis and giardiasis is 10 (IQR 3–14) and 11 (IQR 6–14) days respectively.

To estimate the waterborne child diarrhea disease burden in the study population attributable to Cryptosporidium and Giardia ... we used an SIR model coupled with the QMRA model, above, to longitudinally track the daily infection status of each individual child in our study population over time (Fig 2)...

Using results from the SIR model, we estimated the diarrheal disease burden associated with drinking tubewell water during each monsoon season (mid-June to mid-September 2012 and 2013) for the population of < 5 children in the Sanitation Trial (population burden) and compared modeling estimates to observed levels of child diarrhea measured in the Sanitation Trial over the same periods.
255.Rationale: This option is correct because the QMRA provides daily infection probabilities and the SIR model uses an illness-duration parameter to represent how long infections last, allowing the simulation to accumulate episodes over the study period for each child. Those simulated episodes are then used to estimate total diarrhea burden (episodes/prevalence) over the monsoon period.

255.Option: The combined model estimates the probability of infection from a single exposure event and directly calculates the total number of symptomatic cases over the study period without accounting for individual immunity or reinfection
Evidence: In our QMRA, we used information about the hazard (i.e. contaminated drinking water), the exposure (i.e. amount drunk, amount treated, etc.), the target pathogen (i.e. Cryptosporidium and Giardia) and the host (i.e. children < 5), described by statistical distributions or point estimates, to estimate the probability that exposure resulted in ingestion of a target pathogen, and that the estimated amount ingested resulted in an infection for the host.
Once the illness event ended a child was assumed to transition into the recovered state and be temporarily immune to reinfection for a duration of seven days post-infection [17].

Only children in the susceptible state could transition into the infected state and only if their infection resulted in diarrheic symptoms ... while children in the infected and recovered states were protected from infection in the model.
255.Rationale: This option is incorrect because the combined model does account for immunity and the possibility of reinfection: the SIR component explicitly includes a recovered state with a specified temporary immunity period, and transitions between susceptible, infected (symptomatic), and recovered states are modeled over time rather than assuming independent single exposure events without immunity.

256.Option: Combined facial defects involving the cheek, nose, and upper lip, as these complex lesions are associated with the highest risk of postoperative complications and poor aesthetic outcomes
Evidence: Most defects resulting from Noma involve the lateral and anterolateral aspects of the face and are often combined with severe functional deficits [19].
Nearly 56% (n = 91) of the total facial defects reported in the overall medical records are combined in form.

Most of the surveyed patients suffered from extensive Noma-induced facial disfigurements, which exposed them to eminent functional limitations, social discrimination, and negative psycho-social effects.
256.Rationale: While the passage documents that combined defects are common and often associated with severe functional deficits and psychosocial consequences, it does not single combined defects out as the clinical feature that most strongly predicts poor long-term prognosis after survival of the acute phase. The text specifically identifies trismus as “one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals” and links trismus to persistent functional impairment and poor outcomes; by contrast, combined defects are described as frequent and debilitating but not emphasized as the single strongest predictor of poor prognosis despite survival. The provided quotes therefore support that combined defects are important sequelae but do not support the stronger claim in the option that they are the most predictive feature of poor prognosis post-acute phase.

256.Option: Complete loss of the nose or lips, as these anatomical defects are associated with the most severe facial disfigurement and social stigma
Evidence: A single entire nose and central upper lip loss, two total nose losses, two complete lower lip loss, and one complete upper lip loss.
complete absence of facial tissues such as the nose, upper lip, and lower lip has been reported.
Often, those patients that survive are not only severely disfigured, but also rejected from family and society [22].

Most of the surveyed patients suffered from extensive Noma-induced facial disfigurements, which exposed them to eminent functional limitations, social discrimination, and negative psycho-social effects.
256.Rationale: Complete loss of the nose or lips certainly indicates severe disfigurement and is linked to social stigma and psychosocial harm in the passage. However, the question asks which clinical feature most strongly suggests a poor prognosis despite survival from the acute phase. The passage emphasizes functional sequelae—particularly trismus—as a disabling condition that perpetuates malnutrition, speech and feeding difficulties, and has a poor prognosis for surgical release. Although complete loss of central facial structures implies severe long-term morbidity, the text provides more direct language linking trismus to ongoing functional impairment and poor prognosis among survivors. Therefore, while complete tissue loss is a serious outcome, the passage supports trismus as the stronger predictor of poor long-term prognosis.

256.Option: Severe trismus, as it leads to impaired mastication and swallowing, which can perpetuate malnutrition and stunting in survivors
Evidence: Most survivors present with prominent facial deformities, trismus or ankylosis of the temporomandibular joint, and extensive muscle and skin contracture, which leads to the difficulty of opening and closing of the mouth, thereby, trouble in mastication and swallowing, oral incontinence, and speech difficulties [12].
The problems in mastication and swallowing can also further exacerbate or cause malnutrition and as a result, many young patients experience significant stunting of growth [13].

A retrospective study that investigated the long-term results of trismus release among Noma patients reported a poor prognosis. This study showed that trismus is one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals [41].
Out of the 163 medical records reviewed, 74.9% (n = 122) have reported grade 1 to grade 4 levels of trismus
256.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies trismus (ankylosis of the temporomandibular joint) as a common, functionally disabling sequela among survivors and links it to difficulty opening the mouth, impaired mastication and swallowing, and subsequent exacerbation or causation of malnutrition and stunting. It also cites a study showing poor prognosis after trismus release, directly supporting the assertion that severe trismus most strongly predicts poor long-term outcomes despite surviving the acute phase. These direct statements make severe trismus the best-supported answer in the passage.

256.Option: Grade-4 facial tissue damage (75–100% anatomical damage), as it represents the most severe structural destruction and is associated with the highest mortality risk
Evidence: The NOIPTUS classifies facial anatomical tissue damage into four classes. The classes are graded from 1 to 4, depending on the severity of facial tissue damage. Grade-1, Grade-2, Grade-3 and Grade-4 imply 0–25%, 25–50%, 50–75%, and 75–100% facial anatomical damages respectively.
Nearly 8% have reported 75–100% facial anatomical landmarks damage.
The acute phase of the disease has a high mortality, and the evolution is exceptionally rapid, causing the loss of soft and bony facial tissues within days [17,18].

Even after modern and sophisticated interventions (particularly reconstructive and plastic surgeries), Noma is associated with considerable morbidity and mortality, and, understandably, psychosocial impacts on the patients remain substantial [21].
256.Rationale: Grade-4 represents the most extensive tissue destruction and the passage notes that severe disease and the acute phase have high mortality. However, the question asks about a feature that most strongly suggests a poor prognosis despite survival from the acute phase. The passage does not explicitly state that grade-4 damage is the single strongest predictor of poor long-term prognosis among survivors; instead it emphasizes disabling functional sequelae such as trismus and their role in perpetuating malnutrition and poor outcomes. Grade-4 damage is clearly severe and linked to high mortality in the acute phase, but the passage provides stronger, explicit evidence for trismus as the feature most predictive of poor prognosis among survivors.

256.Option: The presence of multiple missing teeth, as this indicates extensive dental destruction and is linked to poor oral hygiene and malnutrition
Evidence: On the other hand, 25.8% (n = 42) are reported to have healthy teeth, while the remaining 66.9% (n = 109) have reported a varying number of missing teeth. The number of missing teeth ranges from 1 to 19 in patients affected by the disease.
Out of the total 163 patients reviewed, 7.4% (n = 12) had no dental information.

Most survivors present with prominent facial deformities, trismus or ankylosis of the temporomandibular joint, and extensive muscle and skin contracture, which leads to the difficulty of opening and closing of the mouth, thereby, trouble in mastication and swallowing, oral incontinence, and speech difficulties [12].

A retrospective study that investigated the long-term results of trismus release among Noma patients reported a poor prognosis. This study showed that trismus is one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals [41].
256.Rationale: Multiple missing teeth are commonly reported in the cohort and reflect extensive dental involvement, but the passage does not link missing teeth specifically to being the strongest predictor of poor long-term prognosis after surviving the acute phase. By contrast, the passage highlights trismus as a disabling sequela associated with persistent functional impairment (feeding, swallowing) and worsening malnutrition, and even cites poor prognosis after trismus release. Therefore, although missing teeth indicate serious morbidity, they are not presented in the passage as the most prognostically important feature among survivors.

257.Option: High levels of synonymous polymorphism across multiple lineages of the pathogen, indicating relaxed selection on the gene
Evidence: The neutral theory of evolution states that most evolutionary change at the molecular level is caused by the fixation of neutral alleles through random genetic drift [1].
Positive selection is inferred when ω [13] (the ratio of non-synonymous (dN) to synonymous (dS) mutations between species) exceeds 1.
None of these genes showed evidence of being under positive selection in the P. brasiliensis branches, as illustrated in Table 2.
257.Rationale: High levels of synonymous polymorphism reflect neutral evolution or relaxed constraint, not adaptive antigenic change driven by host immunity. The passage contrasts neutral expectations with positive selection (dN/dS > 1) as the signature of adaptive, host-driven change. Therefore synonymous polymorphism alone would not strongly indicate surface-antigen alteration driven by host defenses.

257.Option: A significant excess of non-synonymous substitutions in the gene's coding region compared to synonymous substitutions, detected using the M-K test
Evidence: Positive selection is inferred when ω [13] (the ratio of non-synonymous (dN) to synonymous (dS) mutations between species) exceeds 1.

M-K tests.M-K tests [18] between the P. brasiliensis sensu lato and H. capsulatum, using the aligned regions previously sequenced as well as sequences retrieved from GenBank, were calculated using the DNASP analysis program [42].
M-K tests were significant for p27, cdc42 and fks (p27: P = 0.043594; cdc42: P = 0.000993; fks: P = 0.000017; Table 3) when H. capsulatum was used as an outgroup.
257.Rationale: An excess of non-synonymous substitutions relative to synonymous substitutions (significant McDonald–Kreitman test) is classical evidence of adaptive evolution at the protein level. For a surface-exposed virulence factor, such a signal is consistent with selection altering amino acids (e.g., antigenic sites) in response to host defenses. Thus a significant M-K result supports host-driven adaptive change in the gene.

257.Option: Persistent non-synonymous polymorphisms in antigenic regions predicted to be recognized by host immune cells
Evidence: The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection [51].

Using the Bayesian Empirical Bayes (BEB) analysis, 19 sites with a posterior probability greater than 95% of having a greater than 1 value were identified. ... seven of the sites under positive selection were located as potential epitopes as predicted with SYFPEITHI.

In gp43, the M-K test was not able to detect positive selected within the P. brasiliensis lineage due to the excess of non-synonymous substitutions within and across species. The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection [51].
257.Rationale: Persistent non-synonymous polymorphisms specifically located in predicted antigenic/epitope regions, maintained across lineages, are a hallmark of balancing or diversifying selection driven by host immune recognition. The passage uses gp43 as exactly this example: non-synonymous variation in predicted epitopes maintained by selection, supporting antigenic alteration as a driver of molecular evolution.

257.Option: A high dN/dS ratio in a gene with no known antigenic function, suggesting general adaptive evolution unrelated to host immunity
Evidence: Positive selection is inferred when ω [13] (the ratio of non-synonymous (dN) to synonymous (dS) mutations between species) exceeds 1.

Only two genes (p27 and gp43) have unknown functions. All the others were classified in four different categories of genes according to their functions: metabolic related genes (fas2, his1), cell wall related genes (fks, mnn5, ags1), heat shock proteins, detoxification related genes (tsa1, sod1, hsp88) and signal transduction genes (cdc42, cst20).
Some of these genes are involved in the escape from immune recognition (tsa1, sod1).
257.Rationale: A high dN/dS ratio indicates adaptive evolution, but if the gene lacks any known surface/antigenic function, it is not strong, specific evidence that antigenic alteration driven by host defenses is the cause. The passage distinguishes genes involved in immune escape from other functional categories; adaptive change in a non-antigenic gene may reflect other selective pressures (metabolism, environment), so this pattern alone does not most strongly support surface antigen alteration by host immunity.

257.Option: A recent fixation of a single non-synonymous mutation in a virulence gene across all pathogen lineages, indicating strong directional selection
Evidence: p27, cdc42 and fks are genes that are depauperate in genetic variation, as is expected for regions in which advantageous amino acid replacements have been fixed by positive selection.
The TRMCAs for cdc42, p27 and fks were significantly lower than the other genes as is expected if a gene is under positive directional selection.

The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection [51].
257.Rationale: Fixation of a single advantageous non-synonymous mutation (a selective sweep/directional selection) indicates past strong selection, but it reduces polymorphism and does not produce the persistent antigenic diversity typical of immune-driven surface antigen alteration (which often shows maintained non-synonymous polymorphisms or high variability at epitopes). The passage contrasts genes showing directional sweeps (low variation) with gp43, which shows maintained polymorphism and epitope variation—the latter better supports ongoing host-driven antigenic strategy.

258.Option: The use of blood samples collected close to the time of exposure reduced misclassification of pre-existing immunity, enhancing the accuracy of the exposure-outcome relationship
Evidence: Two major strengths of this study design likely explain why a significant association between NTs and protection from infection was observed in contrast to previous prospective cohort studies.

Whereas earlier cohort studies typically analyzed NTs in blood samples collected up to six months or more prior to the incident DENV infection, our study provided blood samples from within two weeks prior to virus exposure, thus minimizing the confounding factor of variations in antibody kinetics.

Although we could not document the exact day of virus inoculation in “susceptible” subjects, the two week interval was likely sufficient to ensure that day 0 blood collections occurred before exposure.
DENV IgM ELISA was negative at day 14/15 indicating that infection was still early in its course.
258.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies the short interval between blood collection (day 0) and presumptive exposure (within two weeks) as a major strength that minimized misclassification due to waning or changing antibody titers. By measuring neutralizing titers immediately prior to exposure, the investigators obtained a much more accurate measure of pre-existing immunity relevant to protection, which increases the study's ability to detect an association even with a small sample size.

258.Option: The use of PCR-positive cases and PCR-negative controls from the same household ensured that both groups were exposed to the same viral strain, reducing confounding by serotype variation
Evidence: In order to create a “non-susceptible” comparison group, we identified contact subjects from the two studies who lived in the same house as another subject who was DENV positive on day 0, but were themselves DENV negative by nested PCR and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 15 (KPSII study), or by NS1 ELISA and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 14 (DEVOL study).
These DENV negative “non-susceptible” subjects were presumed to have a high likelihood of exposure to DENV during the cluster investigation, yet still did not become infected.
In both the KPSII and DEVOL studies, the exposure serotype for all “non-susceptible” subjects was presumed to be the same as the infecting serotype for the index case from the same cluster.
258.Rationale: The investigators deliberately selected non-susceptible controls from the same household/cluster as infected individuals, and they state that the exposure serotype was presumed to match the index case. This shared household clustering increases the likelihood that cases and controls experienced the same exposure and serotype, reducing confounding by serotype differences and improving comparability between groups—another study design feature that would help detect a true association despite limited sample size.

258.Option: The use of a two-week window for blood collection allowed for the detection of both pre-existing and newly formed neutralizing antibodies, increasing the likelihood of identifying protective immunity
Evidence: our study provided blood samples from within two weeks prior to virus exposure, thus minimizing the confounding factor of variations in antibody kinetics.

Although we could not document the exact day of virus inoculation in “susceptible” subjects, the two week interval was likely sufficient to ensure that day 0 blood collections occurred before exposure.
DENV IgM ELISA was negative at day 14/15 indicating that infection was still early in its course.
No data or blood was collected after day 15.
258.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that the two-week window was used to capture pre-exposure neutralizing antibody status and to avoid confounding from antibody kinetics. It does not claim the window was intended to detect newly formed antibodies; in fact, no blood was collected after day 14/15, and IgM negativity at day 14/15 indicates infection was early. Thus the two-week window reduced misclassification of pre-existing immunity rather than detecting newly formed antibodies, so this option's wording is incorrect.

258.Option: The use of multiple DENV serotypes in the PRNT assay allowed for the detection of cross-protective immune responses, increasing the sensitivity of the association analysis
Evidence: To determine neutralizing antibody status just prior to exposure for all “susceptible” and “non-susceptible” subjects, day 0 blood samples were tested by an in-house plaque reduction neutralization test (PRNT) using all four DENV serotypes and JEV as previously described [24], [25].

Homotypic NTs were more strongly associated with protection than heterotypic NTs except in the case of DENV-2 infections, in which pre-existing heterotypic DENV-4 NTs were also positively associated with protection against DENV-2.

This is the first human epidemiological study to report such an association and provides some support for the use of PRNT titers as a correlate of protective immunity in dengue epidemiology studies and vaccine development efforts.
258.Rationale: While the investigators did use multiple serotypes in PRNT and explored heterotypic as well as homotypic responses (noting, for example, a heterotypic DENV-4 association with DENV-2 protection), the passage identifies the timing of blood collection and the household exposure selection as the primary strengths that explain why the association was detectable despite small numbers. Use of multiple serotypes aided interpretation (e.g., identifying serotype-specific differences) but the text does not claim it was the key factor that overcame the small sample size to produce a significant result; therefore this option overstates that role.

258.Option: The inclusion of both children and adults in the study increased the diversity of immune responses, improving the generalizability of the association between neutralizing antibodies and protection
Evidence: Because of the small numbers, we used data from two different geographic cluster studies over multiple years. The inclusion of both adults and children with likely differing immunological backgrounds may have affected our findings for the DENV-2 analysis, possibly accounting for the higher DENV-2 NTs associated with protection.
The inclusion of adults in the DEVOL study compared to only children in the KPSII study could have introduced an additional confounding factor in interpreting NT values.
258.Rationale: The passage actually raises the inclusion of adults as a potential source of confounding and a limitation, not as a strength that improved power or generalizability. The authors caution that differing immunological backgrounds between adults and children may have affected results (particularly for DENV-2). Thus this option is not supported by the text.

259.Option: In high-transmission settings, the high proportion of sub-microscopic infections indicates that routine surveillance using light microscopy is sufficient to detect most P. vivax cases and guide interventions effectively
Evidence: A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) in cross-sectional surveys where the LM P. vivax prevalence is less than 45% (P<0.001, Deviance = 7.32, df = 29).

This suggests that a relatively larger proportion of P. vivax infections are SM in areas with low transmission intensity compared to areas with high transmission intensity, although SM P. vivax is prevalent in both type of settings.

SM P. vivax constitutes between 1.5% and 100% of all P. vivax infections detected by PCR (average 69.5%) in these settings, and this proportion is significantly higher than that of the sympatric P. falciparum in these settings.
259.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the proportion of sub-microscopic (SM) infections is larger in low-transmission settings and that SM infections are common across settings. It does not support the claim that routine LM is sufficient in high-transmission settings to detect most P. vivax cases; indeed, PCR prevalence was often higher than LM and SM infections can range widely (average 69.5% of PCR-detected infections). Thus the option's assertion is not supported by the evidence.

259.Option: Since sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are asymptomatic, they do not contribute to transmission and can be safely ignored in elimination planning
Evidence: mature gametocytes are present earlier in P. vivax infections meaning they can infect mosquitoes at an early stage of infection, with SM P. vivax gametocytes shown to be able to successfully infect mosquitoes [8], [9].

This suggests that without PCR or other comparably sensitive methods, an average of 69% of P. vivax infections would be undetected hence antimalarial treatment will usually not be sought. This results in a potentially large group of people carrying parasites that have the potential to transmit P. vivax [8], [9] and maintain the transmission cycle.

SM P. vivax infections not only have negative health impact on the infected individual, but will also contribute to P. vivax transmission both from the current infection and subsequent relapses, and thus present a major challenge for malaria elimination programs.
259.Rationale: Although many SM infections are asymptomatic (the passage reports very high proportions asymptomatic), the passage also states that SM P. vivax can infect mosquitoes and contribute to transmission, including via future relapses. Therefore SM infections cannot be ignored in elimination planning.

259.Option: Because SM P. vivax infections are more common in adults, surveillance should focus on adult populations to detect the majority of transmissible infections
Evidence: The meta-analysis revealed that the SM P. falciparum is more common in adults, in areas with low transmission intensities and in chronic infections [6].

Fifteen of the 25 publications (21 of the 31 surveys) described prevalence of P. vivax infection in different age groups, but only 12 publications (16 surveys) analysed the relationships between P. vivax LM or PCR prevalence and age.

Seven publications (eight surveys) found no significant association between the relative proportion of SM P. vivax infection and host age [14], [15], [17], [22], [27], [28], [30], while five publications (eight surveys) reported that the relative proportion of SM P. vivax increased with age [12], [23], [31], [32], [34]. Neither the LM nor the PCR P. vivax prevalence was significantly different between the studies that did and did not find an association with age (Mann Whitney test, P>0.05).
259.Rationale: The passage shows mixed and inconsistent findings on age and SM P. vivax: some studies find higher SM in older age groups, others find no association. It does not justify focusing surveillance solely on adults; SM infections occur across ages and surveillance strategies should not be limited to adults based on the available evidence.

259.Option: The negative relationship between LM prevalence and SM proportion suggests that increasing the sensitivity of light microscopy would reduce the proportion of sub-microscopic infections across all transmission settings
Evidence: A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7)...

From a technical point of view, the quality of microscopy is an important factor that can contribute to the level of LM P. vivax, and thus SM P. vivax. The prevalence and relative proportion of SM P. vivax would increase if the microscopist is relatively poor at detecting parasites, or decrease if there are false positive results (eg cell debris wrongly classified as a parasite).

PCR P. vivax prevalence was significantly higher than that of LM P. vivax prevalence in these surveys, demonstrating the presence of a sub-population carrying SM P. vivax across all transmission settings.
259.Rationale: While better microscopy quality could reduce some PCR+/LM- classifications (i.e. reclassify some infections as LM-detectable), the negative relationship described is primarily about transmission intensity (LM prevalence) and not solely a function of microscopy sensitivity. Many SM infections have parasite densities below the practical detection limit of microscopy even with skillful readers, and PCR detects substantially more infections. Thus the statement that simply increasing LM sensitivity would reduce SM proportion across all settings is overstated and not fully supported by the passage.

259.Option: In low-transmission settings, surveillance based on light microscopy will substantially underestimate the true burden of P. vivax and fail to identify a major reservoir of transmissible infections, necessitating more sensitive diagnostics for elimination success
Evidence: A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7)... This suggests that a relatively larger proportion of P. vivax infections are SM in areas with low transmission intensity compared to areas with high transmission intensity, although SM P. vivax is prevalent in both type of settings.

SM P. vivax constitutes between 1.5% and 100% of all P. vivax infections detected by PCR (average 69.5%) in these settings... This suggests that without PCR or other comparably sensitive methods, an average of 69% of P. vivax infections would be undetected hence antimalarial treatment will usually not be sought.

SM P. vivax infections not only have negative health impact on the infected individual, but will also contribute to P. vivax transmission both from the current infection and subsequent relapses, and thus present a major challenge for malaria elimination programs.
In order to maximize PCR sensitivity in detecting SM parasite infections, >150 µL of whole blood should be used when possible.
mass screening using PCR-based or comparably sensitive methods and radical cure approach would be required to identify and treat all SM P. vivax infected subjects.
259.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that in low-transmission settings a larger share of infections are sub-microscopic, that LM substantially under-detects infections (on average 69% would be undetected without PCR), and that these SM infections can sustain transmission and relapses. It therefore supports the conclusion that microscopy-based surveillance will substantially underestimate burden in low-transmission settings and that more sensitive diagnostics (or alternative strategies like mass screening or MDA) are needed for elimination.

260.Option: The lack of integration of NTDs into the core curriculum's transmission-based classification may result in students failing to recognize the shared epidemiological patterns among these diseases
Evidence: The epidemiology of communicable diseases course for medical and nursing students is divided into general epidemiology (infectious cycle, pattern of disease occurrence, and general measures of prevention and control of communicable diseases) and specific epidemiology. The specific epidemiology of infectious diseases is divided according to the 4 main modes of transmission, namely: droplet, foodborne, contact, and arthropod borne infections.
Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs;
260.Rationale: The passage states the course is organized by transmission modes and that individual NTDs are covered, yet explicitly notes that the term 'NTDs' is not mentioned in the course materials; concurrently only 26% of students knew the meaning of NTDs. This combination supports the interpretation that lack of explicit integration or labeling of these conditions as a unified group in the transmission-based curriculum can prevent students from recognizing their shared epidemiological patterns and appreciating them as a coherent category.

260.Option: The absence of a unifying conceptual framework for NTDs in the curriculum may prevent students from recognizing these diseases as a distinct public health priority despite exposure to individual diseases
Evidence: Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs;
Hence, Egyptian medical and nursing curricula should be updated. NTDs concepts, epidemiology, and management in detail must be integrated in the medical and nursing curricula and practical training.
260.Rationale: The passage documents that while constituent diseases are taught, the unifying label and conceptual framing 'NTDs' are absent from course materials, and that only a minority of students know the term. The authors explicitly recommend integrating NTD concepts into curricula. This supports the conclusion that lack of a unifying framework (i.e., treating them as a distinct public health priority) explains poor recognition despite exposure to individual diseases.

260.Option: The low awareness among students may be due to the fact that NTDs are not covered in the clinical training phase of medical education, which limits their real-world exposure
Evidence: Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Only 9% stated that they had seen a case of NTDs.
260.Rationale: While the passage does show limited real-world exposure (only 9% had seen a case), it does not state that NTDs are omitted from clinical training nor provide evidence about the clinical-phase curriculum. In fact, it says most NTDs are included in the epidemiology course. Therefore there is insufficient direct evidence to attribute low awareness to absence of coverage during the clinical training phase specifically.

260.Option: The lack of emphasis on NTDs in the epidemiology course may stem from the absence of standardized national guidelines for teaching these diseases in medical education
Evidence: Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Hence, Egyptian medical and nursing curricula should be updated. NTDs concepts, epidemiology, and management in detail must be integrated in the medical and nursing curricula and practical training.
260.Rationale: The passage documents that the epidemiology course did not explicitly use the term 'NTDs' and recommends curriculum updates, but it does not mention national teaching standards or the presence/absence of standardized national guidelines. There is no direct evidence in the text linking the lack of emphasis to an absence of national guidelines, so this causal explanation is not supported by the passage.

260.Option: The limited exposure to NTDs in the epidemiology course may be due to the course's focus on high-profile diseases such as HIV and tuberculosis, which overshadow less common but endemic conditions
Evidence: Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

Although the Ministry of Health in Egypt applies control programs for NTDs by offering anti-helminthic drugs free of charge and deworming campaign, this study showed that only 5% of the included medical and nursing students knew specific activities for prevention and control of these disease in Egypt.
260.Rationale: The passage does not state that the epidemiology course prioritizes HIV, tuberculosis, or other high-profile diseases over NTDs, nor does it provide evidence that such overshadowing occurred. It actually notes that many NTDs are included in the course material but that the unifying term 'NTDs' was not used and that knowledge of control activities was very low. Thus the specific claim about focus on HIV/TB overshadowing NTDs is unsupported by the text.

261.Option: Perform the assay within 1 hour of drug addition to minimize the impact of time-dependent changes in parasite motility and drug metabolism
Evidence: The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that as was seen in Figs 2, 3 and 4 (Effect of Parasite Quantity), the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.
As shown in Fig 15, there is an instantaneous effect at 1min that remains with similar activity values at least for one hour for all the benznidazole concentrations.
261.Rationale: The option recommends a 1-hour window to control for time-dependent changes, but the passage indicates the Biospeckle signal is stable well beyond 1 hour (up to ~200 minutes) and that an instantaneous effect is observable at 1 minute and persists for at least an hour. Thus performing within 1 hour is neither necessary nor sufficient to control the key physical confounder highlighted in the paper (desiccation/evaporation). The passage emphasizes controlling desiccation/evaporation rather than arbitrarily limiting to 1 hour.

261.Option: Limit the assay duration to less than 200 minutes to prevent desiccation from altering drug concentration and confounding the interpretation of parasite activity
Evidence: The desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells and its implications on the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was evaluated in several ways.

The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that ... the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.
It can be assumed that desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.

We selected the following conditions: 100μL in the test well, parasites in exponential phase, 4x104 parasites per well, a maximum time of evaluation of the activity of the Biospeckle pattern of 204 minutes.
261.Rationale: The passage identifies desiccation (evaporation) of the liquid in air-exposed wells as a physical process that can alter drug concentration over time and explicitly notes stability of measurements only up to ~200 minutes. Limiting assay duration below this threshold is therefore a reasonable control to avoid evaporation-driven concentration changes that would confound interpretation of drug effects.

261.Option: Use a sealed chamber or humidified environment to prevent evaporation and maintain constant liquid volume throughout the assay
Evidence: The desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells and its implications on the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was evaluated in several ways.
The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes ...
It can be assumed that desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.
261.Rationale: The passage identifies desiccation/evaporation of the liquid in air-exposed wells as a key physical confounder that can concentrate the drug and alter measured parasite activity. Using a sealed chamber or humidified environment directly addresses this physical process by preventing evaporation and maintaining constant liquid volume, thereby ensuring observed changes are due to the drug rather than volume loss.

261.Option: Standardize the initial volume of liquid in each well to ensure consistent baseline conditions across all experimental groups
Evidence: Unless otherwise stated, all the assays were performed with an initial volume of 100μL.

With these dimensions and the approximate size of the parasite (length 20μm and width 5μm, mean 12.5μm), the object’s plane (surface disk containing the most exposed parasites) available for the Biospeckle technique has a mean height of 0.0125 mm, a mean volume of 1.66μL and contains approximately 670 parasites when the density is 4x105 parasites per mL (4x104 parasites per well).

These assays allowed the selection of the conditions of the method using VDRL plates. We selected the following conditions: 100μL in the test well, parasites in exponential phase, 4x104 parasites per well, a maximum time of evaluation of the activity of the Biospeckle pattern of 204 minutes.

Since the object’s plane has a constant volume of 1.66μL due to the parasite’s dimensions (length 20μm and width 5μm, mean 12.5μm), the percentage that it represents in the constant density experiment increases as the total volume of liquid in the well decreases.
261.Rationale: The paper repeatedly emphasizes using a standardized initial volume (100 μL) and shows that total volume affects the percentage of the object’s plane and the Biospeckle signal. Standardizing initial volume across wells controls this physical source of variability so differences in activity are attributable to the drug rather than baseline volume differences or related optical effects.

261.Option: Match the concentration of benznidazole across all wells to ensure that differences in activity are due to parasite viability rather than drug concentration variation
Evidence: Benznidazole was used as a trypanocidal reference drug. ... containing 7 different concentrations of benznidazole: 0, 1.95x10-2, 7.81 x10-2, 31.25 x10-2, 1.25, 5 and 20 μg/mL (ranging from 0.075 to 76.8μM) and at different times (1, 61, 128 and 204 minutes) a video was recorded ... and in each case the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was calculated.
It can be assumed that desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.
In this work the concentration of benznidazole is used in a range of 0.0195 to 20μg/mL (from 0.075 to 76.8μM) and the activity is detected in a minimum time of 1 minute.
261.Rationale: While ensuring known and controlled drug concentrations is essential experimental practice, the question asks which factor must be controlled to avoid a confounding physical process. The passage highlights evaporation/desiccation (which can change concentration over time) as that confounder. Simply matching intended starting concentrations across wells does not prevent evaporation-driven concentration changes during the assay. Moreover, the authors intentionally varied benznidazole concentrations to measure dose effects, so matching concentration is not the specific physical confounder emphasized in the text.

262.Option: Living in homes with earthen/mud floors, which reflects poor housing conditions and limits access to protective footwear
Evidence: A large percentage of the population (79.4%) stay in homes with earthen/mud floors [20].
Factors that have been reported to aggravate the problem include earthen and dusty floors, non regular use of closed footwear, common resting place outside the house and pigs on the compound [10].
Variables retained in the model apart from age are indicative of low economic status.
262.Rationale: The quoted text shows that earthen/mud floors are a structural housing condition strongly associated with tungiasis. However, the option frames this as reflecting an interplay between behavior and structure; in the passage earthen floors are presented primarily as a structural risk factor rather than an individual behavior. While earthen floors contribute to exposure risk and may indirectly affect behavior (e.g., increased contact with infested soil), they do not most directly capture the behavioral component the question asks for (individual practices shaped by structural constraints). Therefore this option is not the best single choice to represent the described interplay.

262.Option: Inconsistent use of closed footwear, driven by economic constraints and shaped by high child population density
Evidence: Factors that have been reported to aggravate the problem include earthen and dusty floors, non regular use of closed footwear, common resting place outside the house and pigs on the compound [10].
In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2).
A large percentage of the population (79.4%) stay in homes with earthen/mud floors [20].
It has a population of 554,622 with 45% of these being 14 year olds and below.
This can be attributed to the fact that they are more exposed due to them playing barefoot [10] and also getting reinfected at school in endemic areas.
262.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies 'non regular use of closed footwear' and 'occasional or not using closed footwear' as risk factors and links children (a large proportion of the population) to increased exposure because they play barefoot. Footwear use is a clear individual behavior that is directly shaped by structural/economic conditions (poverty, housing, population context) in the text. This combination most directly embodies the interplay between behavior and structure that increases vulnerability to persistent host-surface infestation and exposure dynamics described in the question, so this option is correct.

262.Option: Age ≤14 years, which reflects both developmental vulnerability and high exposure due to outdoor play in endemic areas
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2).
This is consistent with a previous study [5] which reported a high prevalence of tungiasis in children of this age group.
This can be attributed to the fact that they are more exposed due to them playing barefoot [10] and also getting reinfected at school in endemic areas.
262.Rationale: Age ≤14 clearly indicates a group with higher exposure and vulnerability, and the passage links this to behaviors such as playing barefoot. However, age itself is a demographic characteristic rather than a direct behavioral variable or structural condition. The question asks for the factor that most directly reflects the interplay between individual behavior and structural conditions; age is an important determinant of exposure but does not itself represent that behavioral–structural interplay as directly as footwear use does. Therefore this option is not the best single answer.

262.Option: Lack of electricity and lack of a toilet, which are indicators of low economic status and contribute to poor hygiene practices
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2). Others were being illiterate or low literacy level, bathing without soap, common resting place inside the house, having rats in the compound, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.
Variables that point to low economic status.
In the multivariate analysis only the age and the monthly income bracket of <1000 remained significant.
262.Rationale: Lack of electricity and toilets are clearly structural indicators of poverty cited in the passage and they plausibly contribute to hygiene-related behaviors. However, they are upstream structural determinants rather than the proximate, directly behavioral practices that determine exposure to the parasite on host surfaces. The question asks for the factor that most directly reflects the interplay between individual behavior and structural conditions; while lack of electricity/toilets are important contributors, they are less proximate than a specific protective behavior (e.g., footwear use) and thus are not the best single choice.

262.Option: Bathing without soap, which reflects poor hygiene and is associated with low literacy and lack of health education
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2). Others were being illiterate or low literacy level, bathing without soap, common resting place inside the house, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.
Questions abound as to if the action of washing up in a proper way may contribute in a way to the reduction of the occurrence or intensity of tungiasis infestation in humans.
Almost a half, 45% of the participants interviewed had no knowledge on its transmission.
being illiterate or having not completed primary school
262.Rationale: Bathing without soap is an individual hygiene behavior explicitly identified in the passage and linked to low literacy/knowledge about transmission. This captures behavioral aspects shaped by structural constraints (education, poverty). However, compared with inconsistent use of closed footwear, bathing without soap is less directly tied to the specific exposure pathway for tungiasis (contact of feet/hands with infested soil) described in the text. Because the question emphasizes vulnerability to persistence on host surfaces shaped by exposure dynamics, footwear use is the more direct behavioral–structural factor; bathing without soap is important but not the single most direct reflection of that interplay in this study.

263.Option: The delayed peak in dengue cases is caused by a lag in public health reporting, with more cases being diagnosed and recorded in October due to increased surveillance during the peak transmission season
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).
In Hanoi dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall.
Our study is limited by its reliance on passively notified cases of clinically apparent dengue infection.
If the size of the 2006–2008 outbreaks were inflated by the reporting of an unusually large number of non-dengue illnesses, the increase in dengue incidence we have identified may be false.
263.Rationale: Although the authors note limitations of passive surveillance and acknowledge reporting artifacts could affect trends, the passage explicitly links the timing of the seasonal peak (July start, October peak) to wet and hot periods and climate-driven vector dynamics with an observed lag. That provides a biological explanation for the late-summer/early-autumn peak rather than attributing it to a reporting lag. Thus the reporting-lag hypothesis is not the most likely explanation for the observed seasonal peak.

263.Option: The peak in dengue cases in October is due to increased susceptibility among children who return to school after summer break, leading to higher transmission in densely populated urban areas
Evidence: The majority of notified cases (85%) occurred in individuals aged over 15 years (figure 2A).
