Total cell counts of ... eosinophils ... in the thoracic cavity were not different between the two mouse strains (Fig 1D).
Total number of skin cells (A), eosinophils (B), neutrophils (C), and macrophages (D) ... 3h after intradermal L. sigmodontis L3 or PBS injection.
315.Rationale: The option attributes the protective effect in S100A8/A9-/- mice to eosinophils and increased lung mucus. The passage provides stronger evidence that neutrophils mediate protection: neutrophil depletion restored worm counts in knockouts and in vitro knockout neutrophils impaired L3 motility. By contrast, eosinophil counts were generally comparable between genotypes in the thoracic cavity/BAL, and S100A8/A9-/- mice actually showed a trend toward fewer goblet-cell–positive bronchi (mucus) rather than increased mucus. Therefore the evidence supports neutrophil-mediated protection rather than an eosinophil/mucus-mediated mechanism.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 acts as a systemic immunosuppressant that dampens all innate immune responses, leading to increased larval survival in wild-type mice
Evidence: Our data indicates an anti-inflammatory role of S100A8/S100A9 on neutrophil activation, which reduced inflammatory responses and facilitated the migration of infective L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.
S100A8/A9 inhibits L3-induced inflammation by decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation.

Given that the phenotype of the S100A8/A9-/- mice with a reduced worm burden was abolished following neutrophil depletion and S100A8/A9 deficient neutrophils were shown to impair L3 motility more efficiently in vitro, it can be assumed that neutrophils are involved in the enhanced clearance of L3 larvae in S100A8/A9 deficient mice.
Thus, the impact of S100A8/A9 may differ depending on the inflammatory milieu and location [2,3].
315.Rationale: While the passage supports that S100A8/A9 has an anti-inflammatory effect—specifically decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation—it does not support the sweeping claim that it is a systemic immunosuppressant that dampens all innate responses. The text emphasizes effects on neutrophils and notes that the impact may differ by tissue and context. The protection phenotype is neutrophil-dependent rather than reflecting a universal suppression of all innate immunity. Therefore the statement is overly broad and not supported as written.

315.Option: S100A8/A9 modulates neutrophil activation to balance protective immunity and excessive inflammation during early larval migration
Evidence: Our data indicates an anti-inflammatory role of S100A8/S100A9 on neutrophil activation, which reduced inflammatory responses and facilitated the migration of infective L3 larvae into the thoracic cavity.

S100A8/A9-/- mice were characterized by an increased number of thoracic cavity cells and elevated concentrations of neutrophil-associated chemokines such as CXCL-1, CXCL-2, CXCL-5, and elastase, which may contribute to the elimination of L. sigmodontis in S100A8/A9-/- mice.

Bone marrow-derived neutrophils of S100A8/A9-/- mice cultured in the presence of L3 larvae exhibited an increased MHCII expression compared to neutrophils of WT mice (Fig 4C). This increased activation of S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils was further associated with an increased apoptosis...

L3 larvae cultured with S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils showed an earlier inhibition of L3 motility compared to L3 larvae cultured with neutrophils of WT mice (Fig 4E). Addition of DNase abrogated the inhibitory effect of S100A8/A9-/- neutrophils on L3 motility, suggesting that the effect is DNA trap-dependent.

Depletion of neutrophils led to a significantly increased worm recovery in S100A8/A9-/- mice compared to isotype-treated S100A8/A9-/- controls, resulting in a worm burden that was comparable to isotype-treated WT controls (Fig 5).
S100A8/A9 inhibits L3-induced inflammation by decreasing chemokine production and neutrophil activation.
315.Rationale: The passage consistently shows that S100A8/A9 influences neutrophil activity: its presence dampens chemokine production and neutrophil activation (an anti-inflammatory effect) and facilitates larval migration, whereas deficiency results in heightened neutrophil activation, increased neutrophil-associated chemokines and enzymes, enhanced neutrophil-mediated inhibition of larval motility, and reduced worm burden. Neutrophil depletion reverses the protective phenotype in knockouts. These findings support the interpretation that S100A8/A9 modulates neutrophil activation to strike a balance between protection (clearance by activated neutrophils) and limiting excessive inflammation that might otherwise occur during early larval migration.

316.Option: The lack of detectable viremia indicates that T-705 may have induced a sterilizing immunity, preventing any viral replication from occurring.
Evidence: All animals of the treatment group survived the infection and showed no signs of disease. Virus was detected neither in blood nor in the organs throughout the observation period (Fig. 6 and data not shown).

To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.
T-705 (favipiravir) is currently in late stage clinical development for the treatment of influenza virus infection.

Following conversion to T-705-ribofuranosyl-5′-triphosphate, it presumably acts as a nucleotide analog that selectively inhibits the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase or causes lethal mutagenesis upon incorporation into the virus RNA [36]–[40].
If given immediately post-exposure, the drug suppresses virus replication below the level required to elicit antibodies.
316.Rationale: The passage attributes the absence of detectable virus to potent drug-mediated suppression of replication (including inhibition or lethal mutagenesis), not to the host mounting sterilizing immune protection. The authors explicitly state that post-exposure T-705 suppressed replication below the level needed to elicit antibodies, which argues for pharmacologic suppression rather than sterilizing immunity driven by the host immune response.

316.Option: The absence of detectable viremia reflects complete suppression of viral replication below the threshold for immune activation, not a failure of infection or treatment
Evidence: All animals of the treatment group survived the infection and showed no signs of disease. Virus was detected neither in blood nor in the organs throughout the observation period (Fig. 6 and data not shown).

To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.
If given immediately post-exposure, the drug suppresses virus replication below the level required to elicit antibodies.

Following conversion to T-705-ribofuranosyl-5′-triphosphate, it presumably acts as a nucleotide analog that selectively inhibits the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase or causes lethal mutagenesis upon incorporation into the virus RNA [36]–[40].
316.Rationale: The passage directly supports this interpretation: treated animals had no detectable virus, and antibody testing showed that post-exposure treatment often prevented an antibody response, which the authors interpret as suppression of replication below the level needed for immune activation. The described mechanism of T-705 (polymerase inhibition/lethal mutagenesis) is consistent with strong pharmacologic suppression rather than lack of infection.

316.Option: The lack of detectable viremia may indicate that the animals were not truly infected, suggesting a flaw in the infection protocol or inoculation dose.
Evidence: To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.
Before testing antivirals in the IFNAR−/− mouse model, we aimed at determining the optimal infection dose for CCHFV strain Afg09-2990 and characterizing the disease caused by this particular strain.

Animals died from the infection even after inoculation with 0.3 FFU (inoculum, died/infected: 0.3 FFU, 4/5; 1 FFU, 5/5; 3 FFU, 4/5; 10 FFU, 6/8; 100 FFU 13/13; 1,000 FFU, 8/8; 10,000 FFU, 3/3). This indicates that only a few infectious virus particles of CCHFV strain Afg09-2990 are sufficient to initiate a productive infection.
316.Rationale: The study validated the infection model and demonstrated that very low inocula reliably produce infection and disease in IFNAR−/− mice. The authors explicitly measured antibodies to confirm infection in treated groups and interpreted low seroconversion as drug-mediated suppression of replication rather than failure to infect. Therefore, absence of viremia is better explained by potent antiviral effect than by a flawed inoculation.

316.Option: The lack of detectable viremia suggests that the high dose of T-705 may have caused immune suppression, preventing the detection of viral antigens.
Evidence: Following conversion to T-705-ribofuranosyl-5′-triphosphate, it presumably acts as a nucleotide analog that selectively inhibits the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase or causes lethal mutagenesis upon incorporation into the virus RNA [36]–[40].

All animals of the treatment group survived the infection and showed no signs of disease. Virus was detected neither in blood nor in the organs throughout the observation period (Fig. 6 and data not shown).

To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.
316.Rationale: There is no evidence in the passage that T-705 causes immune suppression. Instead, the authors attribute the absence of detectable virus to direct antiviral activity (polymerase inhibition/lethal mutagenesis) that suppressed replication below detection and, in some regimens, below the threshold to elicit antibodies. The data and interpretations support pharmacologic suppression rather than drug-induced immunosuppression preventing antigen detection.

316.Option: The lack of detectable viremia may be due to the timing of sample collection, as viral load may have been transiently suppressed and later rebounded.
Evidence: Virus was detected neither in blood nor in the organs throughout the observation period (Fig. 6 and data not shown).
Blood samples of 30–80 µl per animal were drawn by tail vein puncture in intervals of 1–4 days over a period of 14 days (≤5 blood drawings in total) for clinical chemistry and viremia measurement.

To provide evidence for infection of the animals in the T-705 treatment groups, the development of CCHFV-specific antibodies was measured 21 days p.i. Only 1/10 (10%) of the animals treated post-exposure, but 10/10 (100%) of the animals treated from day 1 or 2 p.i. developed antibodies, indicating that virus replication under post-exposure treatment with T-705 was even not sufficient to elicit antibodies.
316.Rationale: The study included repeated blood and organ sampling over a two-week observation period and explicitly reports that virus was undetectable throughout that period. In addition, antibody testing at day 21 was used to detect prior replication; the near-absence of seroconversion in the post-exposure treated group supports the authors' conclusion that replication was suppressed rather than transiently missed and then rebounded. Thus timing-of-sampling rebound is not the favored explanation in this dataset.

317.Option: Parasite-induced increases in vector biting rates enhance transmission while reducing vector fitness, creating a stable equilibrium in natural populations
Evidence: Perhaps the most elegant demonstration that the interests of parasite and vector are not entirely aligned is parasite-induced increases in biting rates in mosquitoes [5–9], sand flies [10] and tsetse flies [11,12]—this is likely to increase transmission (and thereby fitness) of the parasite while the vector is liable to suffer a reduction in fitness due to excessive energy expenditure and increased risk of mortality when attempting to bite.
317.Rationale: The passage documents parasite-induced increases in biting rates as an example of a parasite trait that raises transmission while harming vector fitness, but it does not present this mechanism as the most critical factor determining the balance between parasite fitness and vector survival in natural settings. The study's primary focus and strongest evidence concern temperature-dependent effects on parasite growth and vector mortality. Thus, while increased biting rates can shift the balance, the passage does not support the claim that this mechanism is the single most critical factor producing a stable equilibrium in natural populations.

317.Option: The duration of the vector's feeding cycle determines the window for parasite transmission, thereby balancing parasite fitness and vector survival
Evidence: Trypanosoma cruzi has been reported to take approximately up to month at 28°C to colonize the intestine, reach the rectum and differentiate into infective stages [57, 58]. As triatomines will only feed again after they have moulted, it would benefit the parasite if their moult, and thus the next bloodmeal, were delayed until such a time as the parasite is in the right place and life stage to be transmitted to a vertebrate host.
317.Rationale: The passage notes that timing of moult and subsequent feeding can influence transmission opportunity and that parasite-induced moulting delays could benefit parasite fitness. However, this is treated as one adaptive explanation among others rather than the central determinant. The strongest and repeatedly emphasized determinant in the study is temperature-dependent parasite growth relative to vector thermal biology; feeding-cycle duration is relevant but not presented as the most critical factor shaping the trade-off in natural settings.

317.Option: The presence of gut symbionts such as Rhodococcus rhodnii modulates parasite-induced virulence by competing for nutritional resources in the vector's intestine
Evidence: Previous studies showed no effect of T. cruzi on the development of Nocardia sp. and Rhodococcus rhodnii, gut symbionts of Triatoma infestans and Rhodnius prolixus, respectively [25].

As the parasite does not invade the insects’ body but develops rather in its intestine, we might expect effects on the insects’ fitness to be marginal. ... However, as a consequence of living only in the insect intestinal tract, T. cruzi probably competes with its host for nutritional resources.
317.Rationale: The passage mentions gut symbionts and competition for resources, but specifically reports previous studies showing no effect of T. cruzi on those gut symbionts. The dominant evidence in this paper highlights temperature-dependent parasite growth and associated vector mortality as the critical influence. The passage does not provide evidence that gut symbionts like Rhodococcus rhodnii are the primary modulators of parasite-induced virulence in natural settings.

317.Option: The parasite's ability to persist in the vector over long periods ensures sustained transmission, thereby minimizing harm to the vector and maintaining fitness balance
Evidence: At this range of temperatures, the parasite has a high in vitro growth rate (Fig. 2) so we hypothesize that its strategy in the vector is close to unrestrained growth, trading off an increased chance of transmission (due to a high population density in the intestine) with the cost of killing its vector and so effecting zero transmission.
In fact, it has already been demonstrated that triatomines can eliminate T. cruzi infections after long periods of starvation [39].
317.Rationale: The passage contradicts the assertion that long persistence necessarily minimizes harm. Instead, it describes scenarios where high parasite growth (particularly at temperatures preferred by the vector) can harm or even kill the vector, reducing transmission. It also notes that infections can be lost under starvation. Thus persistence does not inherently minimize vector harm or ensure balance; temperature-dependent growth and resultant trade-offs are emphasized as the critical determinant.

317.Option: Temperature-dependent parasite growth rate relative to vector thermal preference determines the trade-off between transmission success and vector survival
Evidence: Temperature is a factor of particular importance in host-parasite interactions, especially when the host is ectothermic.
We therefore chose to conduct our study under four thermal regimes, and to use a comparatively narrow range of temperatures to keep the test conservative.
The population growth of T. cruzi in vitro increased consistently with increasing temperature (Fig. 2; p<0.0001 for temperature effect).

This accelerated mortality of R. prolixus infected with T. cruzi, under conditions of starvation (commonly experienced by these insects—[28]), was seen over a narrow range of temperatures (i.e., at 24 and 27°C but not at 21 or 30°C).
R. prolixus prefers temperatures of 25.0–25.4°C when offered a choice and performs best around these temperatures [40].

At this range of temperatures, the parasite has a high in vitro growth rate (Fig. 2) so we hypothesize that its strategy in the vector is close to unrestrained growth, trading off an increased chance of transmission (due to a high population density in the intestine) with the cost of killing its vector and so effecting zero transmission.
317.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies temperature as the key factor shaping the interaction: parasite growth increases with temperature; infected-vector mortality is accelerated at temperatures (24–27°C) near the vector's thermal preference (~25°C); and the authors explicitly frame a trade-off where high parasite growth at the vector's preferred temperatures enhances transmission potential but increases vector mortality. Collectively, these lines make temperature-dependent parasite growth relative to vector thermal biology the most critical determinant of the balance between parasite fitness and vector survival in natural settings.

318.Option: A genetic variant in the chitotriosidase gene leading to reduced enzyme activity
Evidence: Polymorphisms in the genes for these chitinases have been described that are associated with either increased or decreased enzyme activity [12,14–17].
In the gene encoding for chitotriosidase, a 24-bp insertion in exon 10 was described previously [12, 14, 19, 20].
Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).

Chitinases hydrolyze chitin, which is the main component of the fungal cell wall. Decreased chitinase activity could, therefore, result in an enhanced susceptibility towards infections by chitin bearing fungi.
318.Rationale: The passage links chitotriosidase polymorphisms to altered chitinase activity and an increased risk of mycetoma, but it does not describe chitotriosidase variants as indicators of an inherited defect of phagocyte oxidative burst (i.e., chronic granulomatous disease caused by NADPH oxidase deficiency). The chitotriosidase defect pertains to chitin degradation by innate enzymes, not the phagocyte respiratory burst machinery responsible for oxidative microbial killing; therefore this option does not correctly identify the host factor asked for.

318.Option: The presence of chitin in the fungal grain, which triggers a host immune response involving chitinases
Evidence: Since chitin is present in the M. mycetomatis grain and that two human chitinases are found in the vicinity of this grain, in reaction to exposure to M. mycetomatis mycetoma.
Chitinases are part of the innate immune system and, in general, their production by macrophages and other cells is upregulated by the host’s tissue exposure to chitin.
Chitinases hydrolyze chitin, which is the main component of the fungal cell wall.
318.Rationale: The presence of chitin is an environmental/pathogen feature that elicits a chitinase-mediated innate response; it does not indicate an inherited disorder of phagocyte oxidative burst. The question asks for a host factor that would point toward an inherited oxidative-burst defect (e.g., NADPH oxidase deficiency), whereas chitin in the grain reflects the pathogen substrate that induces chitinases, not a host genetic defect in oxidative killing.

318.Option: A polymorphism in the AMCase gene that results in decreased enzyme activity
Evidence: Neither the A50G and the A290G SNP, nor the 10-bp insertion in the 5’UTR region of AMCase is associated with invasive mycetoma
Allele frequencies of A50G and A290G in the patients were similar to those in the controls (p = 0.65; odds ratio 1.2; 95% CI 0.6–2.1; and p = 0.72; odds ratio 1.1; 95% CI 0.7–1.8, respectively).
Furthermore, no difference was found in the presence of the 10-bp insertion between patients and controls (p = 0.72; odds ratio 1.1; 95% CI 0.7–1.8).
318.Rationale: The passage explicitly states AMCase polymorphisms studied were not associated with risk of mycetoma. More importantly, AMCase (an endochitinase) activity relates to chitin processing and immune modulation, not to the phagocyte oxidative burst machinery. Thus an AMCase polymorphism would not be an indicator of an inherited disorder impairing oxidative burst.

318.Option: A Th2-polarized immune response that results in increased production of matrix metalloproteinases and tissue inhibitor of MMP-1
Evidence: The development of mycetoma is associated with a Th2 response.

Based on immunohistochemistry studies in various mycetoma causative agents, it appeared that the cytokine pattern surrounding the mycetoma grain is a Th2 response. IL-10 and IL-4, both Th2-associated cytokines, were highly expressed around the fungal grain [7, 26, 27].
Sandler et al [28] showed that mice with a Th2 response have increased expression of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and of tissue inhibitor of MMP-1 (TIMP-1).
318.Rationale: A Th2-polarized response is an acquired/host immune polarization state associated with mycetoma pathology in this study, including MMP/TIMP changes; it is not diagnostic of an inherited defect in the phagocyte oxidative burst (chronic granulomatous disease). CGD is a specific genetic deficiency of the respiratory burst in phagocytes, whereas Th2 polarization describes cytokine milieu and tissue remodeling, so this option does not correctly answer the question.

318.Option: A history of schistosomiasis, which is associated with a Th2-polarized immune response
Evidence: it is not clear why only a minority of the exposed humans develop overt clinical infection. Several environmental and patient-related factors have been described to influence the risk for the development of mycetoma, including concurrent schistosomiasis [5].
Indirect evidence for a Th2 response was also found in the association between schistosomiasis, associated with a Th2 response, and eumycetoma [5].
318.Rationale: Schistosomiasis is an acquired parasitic infection that skews the immune system toward a Th2 profile and is cited as a risk factor for mycetoma in this study; it is not an inherited defect of phagocyte oxidative burst. Therefore a history of schistosomiasis would not indicate the genetic, NADPH-oxidase–related disorder the question describes (chronic granulomatous disease).

319.Option: The high prevalence of trachoma in Njoge, where most positive environmental samples were found, indicates that a single dominant transmission route is responsible for sustained disease in hyperendemic areas
Evidence: The majority of our positive environment swabs came from the one village, Njoge, with the highest prevalence of trachoma.

This difference suggests that the practices of children and caretakers in Njoge may contribute to diverse environmental presence of Chlamydia, which in turn may be contributing to the high levels of trachoma.

flies seem to play a larger role in transmission in areas of low prevalence, like The Gambia, but not in areas of high prevalence [14, 18]. This difference has been attributed to the multiple routes of transmission in hyperendemic areas such that elimination of any one source has minimal effect on transmission [14].
319.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that most positive swabs came from Njoge but interprets that finding as evidence for diverse environmental presence and local practices contributing to transmission, not as evidence of a single dominant route. The authors also state that in hyperendemic areas multiple routes of transmission operate such that removing one source has minimal effect. Therefore the option asserting a single dominant route is not supported.

319.Option: The fact that all positive environmental samples were collected from households with children who had active trachoma suggests that environmental contamination is a consequence of infection rather than a driver of transmission
Evidence: Obtaining at least one positive environmental swab from the household was associated with village of residence (ranging from 0% in Mkutani and Banyi Banyi to 34% in Njoge, p = 0.011) and presence of ocular infection in the index child (percent positive swabs 34% vs. 3%, p = 0.001) (Table 4).
The findings do not mean that the environmental source contained live organisms capable of transmission, as we did not test for replicating organisms or load.
it does suggest the presence at one point of a possible environmental source of infection, and these may have the potential to contribute to disease transmission.
319.Rationale: While positive environmental swabs were strongly associated with presence of ocular infection in the index child, the study design intentionally sampled households of children with active trachoma, so positives necessarily came from such households. The authors explicitly caution that presence of DNA does not prove viable organisms or directionality (consequence versus driver). They also state environmental sources 'may have the potential to contribute to disease transmission,' so the evidence does not support the definitive causal interpretation proposed in the option.

319.Option: The presence of C. trachomatis DNA on household furniture and children’s hands indicates that flies are the primary vector for transmission in hyperendemic areas
Evidence: Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1). Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%).

In studies of the contribution of flies to transmission of trachoma and infection, flies seem to play a larger role in transmission in areas of low prevalence, like The Gambia, but not in areas of high prevalence [14, 18].
319.Rationale: The presence of DNA on furniture and hands demonstrates environmental contamination but does not implicate flies as the primary vector. The passage explicitly states flies tend to be more important in low-prevalence settings and not in high-prevalence (hyperendemic) areas; the authors argue multiple routes operate in hyperendemic settings. Thus the evidence contradicts the claim that flies are the primary vector here.

319.Option: The presence of C. trachomatis DNA on both children's hands and household furniture, combined with uneven environmental contamination across villages despite similar infection rates, suggests multiple transmission pathways are operating in hyperendemic areas
Evidence: Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1). Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%).
The 23 positive environmental swabs were obtained from 13 households (Table 3). Twenty-one of the positive swabs, collected from 11 households, were from the same village, Njoge.

This difference suggests that the practices of children and caretakers in Njoge may contribute to diverse environmental presence of Chlamydia, which in turn may be contributing to the high levels of trachoma.

flies seem to play a larger role in transmission in areas of low prevalence, like The Gambia, but not in areas of high prevalence [14, 18]. This difference has been attributed to the multiple routes of transmission in hyperendemic areas such that elimination of any one source has minimal effect on transmission [14].
319.Rationale: The combination of (a) detection of chlamydial DNA on multiple types of household sites including hands and furniture, and (b) marked heterogeneity in environmental contamination across villages (most positives clustered in Njoge) despite comparable infection rates, supports the interpretation that transmission in hyperendemic settings is not driven by a single dominant route but by multiple, overlapping pathways influenced by local behaviors and contexts. The authors explicitly make this point.

319.Option: The detection of C. trachomatis DNA on children's hands and household furniture suggests that direct person-to-person transmission is the primary mechanism, with environmental surfaces playing a minor role
Evidence: Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1). Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%).

These findings suggest that behavior change communication around hygiene may need to be enlarged to focus on keeping ocular and nasal secretions off faces and hands, and promoting washing of clothing as well.
319.Rationale: The detection of DNA on hands and surfaces indicates that environmental/fomite pathways could be important; the authors recommend expanded hygiene measures (washing clothing, keeping secretions off hands) indicating surfaces may play a meaningful role. They do not conclude direct person-to-person transmission is primary with surfaces minor; rather, they highlight multiple possible transmission avenues and potential importance of environmental contamination.

320.Option: Increase sampling frequency at all locations to compensate for delays in clinical reporting and ensure detection of asymptomatic cases regardless of flow dynamics
Evidence: ES offers an anonymous, non-invasive approach to monitor pathogen circulation in a population that does not rely on clinical testing availability and behavior and can be less expensive than many other forms of clinical surveillance [4,8].

some individuals infected with S. Typhi are likely to be missed by clinical surveillance methodologies, as some proportion of infected individuals are known to become asymptomatic, chronic carriers, capable of shedding large numbers of bacteria into the environment, but unlikely to seek medical care or testing [12–14].

The sensitivity of sampling at Branch 573 may be increased by increasing the sampling frequency or increasing the number of samples collected at each sampling event (so as to increase total sampling volume). However, these options may be challenging and expensive, and adding one or two upstream sampling sites to replace Branch 573 that will still capture most of the catchment area may be a preferable solution.
320.Rationale: The passage supports the general idea that increasing sampling frequency or sample volume can improve detection sensitivity (and that ES can capture asymptomatic shedders when clinical surveillance is limited). However, it explicitly cautions that increasing frequency at all locations is costly and may be less effective than strategically selecting sites (for example adding upstream sites) to maximize detection. Thus the blanket recommendation to increase frequency at all locations is not supported by the model-based guidance in the passage.

320.Option: Conduct sampling at downstream convergence points during early morning hours to capture the cumulative shedding from the entire catchment area before dilution increases
Evidence: The highest probabilities of detection are concentrated in upstream branches that do not have ‘parent’ branches, and the lowest probabilities of detection are concentrated in downstream branches with large catchment populations, where larger volumes of wastewater flow and environmental flow converge (Fig 4).

For this modeled scenario, the mean probability of detection across all sections of all branches at all hours was 0.56, with the lowest mean probability occurring at 05:00 (0.48) and the highest mean probability occurring at 12:00 (0.60).

a negative sampling result at downstream branches such as 573 and 815 does not indicate that there are no infected persons in the wastewater network, and infected persons are likely to be missed by environmental surveillance if these sites are relied upon. In this case, sampling further upstream is more likely to yield positive sampling results.
320.Rationale: The passage shows downstream convergence points tend to have lower detection probability because of dilution and decay, and explicitly warns that negative results at downstream sites do not rule out infections. The modeled data show lowest mean detection around 05:00 (not necessarily early morning as optimal) and highest around midday. Therefore the recommendation to sample at downstream convergence points in the early morning to capture cumulative shedding is contradicted by the model findings.

320.Option: Use a uniform sampling strategy across all branches and times to ensure equal representation of the population and avoid bias from location-specific detection probabilities
Evidence: When developing an ES sampling plan, the identification of optimal sampling locations and sampling times that maximize the probability of detection while being logistically feasible presents a challenge [15].

The highest probabilities of detection are concentrated in upstream branches that do not have ‘parent’ branches, and the lowest probabilities of detection are concentrated in downstream branches with large catchment populations, where larger volumes of wastewater flow and environmental flow converge (Fig 4).

In scenarios with low prevalence, detection probabilities remain low throughout the system, even across different flow scenarios… In these low prevalence scenarios, large environmental surveillance wastewater channels may be unlikely to yield positive detections and sampling further upstream, at shared toilets or the building level, may be warranted
This model provides a framework to begin answering these questions and a foundation to build upon to produce more specific models on a community-by-community basis.
320.Rationale: The passage emphasizes heterogeneity in detection probability across branches and times and recommends tailoring site selection and timing (e.g., upstream vs downstream, time of day) to maximize detection and feasibility. A uniform strategy ignores modeled spatial and temporal variation and is therefore not supported by the passage's recommendations.

320.Option: Prioritize sampling at upstream branches during midday hours to maximize detection of asymptomatic shedding and reduce delays in identifying transmission
Evidence: The highest probabilities of detection are concentrated in upstream branches that do not have ‘parent’ branches, and the lowest probabilities of detection are concentrated in downstream branches with large catchment populations, where larger volumes of wastewater flow and environmental flow converge (Fig 4).

For this modeled scenario, the mean probability of detection across all sections of all branches at all hours was 0.56, with the lowest mean probability occurring at 05:00 (0.48) and the highest mean probability occurring at 12:00 (0.60).
Branch 850: Section 0 at Hour 12; Branch 912: Section 0 at Hour 11
Branch 850, Section 0 and Branch 912, Section 0 have a peak probability of detection at 12:00 and 11:00, respectively, but the detection probability remains above 50% for the remainder of the day.

some individuals infected with S. Typhi are likely to be missed by clinical surveillance methodologies, as some proportion of infected individuals are known to become asymptomatic, chronic carriers, capable of shedding large numbers of bacteria into the environment, but unlikely to seek medical care or testing [12–14].
320.Rationale: The passage finds upstream branches often have higher detection probabilities (especially when prevalence is low) and identifies midday hours (around 11–12:00) as times with higher detection in the applied scenario. It also notes ES can detect asymptomatic shedders missed by clinical surveillance. Prioritizing upstream sampling during midday aligns with model outputs to maximize detection and helps mitigate delays/under-ascertainment from limited clinical reporting.

320.Option: Schedule sampling at the end of the day to align with peak wastewater flow and maximize the chance of detecting asymptomatic shedding
Evidence: For this modeled scenario, the mean probability of detection across all sections of all branches at all hours was 0.56, with the lowest mean probability occurring at 05:00 (0.48) and the highest mean probability occurring at 12:00 (0.60).

Branch 937, Section 0 appears to have peak detection probability in the middle of the night (between 23:00 and 02:00). However, detection probabilities greater than 0.5 are also indicated in the late afternoon and early evening (after 16:00) (Fig 5B).

The temporal and spatial autocorrelation of the sampling probability along each branch and throughout the day suggest that a sampling field team would be able to utilize sections near the optimal section and sample at hours within four hours of the optimal hour and achieve a probability of detection similar to the maximum modeled detection probability.
320.Rationale: The passage does not identify 'end of day' as a universal peak time; optimal times vary by branch (midday peaks in some branches, midnight peaks in others). The model highlights branch- and time-specific dynamics and recommends choosing times near the modeled optima (within a few hours), rather than a blanket end-of-day schedule. Therefore scheduling universally at the end of day is not supported.

321.Option: Higher expression of CD10 on neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: We recently showed that in Northwest Ethiopia, 78.1% of VL/HIV patients experienced at least one episode of VL relapse over a period of three years. Importantly, we showed that despite clinical cure, VL/HIV patients maintained high parasite loads, failed to restore both CD4+ T cell counts and antigen-specific IFNγ production and maintain higher expression of PD1 on CD4+ and CD8+ T cells.
CD10 expression on neutrophils was also significantly higher in VL/HIV patients who did not relapse over time (Fig 3B).

CD10 is significantly lower in both groups at ToD as compared to controls (p<0.0001, Table 5). CD10 expression increased at EoT in VL patients and was restored at 3m (Fig 3A, Table 5). In contrast, in VL/HIV patients, CD10 levels remained similar at EoT, and only increased marginally over time. CD10 expression on neutrophils was also significantly higher in VL/HIV patients who did not relapse over time (Fig 3B).
321.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that CD10 expression on neutrophils was significantly higher in VL/HIV patients who did not relapse, indicating that post-treatment CD10 distinguishes those with better clinical trajectory from those who relapse despite clinical cure. The first quote establishes that many VL/HIV patients relapse and maintain high parasite loads despite clinical cure; the subsequent quotes directly link higher CD10 post-treatment with non-relapse, supporting this option as the best discriminator among those listed.

321.Option: Higher production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: The ability of neutrophils to produce ROS was similar (p = 0.1777) in VL/HIV patients who relapse (206464 [119571]) and those who didn’t relapse during follow-up (227781 [155669–259241]).
Whereas ROS production in response to pyocyanin was impaired at ToD in both VL and VL/HIV patients, it was restored faster in VL/HIV patients.
321.Rationale: The passage reports no significant difference in ROS production between VL/HIV patients who relapsed and those who did not (p = 0.1777), so higher ROS post-treatment does not distinguish reduced relapse risk. Although ROS dynamics are discussed, the data show similarity between outcome groups, contradicting the option.

321.Option: Higher phagocytic capacity of neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: This was restored to levels similar to those of controls at EoT in VL patients, but remained impaired at all time points in VL/HIV patients (Table 7). In the latter group, the ability of neutrophils to phagocytose bioparticle was similarly impaired in patients who relapse and those who didn’t during follow-up (Fig 5B, Table 7).
As shown in Fig 5, at ToD phagocytosis was significantly impaired in VL and VL/HIV patients (p = 0.0006 and p = 0.0003, respectively, Table 7).
321.Rationale: The passage states that phagocytic capacity remained impaired in VL/HIV patients at all time points and that phagocytosis was similarly impaired in patients who relapsed and those who did not. Therefore, higher phagocytic capacity post-treatment does not distinguish relapse risk in this cohort.

321.Option: Higher expression of CD62L on neutrophils post-treatment is associated with reduced risk of relapse in VL/HIV patients despite clinical cure
Evidence: There were no significant differences (p = 0.1750) in CD62L MFI between VL/HIV patients who relapsed (13380 [7986–17068]) and those who didn’t (10022 [7155–13582]).

CD62L MFI were lower on neutrophils from VL and VL/HIV patients as compared to controls (Fig 2B; p = 0.0003 and p = 0.0016, respectively, Table 3) at ToD; CD62L MFI were restored to levels similar to those of controls at EoT in both groups (Fig 2B, Table 3).
321.Rationale: The passage reports no significant difference in CD62L expression between relapsers and non-relapsers (p = 0.1750). Although CD62L is altered at diagnosis and restored after treatment, it does not discriminate relapse outcome in VL/HIV patients, so this option is not supported.

322.Option: Antigenic divergence in non-lethal venom components, particularly PLA2 and β-cardiotoxin, likely prevents effective neutralization despite strong immunoreactivity to neurotoxins
Evidence: O. hannah venom represents the only venom that could not be neutralized by FNAV in current study, although FNAV did cross-reacted intensely with the major lethal toxins (neurotoxins, which formed 50% of proteome based on our venom proteome and immunoprofiling analyses as shown in Figs 3 and 6 and S5 Table).

For HPLC-fractionated O. hannah venom, NTX (fractions 9–18), CRISP (fraction 32, 35) and SVMP (fraction 33, 35, 38, 40, 42–44) immunoreacted strongly toward FNAV, whereas those in fractions 19 (band 3), 20, 21 (band 2), 22, 23, 28 and 29 were weakly detected, or not detected, by Western blotting (Fig 6 and Table 2). The major protein in these fractions with lower immunereactivity was identified as β-cardiotoxin, a member of the CTX protein family (S5 Table).
Moreover, the major proteins in fractions 19, 21 and 27, identified as PLA2 family proteins, also showed poor immunoreactivity to FNAV.
This analysis revealed that the sequence similarity is quite low between O. Hannah PLA2 and N. atra PLA2 (64%) or between O. Hannah PLA2 and B. multicinctus PLA2 (59%).
322.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents that FNAV strongly cross-reacts with O. hannah neurotoxins yet FNAV failed to neutralize O. hannah venom. It also identifies specific O. hannah components (β-cardiotoxin and PLA2 fractions) that were weakly or not recognized by FNAV and reports low sequence similarity for O. hannah PLA2 compared with the immunogen venoms. These statements support antigenic divergence (sequence and immunoreactivity differences) in those non-neurotoxin components as a plausible explanation for why immunoreactivity to some proteins (neurotoxins) does not translate into overall neutralization: key toxin components (here PLA2 and β-cardiotoxin) are antigenically different and poorly recognized, reducing neutralizing capacity despite strong binding to neurotoxins.

322.Option: The lack of neutralization is due to the high concentration of non-neutralized neurotoxins in O. hannah venom, which overwhelms the antivenom's binding capacity despite strong immunoreactivity
Evidence: O. hannah venom represents the only venom that could not be neutralized by FNAV in current study, although FNAV did cross-reacted intensely with the major lethal toxins (neurotoxins, which formed 50% of proteome based on our venom proteome and immunoprofiling analyses as shown in Figs 3 and 6 and S5 Table).

For HPLC-fractionated O. hannah venom, NTX (fractions 9–18), CRISP (fraction 32, 35) and SVMP (fraction 33, 35, 38, 40, 42–44) immunoreacted strongly toward FNAV, whereas those in fractions 19 (band 3), 20, 21 (band 2), 22, 23, 28 and 29 were weakly detected, or not detected, by Western blotting (Fig 6 and Table 2).
322.Rationale: The option posits that an excess of neurotoxins overwhelms binding capacity. The passage, however, states that FNAV "cross-reacted intensely" with the neurotoxins (which constitute ~50% of the proteome), implying binding to those neurotoxins is strong rather than insufficient. The authors instead highlight poor recognition of specific non-neurotoxin components (β-cardiotoxin and PLA2) as salient, not an inability to bind neurotoxins due to concentration. The passage provides no evidence of an overwhelmed binding capacity by neurotoxins.

322.Option: The antivenom's failure to neutralize O. hannah venom is due to the degradation of neurotoxins during storage, despite strong immunoreactivity to intact neurotoxins in the assay
Evidence: O. hannah venom represents the only venom that could not be neutralized by FNAV in current study, although FNAV did cross-reacted intensely with the major lethal toxins (neurotoxins, which formed 50% of proteome based on our venom proteome and immunoprofiling analyses as shown in Figs 3 and 6 and S5 Table).
Another possibility is that LAAO protein might have been degraded in the venom after long-term storage and thus could not be detected.
322.Rationale: The passage does mention degradation as a possible explanation for lack of detection of LAAO, but it does not suggest that neurotoxins were degraded. On the contrary, neurotoxins are reported as being strongly cross-reactive with FNAV, which argues against their degradation. There is no evidence in the text that neurotoxin degradation during storage caused the failure to neutralize O. hannah venom.

322.Option: The poor neutralization of O. hannah venom is due to the presence of a novel, non-recognized toxin that is not detected by proteomic analysis, despite strong immunoreactivity to known neurotoxins
Evidence: For HPLC-fractionated O. hannah venom, NTX (fractions 9–18), CRISP (fraction 32, 35) and SVMP (fraction 33, 35, 38, 40, 42–44) immunoreacted strongly toward FNAV, whereas those in fractions 19 (band 3), 20, 21 (band 2), 22, 23, 28 and 29 were weakly detected, or not detected, by Western blotting (Fig 6 and Table 2). The major protein in these fractions with lower immunereactivity was identified as β-cardiotoxin, a member of the CTX protein family (S5 Table).
The conflicting finding that FNAV could react strongly to the major neurotoxins yet failed to neutralize the lethality at the challenge dose used remains unresolved here and warrants further study.
322.Rationale: The passage reports proteomic identification of the poorly recognized fractions (e.g., β-cardiotoxin and PLA2) rather than an unidentified novel toxin. While the authors state the observed failure to neutralize despite neurotoxin recognition is unresolved, they provide specific candidate proteins with poor immunoreactivity. There is no direct evidence in the text for an entirely novel, undetected toxin causing the lack of neutralization; instead, identified antigenic divergence in known components is presented as the more supported explanation.

322.Option: The high sequence similarity between O. hannah neurotoxins and those in the immunogen (N. atra) ensures complete neutralization, but the antivenom's inability to bind to non-toxic venom proteins limits its overall efficacy
Evidence: O. hannah venom represents the only venom that could not be neutralized by FNAV in current study, although FNAV did cross-reacted intensely with the major lethal toxins (neurotoxins, which formed 50% of proteome based on our venom proteome and immunoprofiling analyses as shown in Figs 3 and 6 and S5 Table).
Moreover, the major proteins in fractions 19, 21 and 27, identified as PLA2 family proteins, also showed poor immunoreactivity to FNAV.
This analysis revealed that the sequence similarity is quite low between O. Hannah PLA2 and N. atra PLA2 (64%) or between O. Hannah PLA2 and B. multicinctus PLA2 (59%).
322.Rationale: This option asserts that high sequence similarity ensures complete neutralization of neurotoxins and blames inability to bind 'non-toxic' proteins for limited efficacy. The passage contradicts this: despite strong cross-reaction with neurotoxins, FNAV failed to neutralize O. hannah venom, and the poorly recognized components implicated (PLA2 and β-cardiotoxin) are not described as non-toxic. Moreover, the passage documents low sequence similarity for PLA2 (and poor immunoreactivity), undermining the claim of high similarity and 'complete neutralization.' The text therefore does not support this option.

323.Option: Persistent elevation of serum IgG levels that remain significantly elevated at week 11 p.e. compared to baseline
Evidence: Increases in IgG were detected in 7/12 animals p.e., peaking between weeks 4 and 6 (p<0.0001 between weeks 0 and 4 and between weeks 0 and 6) and slightly decreasing by week 11 (p = 0.04 between weeks 6 and 11).
These results demonstrate that conjunctival Ct exposure elicits systemic circulation of Ct-specific IgA and IgG, which remained elevated to the latest time-point tested.
Serum IgA and IgG increases were correlated with increased bacterial loads
323.Rationale: Although systemic (serum) IgG rose after exposure and remained detectable at the latest time-point, the passage reports that serum IgG was correlated with increased bacterial loads rather than with resolution of disease. The quoted text shows IgG peaked at weeks 4–6 and then "slightly decreased by week 11," and explicitly states serum IgG increases correlated with increased bacterial loads. Therefore serum IgG elevation does not most strongly support use as a correlate of protection tied to disease resolution.

323.Option: Conjunctival monocyte proportion that increases significantly from week 6 p.e. and peaks at week 11 p.e., coinciding with the resolution of clinical signs
Evidence: their proportion among leucocytes steadily increased from 6 weeks p.e. and peaked at 11 weeks p.e. (53 ± 9% of all immune cells, p = 0.0078 for week 11 (final time tested) vs week 1 p.e.).
At these later time-points, although we observed a reduction in local leucocyte populations, we also found a higher proportion of monocytes, the cell type mainly responsible for IL-1RA secretion.
These observations suggest that local monocytes are characteristic of the continuance of clinical signs of inflammation once bacterial infection resolves.
323.Rationale: While monocyte proportions do increase from week 6 and peak at week 11, the authors interpret this late monocyte presence as characteristic of the continuance of clinical signs after bacterial clearance, not as a marker of protection or resolution. The passage explicitly links increased monocytes with persistent clinical signs in the post-infection period, so this parameter does not support use as a correlate of protection associated with disease resolution.

323.Option: Early and sustained increase in conjunctival T cell proportions, which peak at 3 weeks p.e. and remain elevated through the convalescent phase
Evidence: Leucocyte numbers (CD45+ cells normalized to 50,000 observed cells) increased at 1 week p.e., and generally peaked at 3 weeks p.e. (p = 0.0078 baseline vs week 3 p.e.) (Fig 4A).

During the first 2 weeks p.e., the proportion of T cells increased slightly from baseline (57 ± 8% to 67 ± 9%, p = 0.1210), and remained stable (Fig 4B). Between weeks 3 and 4, T cells proportions fluctuated and by week 6 the proportions had decreased.
323.Rationale: T cells do show an early peak around week 3, but the data indicate T cell proportions decline by week 6 rather than remaining elevated through convalescence. Because the pattern is not sustained into the resolution/convalescent phase, conjunctival T cell proportion does not meet the criterion of an immunological parameter whose timing and pattern most strongly support it as a correlate of protection for disease resolution.

323.Option: Delayed peak in pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-17A and IL-1β, which coincides with the resolution of clinical signs and bacterial clearance
Evidence: Cytokines VEGF, IL-12/23, IL-13, IL-17α, IL-10, IFN-y, IL-2, IL-15, sCD40L and IL-1β decreased between weeks 4 or 6, or during both timepoints, but rebounded at week 11 p.e., to levels higher than baseline, and this effect was observed in more macaques.
our observations of the late increase in both IL-12/23 and IL-17A concur with these findings.
Bacterial clearance coincided with a reduction in conjunctival inflammation, although the presence of follicles persisted
323.Rationale: Although IL-17A and IL-1β show delayed increases at week 11, these late pro-inflammatory rises occur after treatment/clearance and are discussed in the context of a convalescent phase that may relate to scarring processes (Th17/IL-23 pathways) rather than to protective resolution. The rebound of these pro-inflammatory cytokines at week 11 does not clearly coincide with improved clinical resolution; instead, they may reflect late inflammatory activity or processes associated with scarring, so they are not a strong correlate of protection.

323.Option: Sustained tear IgA response that persists after bacterial clearance and coincides with the resolution phase of disease
Evidence: IgG and IgA specific to Ct elementary bodies were quantified from tears to determine the local adaptive humoral response to conjunctival Ct exposure. IgG increased comparably at 2 weeks p.e. (p = 0.021), then both IgG and IgA peaked between 4 and 6 weeks p.e. (p = 0.001 and 0.002 between weeks 0 and 6 for IgG and IgA respectively), and then decreased at 11 weeks p.e. (p = 0.0098 and 0.0039 for IgG and IgA respectively) (Fig 5B). These data demonstrate that local adaptive humoral responses developed over the first 6 weeks p.e.
Similarly, we observed a tendency toward decreased ocular signs around 6 weeks p.e. corresponding to a peak in specific anti-Ct IgA in tears, concomitant with a higher proportion of B cells.
In 4/12 macaques, the bacterial load decreased to undetectable levels at 6 weeks p.e. In the 8 remaining macaques the bacterial load decreased to undetectable levels by week 9
323.Rationale: The tear (local mucosal) Ct-specific IgA shows a clear temporal association with disease improvement: tear IgA peaks between weeks 4–6, and the authors note a corresponding tendency toward decreased ocular signs around 6 weeks p.e. This timing aligns with bacterial decline for a subset of animals (undetectable by week 6 in some, and by week 9 in others), supporting a role for local IgA in the resolution phase. Although tear IgA levels decline by week 11, the peak at 4–6 weeks that coincides with clinical improvement makes local tear IgA the parameter in this study that most strongly supports potential use as a correlate of protection tied to disease resolution.

324.Option: Using a crossover design where parasites are exposed to drug and no-drug conditions in alternating periods to control for individual parasite variability
Evidence: Due to significant decline in Wolbachia content with time in culture due to loss of mf production (Fig 4), vehicle treated cultures were ran simultaneously and sampled at 7 or 14 days as matched controls (Fig 5A).
An additional wash-out group, which entailed drug treatment for 7 days followed by a 7-day washout period was included in the study to evaluate any Wolbachia recrudescence.
324.Rationale: The passage describes parallel vehicle-treated control cultures and a wash-out group, not a crossover design. A crossover (alternating drug/no-drug periods for the same parasite) is not described and would be impractical here due to biological carryover and irreversible changes (e.g., Wolbachia depletion, cessation of embryogenesis). The quoted text shows the study used simultaneous matched controls and a washout experiment rather than a crossover across multiple treatment conditions.

324.Option: Comparing Wolbachia load in treated parasites to baseline levels measured immediately after isolation from in vivo hosts
Evidence: Wolbachia yields within female B. malayi after 1, 2 or 3 weeks in LEC+LEC insert co-cultures were compared with those from parasites immediately retrieved from IL-4Rα-/-IL-5-/- mice (Fig 4B).

To more accurately interpret whether these measures reflected a real decline in viability or cessation of mf production, a sample of 62 adult female worms were isolated from CB.17 SCID mouse infections, and approximately equal batches were either processed immediately for metabolic activity and Wolbachia molecular assays, set up into culture within LEC bilayers, or implanted into naïve CB.17 SCID recipients (Fig 4C).
324.Rationale: The investigators did compare culture samples to baseline in vivo levels, which helps characterize natural changes over time. However, baseline comparisons alone do not most directly attribute changes to drug exposure because Wolbachia naturally decline in culture (e.g., with cessation of embryogenesis). The study therefore used concurrent vehicle-treated controls to separate drug effects from time-related physiological changes; baseline comparison is informative but not the single most direct design feature for attributing changes to drug exposure.

324.Option: Using a within-subjects design where each female B. malayi is exposed to multiple drug treatments in sequence to compare effects directly
Evidence: An additional wash-out group, which entailed drug treatment for 7 days followed by a 7-day washout period was included in the study to evaluate any Wolbachia recrudescence.

Due to significant decline in Wolbachia content with time in culture due to loss of mf production (Fig 4), vehicle treated cultures were ran simultaneously and sampled at 7 or 14 days as matched controls (Fig 5A).
324.Rationale: The passage documents a washout group (drug then washout) but does not describe a within-subjects sequence exposing the same individual worms to multiple distinct drug treatments. The design used separate treatment groups and simultaneous vehicle controls rather than serially exposing the same parasite to multiple different drugs, so a within-subject multi-treatment design was not implemented.

324.Option: Including concurrent vehicle-treated control cultures sampled at identical time points to account for natural decline in Wolbachia over time
Evidence: Due to significant decline in Wolbachia content with time in culture due to loss of mf production (Fig 4), vehicle treated cultures were ran simultaneously and sampled at 7 or 14 days as matched controls (Fig 5A).

Because significant decline in Wolbachia content with time in culture due to loss of mf production (Fig 4), vehicle treated cultures were ran simultaneously and sampled at 7 or 14 days as matched controls (Fig 5A).

Removal of DOX at day 7 followed by a 7-day washout initiated a recrudescence of Wolbachia within the cultured female B. malayi worms, resulting in a 57% non-significant reduction of Wolbachia (Fig 5E). Contrastingly, 7-days post-removal of flubentylosin, Wolbachia recrudescence was not apparent, with a significant median Wolbachia reduction of 79% in comparison to the vehicle control group (3.02x106 vs 1.44x107 median Wolbachia loads, P<0.001, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparison post-hoc test, Fig 5E).
324.Rationale: The investigators explicitly ran vehicle-treated control cultures in parallel and sampled them at the same time points as drug-treated cultures to account for the known natural decline in Wolbachia during culture (e.g., from cessation of embryogenesis). These concurrent matched controls allow separation of drug-induced Wolbachia depletion from time-dependent physiological decline, making this the most direct design feature for attributing observed Wolbachia changes to drug exposure.

325.Option: Reduced predation pressure in oil palm plantations due to lower invertebrate diversity
Evidence: This increased abundance was not estimated from TPCs.
as forest conversion to oil palm plantation reduces invertebrate diversity and abundance [76,77], predation and competition may also be reduced.

We estimated the vectorial capacity of Ae. albopictus mosquitoes in oil palm plantations to be 1.5 times that of those in logged forests; a result driven by a greater mosquito abundance in plantations.
325.Rationale: The authors explicitly propose reduced predation and competition resulting from lowered invertebrate diversity after forest conversion as a plausible ecological mechanism explaining greater mosquito abundance in plantations. This is consistent with the observed higher collection counts in plantations despite similar estimates of temperature-dependent life-history traits and TPC-derived parameters.

325.Option: Greater daily temperature fluctuations in oil palm plantations leading to increased mosquito reproductive output
Evidence: daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].

In a previous study conducted at these same field sites, we found that differences in daily temperature fluctuations resulted in faster larval development rates in oil palm plantations [55], an effect not predicted by published TPCs.

Despite warmer temperatures in plantation sites, none of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).
325.Rationale: Although plantations had greater daily temperature fluctuation and prior work at these sites showed faster larval development, the study found that temperature-dependent trait estimates (from TPCs) did not differ significantly between land-use types and concluded abundance differences were not explained by the measured life-history traits. Therefore temperature fluctuation as the primary explanation is not supported as the most likely factor in this study.

325.Option: Higher ambient temperatures in oil palm plantations enhancing mosquito development and survival rates
Evidence: Mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period,

Our observations that mean temperatures did not differ significantly between sites is thus likely due to selection of older plantations and significantly degraded forest, and it’s possible that using temperature data from a wider range of forest and plantations sites to drive our model may have produced different estimates for abundance and vectorial capacity.
325.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between plantations and logged forest during sampling, so higher ambient temperatures in plantations cannot explain the higher mosquito counts in this dataset.

325.Option: Increased availability of artificial breeding sites due to higher human activity and infrastructure investment in oil palm plantations
Evidence: monoculture may reduce breeding habitats by removing trees, but may also increase the availability of artificial breeding sites due to increases in human activity.

The plantation and forest sites sampled in this study were all characterised by labour-intensive economies, but the level of investment in housing infrastructure and public services was considerably higher in the former.
Variation in water storage, waste management and vector control would all have impacted mosquito abundance, but characterising these factors were beyond the scope of this study.
human density, however it was likely higher in plantations than in logged forest and may contribute to the higher mosquito density there.
325.Rationale: The authors identify increased human activity, infrastructure investment, and associated changes (water storage, waste, artificial containers) in plantations as a plausible driver of greater mosquito abundance. This socio-ecological explanation fits the observation of higher trap counts despite similar biological trait estimates and is explicitly discussed as a likely contributing factor.

325.Option: Increased availability of natural breeding sites due to forest degradation in logged forest sites
Evidence: monoculture may reduce breeding habitats by removing trees, but may also increase the availability of artificial breeding sites due to increases in human activity.
Conversion has been at the expense of either selectively logged or old growth forest [41].

Overall, human landing catches conducted in plantations yielded an average 1.5 times more mosquitoes per session than those at logged forest sites (GLM, RR = 0.42, df = 64, 95% CI: 1.17, 2.0, p = 0.002).
325.Rationale: The suggestion that logged forest sites have increased natural breeding sites is not supported by the text; conversion and monoculture tend to remove natural tree habitats. The observed higher abundance was in plantations, not logged forest, and the authors instead point to reduced invertebrate predators and increased artificial breeding opportunities in plantations as explanations.

326.Option: The specificity of pyriproxyfen to mosquito species ensures that non-target organisms remain unaffected despite its long-term presence in the environment
Evidence: PPF is not mosquito specific and has been shown to inhibit the development of other insects [13–15] and to impact the physiology of non-target invertebrates [16, 17].
PPF has been shown to persist up to four months in the environment and is incredibly potent [8, 9].
Mortality in A. mellifera adults was previously observed after exposure to PPF through feeding, topical, or contaminated substrate [22].
326.Rationale: The option asserts mosquito-specificity and no non-target effects, but the passage explicitly states the opposite: PPF is not mosquito specific, has been shown to affect other insects (including honey bees), and persists in the environment. Those statements directly refute the claim that non-target organisms remain unaffected.

326.Option: The self-delivery mechanism of autodissemination reduces the need for repeated applications, thereby decreasing the total amount of insecticide released and minimizing ecological risk
Evidence: There are a couple of advantages to using autodissemination techniques: (1) less labor is required because mosquitoes self-deliver insecticide, and (2) the need for less active ingredient relative to conventional control measures.

However, while direct targeting of cryptic locations seems like a significant advantage over large scale applications of insecticides, this could actually be detrimental to nontarget organisms by delivering these highly potent long-lasting growth inhibitors to the exact places that other beneficial insects visit, such as locations where many anthophilous insects seek nectar.
326.Rationale: While the passage supports that autodissemination reduces labor and the amount of active ingredient, it also warns that this delivery method can increase ecological risk by putting potent, long-lasting inhibitors exactly where non-target beneficial insects visit. Therefore the claim that self-delivery minimizes ecological risk is not supported; the passage presents both the advantage and the countervailing risk.

326.Option: The use of male mosquitoes as delivery agents enhances targeted control of breeding sites while reducing the chance of contamination in non-target habitats due to their limited foraging range
Evidence: As currently practiced, autodissemination consists of two methods: (1) adult male mosquitoes can be treated with an Insect Growth Regulator (IGR) and the males deliver lethal doses to breeding containers, which has been dubbed ‘Autodissemination Augmented by Males’ (ADAM) [5, 6] and (2) placing artificial adult resting sites (‘dissemination stations’) in the field, which are attractive to adult mosquitoes, and are treated with a persistent IGR [7].

Results suggest the direct and indirect transfer of PPF to artificial nectar sources from Ae. albopictus males. Furthermore, A. mellifera when in the same cages with PPF treated Ae. albopictus were also contaminated with PPF.
A. mellifera will travel longer distances to forage compared to Ae. albopictus, so the potential for vectoring PPF across landscapes is high with the observed indirect transfer from nectar sources.
326.Rationale: Although males are used in ADAM and can deliver PPF to breeding containers, the passage documents direct and indirect transfer of PPF from treated males to artificial nectar sources and to honey bees. The text does not support the idea that male use reduces contamination of non-target habitats; instead it shows males can contaminate nectar sources and that bees may then spread PPF more widely.

326.Option: The long environmental persistence of pyriproxyfen enables sustained mosquito control but increases the likelihood of unintended contamination of pollinator habitats
Evidence: PPF has been shown to persist up to four months in the environment and is incredibly potent [8, 9].

However, while direct targeting of cryptic locations seems like a significant advantage over large scale applications of insecticides, this could actually be detrimental to nontarget organisms by delivering these highly potent long-lasting growth inhibitors to the exact places that other beneficial insects visit, such as locations where many anthophilous insects seek nectar.
PPF is not mosquito specific and has been shown to inhibit the development of other insects [13–15] and to impact the physiology of non-target invertebrates [16, 17].
Results suggest A. mellifera can be contaminated with PPF indirectly through the visitation of artificial nectar sources in the presence of PPF treated male Ae. albopictus mosquitoes.
326.Rationale: The passage identifies PPF's multi-month persistence and high potency, linking that persistence to both potential sustained control and a clear risk of contaminating sites bees and other pollinators visit. These statements make long environmental persistence the factor most likely to influence long-term success while simultaneously introducing non-target risk.

326.Option: The high potency of pyriproxyfen at low concentrations ensures effective mosquito control while minimizing exposure to non-target species through rapid degradation in the environment
Evidence: PPF has been shown to persist up to four months in the environment and is incredibly potent [8, 9].
The concentration required to prevent mosquito development (LC50) is only 0.012 parts per billion [10].

However, while direct targeting of cryptic locations seems like a significant advantage over large scale applications of insecticides, this could actually be detrimental to nontarget organisms by delivering these highly potent long-lasting growth inhibitors to the exact places that other beneficial insects visit, such as locations where many anthophilous insects seek nectar.
326.Rationale: While the passage confirms PPF's high potency at very low concentrations, it explicitly contradicts the claim of rapid degradation; instead it states PPF can persist up to four months. Moreover, persistence and potency raise concerns about non-target exposure rather than minimizing it. Thus the assertion that potency plus rapid degradation minimizes non-target exposure is not supported.

327.Option: The ability to detect and resolve multiple melting curve peaks allows identification of mixed blood meals, revealing simultaneous host use by vectors
Evidence: we evaluated the possibility of detecting at least two different blood meal sources. In this case, the melt curve showed two peaks belonging to mouse and chicken amplicons, with their respective Tm. In addition, the dissociation curve had a shape with two falls (Figure S2).

Unspecific peaks led to testing mixed feedings, showing that the technique detects both Tm sources. This allows identification of many species from which the insects have been fed, even when there are no standards for those species.
327.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that HRM melt curves can show two peaks corresponding to different host amplicons and that the technique detects both Tm sources in mixed feedings. That direct ability to resolve multiple melting peaks is what lets researchers identify mixed blood meals and hence infer simultaneous or multiple host use by vectors, which reveals complexity beyond single-host identification.

327.Option: The technique's sensitivity to nucleated erythrocytes allows it to detect blood from species with higher DNA content, thereby revealing the most frequent host species in a vector's diet
Evidence: Chicken blood has nucleated erythrocytes, so a large amount of DNA should remain in the intestine for a long time.
This could explain the presence of unspecific peaks in the melt curve when insects were fed with human blood because the insects are usually fed with chicken blood in laboratory conditions.
The HRM technique proved to work in both types of samples (intestinal and fecal) at least until 30 days after insects were fed.
327.Rationale: While the passage notes that chicken blood (nucleated erythrocytes) yields more persistent DNA and can produce long-lasting or 'unspecific' peaks, this is described as a bias or an explanation for mixed/unspecific signals, not as a feature that reliably reveals the "most frequent" host. Persistence of DNA from nucleated erythrocytes may cause overrepresentation of some hosts (a detection bias), so the statement that this sensitivity reveals the most frequent host is not supported and is misleading.

327.Option: The high confidence percentage values generated by the software allow researchers to assign genotypes with certainty, even when multiple host species are present, enabling accurate reconstruction of transmission networks
Evidence: A specific profile for each species analyzed was observed (Figures 2 and S1), with confidence percentages (%C) ranging from 78.81% and 98.69% (Table 2), indicating that samples were correctly identified in each of the experiments conducted in triplicate.
All samples were correctly classified as blood-fed chicken (C) and just one of the samples obtained from feces (R. prolixus) showed low confidence percentage.

Unspecific peaks led to testing mixed feedings, showing that the technique detects both Tm sources. This allows identification of many species from which the insects have been fed, even when there are no standards for those species.
However, we suggest using sequencing of amplicons only when unidentified samples by HRM analysis are present.
327.Rationale: The passage documents high confidence percentages for single-source genotypes and shows HRM can detect mixed feedings. However, it also reports instances of low confidence and recommends sequencing for unidentified samples. The option overstates the certainty of genotype assignment "even when multiple host species are present." In mixed or ambiguous cases the method may have lower confidence and may require sequencing, so the claim of guaranteed certainty (and direct reconstruction of transmission networks) is not fully supported.

327.Option: The use of a single pair of primers for amplification enables detection of a broad range of host species, facilitating the identification of novel or unexpected blood sources in complex transmission settings
Evidence: Instead, the technique used herein only needs a single pair of primers, making it easy to include other species according to every epidemiological scenario with no other standardizations of PCR conditions.

Unspecific peaks led to testing mixed feedings, showing that the technique detects both Tm sources. This allows identification of many species from which the insects have been fed, even when there are no standards for those species.

In conclusion, we believe that epidemiological studies involving vectorial incrimination and transmission dynamics must identify the blood-meal source to cover the entire panorama of transmission. HRM analysis of the Cyt b gene is the most powerful technique in this type of study because it can accurately identify the species even when the vector has mixed feeding, it has a high resolution power, and it is fast, easy, and inexpensive.
327.Rationale: The passage explicitly highlights that only a single primer pair is required, which simplifies inclusion of additional species without reoptimizing PCRs, and that HRM can detect species even when standards are not present. Those points support the option that a single-pair approach enables broad detection and helps identify novel or unexpected blood sources in complex transmission scenarios.

327.Option: The ability to analyze both intestinal content and feces samples ensures that the timing of blood meals can be estimated, allowing researchers to reconstruct the sequence of host use over time
Evidence: All samples were successfully amplified by qPCR 1, 5, 15, and 30 days post-feeding (Table 3). Intestinal content and feces samples showed successful amplification and detection of the blood-meal source after 30 days post-feeding, indicating the high sensitivity of Cyt b-HRM at this time.
