417.Option: Social and economic consequences, such as loss of income, family strain, and stigma, significantly amplify the perceived severity of lymphedema beyond physical symptoms
Evidence: The main themes that surfaced involved the inability to work, especially during periodic acute episodes and the economic impact of this.
Not only were economic effects noted at the individual level, but participants claimed that the economic impact extended to the entire family.
‘during the [acute] attack the whole family suffers. We all have to look after the patient. Two to three persons have to be always with the patient leaving all their work behind’.
‘We as family members are mentally depressed when she gets an acute attack’.
‘We feel bad when people call us as “GODARI” [Elephantiasis], and discuss our swelling legs mischievously.’

It is noteworthy that the economic impacts of living with lymphedema were a major theme among patients and family members, emphasizing lost productivity and financial losses associated with lymphedema and acute episodes and costs for treatment.
417.Rationale: Multiple passages explicitly identify economic impacts (lost productivity, family economic losses), family strain (caregiving burden, mental depression of relatives), and social stigma (insults, isolation, marriageability issues) as central themes in the focus groups. The authors emphasize these psychosocial and economic consequences as major influences on how patients perceive the severity and impact of their condition, beyond the physical manifestations alone. Therefore this option best reflects the qualitative findings.

417.Option: The clinical stage of lymphedema, as determined by the Dreyer scale, was the primary determinant of perceived severity and daily life impact
Evidence: Program participants were assessed for lymphedema stage according to the Dreyer scale by an experienced grader.
Patients had experienced lymphedema for an average of 29.1 years and the average length of participation in the program was 3.3 years.
The main themes that surfaced involved the inability to work, especially during periodic acute episodes and the economic impact of this.

This qualitative study demonstrates how the psychosocial effects and economic impacts experienced by individuals with filarial lymphedema can be ameliorated with the implementation of a community-based lymphedema management program.
417.Rationale: Although clinical staging using the Dreyer scale was recorded for participants, the qualitative data do not present clinical stage as the primary determinant of perceived severity. Instead, the analysis and participant quotes emphasize social, economic, and psychosocial effects as the dominant factors influencing perceived severity and daily life impact. Thus this option is not supported as the main factor in the qualitative findings.

418.Option: The consistent presence of eschars and the high proportion of farmers suggest that the transmission cycle is maintained through seasonal migration of infected humans into forested areas
Evidence: Scrub typhus is a rickettsiosis which is caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi (formerly named Rickettsia tsutsugamushi), transmitted by the bite of larval trombiculid mites [1, 2].
Since there is no vaccine available, the main current prevention method is vector control and avoidance of exposure.
More than three-quarters (78.5%) of the patients were farmers.
The proportion of patients who had worked or traveled in the forest was 48.5% overall, but only 38.7% in the scrub typhus group.
418.Rationale: Although the passage documents eschars and a high proportion of farmers and forest exposure, it explicitly describes transmission as occurring via larval trombiculid mites and emphasizes vector control and avoidance of exposure. That implies an environmental reservoir and vector-driven infection rather than a cycle maintained by seasonal human migration. The quoted lines support that exposure to environmental vectors (mites) is the relevant pathway; they do not support the claim that infected humans migrating seasonally maintain transmission, so this option is not supported by the passage.

418.Option: The high genetic similarity among O. tsutsugamushi isolates and the frequent detection of rickettsial DNA in blood samples indicate that the disease is maintained through a continuous cycle of human infection and vector acquisition
Evidence: Genetic analysis of the 56-kDa TSA gene sequences demonstrated that the 14 sequences obtained in this study were related to 4 strains, including Karp, Kawasaki, TA716 and Gilliam (JG-v and TG-v) (Fig 2 and Table 2). The majority (9/14; 64.4%) of contemporary O. tsutsugamushi genotypes were related to the Karp strain, with a percentage nucleotide identity (PNI) ranging from 97% to 100% in comparison to Karp strain references (Tables 2 and 3).

Among the 20 cases who tested positive by O. tsutsugamushi qPCR, 12 (60%) tested positive using their eschar only; 5 (25%) had both their blood and eschars positive. Also, the blood of 3 additional patients (15%) tested positive.
PCR on blood has been known to be lower (29%).
Scrub typhus ... transmitted by the bite of larval trombiculid mites
418.Rationale: While the passage reports a predominance of Karp-related sequences, it does not show frequent blood detection; in fact it notes that PCR on blood has lower sensitivity and only a minority of qPCR positives were blood-positive. Moreover, the text identifies transmission via larval trombiculid mites, implying vector-borne transmission with environmental reservoirs rather than a self-sustaining cycle of continuous human-to-vector-to-human transmission. Thus the evidence does not support the specific claim that maintenance is driven primarily by continuous human infection and vector acquisition.

418.Option: The high prevalence of acute undifferentiated fever and the frequent detection of eschars in patients support the idea that scrub typhus is maintained through direct human-to-human transmission with a stable vector population
Evidence: The disease is known to occur throughout the Asia-Pacific region but its range may be larger, with possible case reports in Africa [3] and South America [4, 5].
Scrub typhus is a rickettsiosis which is caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi ... transmitted by the bite of larval trombiculid mites [1, 2].
More than three-quarters (78.5%) of the patients were farmers.
Eschar lesions occurred in 18 patients (56.2%) of the scrub typhus group and 4 patients (12.1%) in the non-scrub typhus/non-murine typhus group.
418.Rationale: The passage clearly states that transmission is via the bite of larval trombiculid mites, i.e. vector-borne, and advocates vector control and avoidance of exposure as prevention. There is no support for direct human-to-human transmission; the presence of AUF and eschars among patients is consistent with vector-acquired infection from an environmental reservoir. Therefore the option claiming direct human-to-human maintenance is not supported by the text.

418.Option: The predominance of the Karp genotype and the high rate of positive qPCR results from eschar swabs indicate that the local transmission cycle is driven by a highly transmissible strain with a broad host range
Evidence: The majority (9/14; 64.4%) of contemporary O. tsutsugamushi genotypes were related to the Karp strain, with a percentage nucleotide identity (PNI) ranging from 97% to 100% in comparison to Karp strain references (Tables 2 and 3).
We found a high rate of positive results of the qPCR using eschar specimens, with 17 positive samples for O. tsutsugamushi in 20 eschars specimens (85%) obtained from patients.
We found an eschar in all cases of Gilliam, Kawasaki and TA716 groups, but in only 7 (77.8%) cases of Karp.
Since there is no vaccine available, the main current prevention method is vector control and avoidance of exposure.
418.Rationale: Although Karp predominance and high eschar qPCR positivity are reported, the passage also documents genotypic diversity and genotype-specific differences in clinical findings (e.g., eschar occurrence varies by genotype). There is no data presented on host-range breadth or measures of transmissibility across hosts. The evidence supports a localized genotype predominance and effective eschar-based detection, but does not justify concluding a highly transmissible strain with a broad host range. Therefore this option over-interprets the findings and is not supported.

418.Option: The high genetic similarity of O. tsutsugamushi isolates and the predominance of farmers among cases suggest a stable, localized transmission cycle involving a persistent reservoir and a vector with a defined ecological niche
Evidence: Scrub typhus is a rickettsiosis which is caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi (formerly named Rickettsia tsutsugamushi), transmitted by the bite of larval trombiculid mites [1, 2].
Quang Nam province is a coastal province in central Vietnam that may be a highly endemic area for O. tsutsugamushi, as well as other rickettsial infections.
More than three-quarters (78.5%) of the patients were farmers.
The proportion of patients who had worked or traveled in the forest was 48.5% overall, but only 38.7% in the scrub typhus group.

The majority (9/14; 64.4%) of contemporary O. tsutsugamushi genotypes were related to the Karp strain, with a percentage nucleotide identity (PNI) ranging from 97% to 100% in comparison to Karp strain references (Tables 2 and 3).
Since there is no vaccine available, the main current prevention method is vector control and avoidance of exposure.
418.Rationale: This option is best supported by the passage. The documented predominance of a closely related genotype (Karp-related sequences with high PNI) together with strong occupational and environmental exposure (78.5% farmers; substantial forest exposure) and explicit identification of transmission by larval trombiculid mites point to a stable, localized transmission cycle involving an environmental reservoir and a vector occupying a defined ecological niche. The recommendation of vector control and avoidance of exposure further aligns with the interpretation that transmission is maintained in the environment rather than by human-to-human spread.

419.Option: The strong association between Ov16 seropositivity and epilepsy, especially in individuals with a family history of seizures, indicates that shared environmental exposure is the primary driver of familial clustering, not inherited susceptibility
Evidence: Ov16 seroprevalence was associated with epilepsy (OR: 2.03, 95% CI: 1.07–3.86), including OAE (OR: 2.26, 95% CI: 1.15–4.47) and probable NS (OR: 5.05, 95% CI: 1.79–14.27).

When added to a univariable logistic regression adjusted for age, sex and village type, having a first-degree relative with epilepsy was strongly associated with Ov16 seropositivity (adjusted OR: 15.02, 95% CI: 2.97–274.7; p = 0.0094) but not significantly related to having a second-degree relative (p = 0.21) or an in-law with epilepsy (p = 0.25).
This is supported by the fact that individuals who tested Ov16 seropositive were more likely to have a first-degree relative with epilepsy.

Still, this association between epilepsy and having a family member with epilepsy was lost when the conditional regression was adjusted for Ov16 seropositivity, indicating that exposure to O. volvulus played a more significant role in the observed relationship.
419.Rationale: The passage reports a clear positive association between Ov16 seropositivity (a marker of exposure to O. volvulus) and epilepsy and shows that family history of epilepsy correlates with Ov16 seropositivity. Importantly, the familial association disappears after adjusting for Ov16 seropositivity, implying that shared exposure to O. volvulus (an environmental factor) explains the clustering better than inherited genetic susceptibility.

419.Option: The near-universal Ov16 seropositivity among individuals with a sibling with epilepsy, despite varying genetic relatedness, suggests that shared environmental exposure overrides genetic risk in the development of epilepsy
Evidence: Notably, almost all epilepsy cases (92.2%) and all controls with a sibling with epilepsy tested seropositive (one case and one control were not tested).

This observation is consistent with the fact that family members, especially first-degree relatives, often live in the same neighbourhood and thus share similar exposures to O. volvulus-infected blackflies [35].
Individuals who tested Ov16 seropositive were more likely to have a first-degree relative with epilepsy.
419.Rationale: The near-universal Ov16 seropositivity among those with a sibling with epilepsy indicates that exposure to O. volvulus is common in these families. Because siblings share both genes and environment, the strong, near-universal marker of exposure supports the interpretation that shared environmental exposure (blackfly bites/O. volvulus) is the dominant explanation for clustering rather than genetic differences alone.

419.Option: The fact that individuals with a family history of epilepsy lived longer in the study area, where onchocerciasis is endemic, indicates that prolonged exposure to blackflies is the main reason for familial clustering of epilepsy
Evidence: Families of persons with epilepsy have lived more years in the villages under study (p<0.0001).
The length of residence in the village was correlated with Ov16 seroprevalence (cor = 0.30, p<0.0001), and the latter was also correlated to a family history of seizures (cor = 0.32, p<0.0001).

Moreover, families of persons with Ov16 antibody seropositivity lived for longer in the study villages (p<0.0001), which are known to have been meso- to hyperendemic for onchocerciasis before CDTI and still have persistent transmission of O. volvulus [19,20].
419.Rationale: Longer residence in an onchocerciasis-endemic area increases cumulative exposure to infected blackfly bites. The passage documents that families of cases have lived longer in the villages and that residence length correlates with Ov16 seroprevalence and family history of seizures, supporting prolonged environmental exposure as a driver of familial clustering.

419.Option: The association between family history of epilepsy and case status disappears after adjusting for Ov16 seropositivity, indicating that shared environmental exposure, not genetics, drives clustering
Evidence: Still, this association between epilepsy and having a family member with epilepsy was lost when the conditional regression was adjusted for Ov16 seropositivity, indicating that exposure to O. volvulus played a more significant role in the observed relationship.
Ov16 seroprevalence was associated with epilepsy (OR: 2.03, 95% CI: 1.07–3.86)...
419.Rationale: A result that an association with family history is attenuated or eliminated after controlling for a marker of exposure (Ov16 seropositivity) indicates confounding by shared exposure. This finding directly supports the conclusion that environmental exposure to O. volvulus (not inherited genetic risk) explains the familial clustering.

419.Option: The higher odds of epilepsy among second-degree relatives, combined with the fact that most individuals with a sibling with epilepsy were Ov16 seropositive, suggests that shared exposure to blackflies explains familial clustering more than genetic factors
Evidence: A person with a second-degree relative with epilepsy had higher odds of having epilepsy, including probable NS.

This observation is consistent with the fact that family members, especially first-degree relatives, often live in the same neighbourhood and thus share similar exposures to O. volvulus-infected blackflies [35].
Notably, almost all epilepsy cases (92.2%) and all controls with a sibling with epilepsy tested seropositive.
419.Rationale: Second-degree relatives share less genetic material than first-degree relatives; finding elevated odds among them argues against a purely Mendelian genetic cause. Coupled with the high Ov16 seropositivity among those with affected siblings and the documented shared environmental exposure (neighbourhood, blackfly contact), this pattern supports environmental exposure as the more plausible explanation for familial clustering.

420.Option: The use of height-based cutoffs in MDA campaigns leads to inconsistent treatment eligibility, which undermines the uniformity of community protection
Evidence: To expedite ivermectin delivery during MDAs, ivermectin dosing is usually based on height and not weight [5] with a cut-off for administration of 90 cm [4].

Based on WHO Child Growth Standards, the median weight for a 90 cm child is 12.7 kg for boys and 12.5 kg for girls, thus a 90 cm child weighing 15kg would be on the 97th weight percentile for boys and the 98th percentile for girls [6].
Therefore, it is likely that children with a weight between 11 and 15 kg in practice are routinely administered ivermectin during MDAs.
Establishing the safety of ivermectin in children weighing less than 15 kg would remove the imprecise weight, height, and age restriction barriers and facilitate more streamlined ivermectin MDAs.
When using ivermectin MDA as a malaria control agent, coverage is the most critical component of efficacy [83].
420.Rationale: While the passage documents that MDA programs often use height-based cutoffs (90 cm) and that these cutoffs introduce imprecision in who is treated (quotes above), the question asks what most critically influences sustained community-level protection. The passage explicitly emphasizes that coverage is the most critical component of efficacy for MDAs. Thus although height-based cutoffs cause inconsistency, the core driver of sustained community protection is overall population coverage (and the reservoir created by untreated children), not merely the use of height-based eligibility per se.

420.Option: The presence of comorbidities such as malnutrition or concurrent infections increases the risk of adverse events, which limits the feasibility of including young children in MDAs
Evidence: One patient given oral ivermectin for strongyloidiasis had symmetrical edema of the feet, but this could not be directly attributed to ivermectin because the child was extremely malnourished and was also given intravenous fluids, iron, and supplements [26].
The evidence summarized here indicates that oral ivermectin caused AEs in 1.4% (15/1,088) children weighing less than 15 kg and there were no SAEs reported.
This review... suggests that ivermectin is as safe and well-tolerated in children weighing less than 15 kg as indeed it is for persons weighing more than 15 kg.
Adequate population coverage is critical for the success of scabies MDA.
420.Rationale: The passage acknowledges comorbidities (e.g., malnutrition) in at least one case but overall reports very low AE rates (1.4%) with no serious adverse events, and concludes ivermectin appears safe and well-tolerated in children <15 kg. The main feasibility barrier emphasized is exclusion reducing coverage and leaving transmission reservoirs, not high adverse event risk from comorbidities. Therefore comorbidities increasing AE risk is not presented as the most critical influence on sustained community-level protection.

420.Option: Incomplete population coverage due to exclusion of young children undermines sustained community-level protection by allowing transmission reservoirs to persist
Evidence: Adequate population coverage is critical for the success of scabies MDA.
Children not treated by ivermectin during MDA serve as a reservoir for future community re-infection unless adequately treated with topical treatments.
When using ivermectin MDA as a malaria control agent, coverage is the most critical component of efficacy [83].
The contraindication of ivermectin for children weighing less than 15 kg excludes roughly 20% of the population in malaria endemic areas, potentially inhibiting MDA efficacy.
Untreated small children serve an important reservoir of Plasmodium and can facilitate onwards transmission to mosquitoes.
420.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that population coverage is critical for MDA success and explicitly describes untreated young children as reservoirs that enable ongoing transmission and reinfection. It also quantifies how exclusion of children <15 kg (roughly 20% in some settings) can inhibit MDA efficacy. Therefore incomplete coverage because of weight-based exclusions is identified in the text as the key factor undermining sustained community-level protection.

420.Option: The pharmacokinetic profile of ivermectin in young children differs significantly from adults, necessitating dose adjustments to maintain sustained protection
Evidence: Recent pharmacokinetic analyses indicate that ivermectin treated children aged less than 12 years reach half the peak concentration and total exposure of adults [93] and a dose increase for young children was suggested [94].

A multi-country, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, dose escalation (200, 400, 800 μg/kg) study, dubbed the Ivermectin Safety in Small Children (ISSC) trial (NCT04332068) is currently underway, which is designed to assess the safety, pharmacokinetics, and efficacy of oral ivermectin in scabies-infected children weighing less than 15 kg [95].
When using ivermectin MDA as a malaria control agent, coverage is the most critical component of efficacy [83].
Adequate population coverage is critical for the success of scabies MDA.
420.Rationale: The passage does report that pharmacokinetics differ in children (lower exposure) and that dose-escalation trials are underway, so dose adjustment is a relevant clinical consideration. However, the question asks which factor most critically influences sustained community-level protection when young children are frequently excluded. The passage emphasizes that overall coverage (inclusion of children) and prevention of reservoirs is the critical determinant of community-level protection, not pharmacokinetic optimization. Thus while PK differences are important for individual dosing and safety/efficacy, they are not presented as the primary driver of sustained community protection.

420.Option: The development of resistance to ivermectin in parasites is more likely when young children are excluded, leading to reduced long-term effectiveness of MDAs
Evidence: When using ivermectin MDA as a malaria control agent, coverage is the most critical component of efficacy [83].
Adequate population coverage is critical for the success of scabies MDA.
Children not treated by ivermectin during MDA serve as a reservoir for future community re-infection unless adequately treated with topical treatments.
420.Rationale: The passage does not discuss the development of resistance to ivermectin as a consequence of excluding young children. The text focuses on how exclusion reduces coverage and leaves untreated children as reservoirs that sustain transmission, which undermines MDA effectiveness. Because resistance development is not raised or supported in the passage as the key issue, this option is not supported by the provided evidence.

421.Option: Using biometric registration to track individual treatment uptake allows for targeted follow-up and ensures that all individuals are treated, thus enabling transmission interruption
Evidence: fingerprint biometric technology and study ID cards are used to register the individual uptake of MDA.

Collection of individual-level treatment data allows the project to assess the impact that the proportion of individuals accepting medication at any one round of treatment (here-after referred to as MDA coverage) [27] and treatment compliance (the proportion of individuals swallowing treatment at each round of MDA) [28,29] has on STH prevalence and infection levels within treated communities.

Future research must develop new tools and new approaches to improve the impact of MDA via increasing coverage and individual compliance for the control of helminth infections. These include molecular epidemiological methods to quantify ‘who infects whom’ within households and villages and between villages, tools to allow the end-user to continue to monitor individual-level treatment data, studies to enhance the identification of the factors influencing non-compliance (these may vary widely from location to location and between different cultural settings), and methods of longitudinal follow up to measure compliance that do not increase the likelihood of non-participation due to intrusive technologies such as biometrics (e.g. fingerprinting, iris scanning or facial scanning).
421.Rationale: While the passage states that biometric registration is used and that individual-level treatment data allow assessment of coverage and compliance, it does not claim that biometrics alone 'ensures that all individuals are treated' or by itself enables interruption. The authors note benefits of tracking but also caution about intrusive biometric methods that may reduce participation. Thus biometrics are a useful tool for measurement and targeted follow-up but are not presented as the singular critical factor that guarantees transmission interruption.

421.Option: Expanding treatment to include all age groups, including adults and adolescents, ensures that transmission is interrupted even if some individuals are never treated
Evidence: More recent STH and schistosome infection integrated control interventions have sought to interrupt transmission by expanding treatment across a broader range of age groups including adults, known as community-wide treatment.
Published research indicates that the interruption of STH and schistosome transmission is unlikely to occur when only treating SAC and WRA [11].

This is of particular importance when considering hookworm, as the majority of infections are harboured by adults, displayed as a rising age-prevalence distribution which plateaus in older age classes [11].
421.Rationale: The passage supports expanding treatment to community-wide MDA because treating only school-aged children and women of reproductive age is insufficient, especially for species concentrated in adults (e.g., hookworm). However, the option's claim that expansion 'ensures' interruption even if some individuals are never treated is not supported. The text emphasizes that interruption depends heavily on treatment compliance patterns and that persistent non-compliers can prevent elimination. Therefore expanding age groups helps but does not guarantee interruption when some individuals remain untreated.

421.Option: Achieving high overall treatment coverage through annual mass drug administration rounds, regardless of individual adherence patterns, ensures long-term reduction in transmission
Evidence: Establishing overall MDA treatment coverage was performed using establishing denominator populations from baseline census as described in per the Geshiyaro protocol [31].

This suggested a substantial reduction in the probability of transmission elimination (between 23–43%) when comparing the observed adherence patterns which recorded partial non-compliance, with an assumption of randomness in who gets treated at each round with defined levels of coverage.

For a high level of MDA coverage, the likelihood of reaching transmission interruption was highly dependent on assumed compliance ranging from 90% in a random non-compliance setting to 0% when there existed a moderate fraction (25% of the population) of persistent non-compliers to treatment in the community [29].
421.Rationale: The passage indicates that overall coverage measurements are important, but it explicitly states that the likelihood of elimination is not determined solely by high coverage; it also depends critically on adherence patterns. High coverage 'regardless of individual adherence patterns' is therefore not sufficient — persistent non-compliers can negate the benefits of high nominal coverage.

421.Option: The presence of persistent non-compliers who consistently avoid treatment across multiple rounds, even in high-coverage settings, undermines transmission interruption
Evidence: There is also a growing number of studies exploring MDA acceptance within treated communities and evidence points towards untreated adult populations serving as a reservoir of infection, thus causing re-infection of treated and untreated individuals [11,14–16].

Between the limits of random acceptance of treatment, to never-treated over many rounds of MDA, a variety of possible patterns pertain for how many rounds of treatment are taken by individuals, and how past behaviour influences behaviour at the next round of MDA (the conditional probability of treatment [30]).

This suggested a substantial reduction in the probability of transmission elimination (between 23–43%) when comparing the observed adherence patterns which recorded partial non-compliance, with an assumption of randomness in who gets treated at each round with defined levels of coverage.

For a high level of MDA coverage, the likelihood of reaching transmission interruption was highly dependent on assumed compliance ranging from 90% in a random non-compliance setting to 0% when there existed a moderate fraction (25% of the population) of persistent non-compliers to treatment in the community [29].
Individuals that are not treated create a reservoir of infection within the community that will lead to a rapid re-emergence of infection after the cessation of MDA.

It is of utmost importance to the success of a deworming programme, particularly one aimed at interrupting transmission, that all individuals are treated and that no individuals are repeatedly missed at each round of MDA.
421.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies patterns of individual non-treatment — especially persistent non-compliers — as the critical determinant of whether transmission interruption is achievable. Modeling and empirical discussion show that even with high overall coverage, a moderate fraction of persistent non-compliers can reduce the probability of elimination to zero and maintain a reservoir that causes re-emergence. Therefore this factor most critically influences long-term transmission reduction.

421.Option: Reducing the mean intensity of infection across all age groups through repeated MDA rounds is the most critical factor for long-term transmission reduction
Evidence: Although prevalence is used as a key metric in many STH epidemiological studies, the intensity of infection is a much more important determinant of the morbidity induced by STH infection. As such it is a better marker of the impact of interventions.
Repeated rounds of treatment act to reduce the overall prevalence and intensity of infection.

However, we have shown in this paper how the non-compliance of a small group of individuals can affect reaching the ambitious goals set for reductions in both the prevalence and intensity of infection in a wide-scale government run deworming study based on mass drug administration and enhanced WaSH activities.

Increased aggregation of infection within those non-compliant individuals after two consecutive rounds of chemotherapy suggests that once prevalence has reached low levels across communities there is a need for MDA mop-up programmes to target and treat those that are non-compliant in order achieve the aim of interrupting the transmission of the soil transmitted helminths.
421.Rationale: While reducing mean intensity is important for morbidity and is an indicator of intervention impact, the passage emphasizes that non-compliance and aggregation of infection in untreated individuals can prevent transmission interruption even when intensity declines in much of the population. Thus reducing intensity alone is not presented as the single most critical factor for long-term transmission reduction in the presence of persistent non-compliers.

423.Option: The high incidence of rabies in wildlife populations indicates that eliminating human cases requires concurrent control of multiple reservoir species
Evidence: evidence suggests that domestic dogs are the only population essential for maintenance [10],[13],[16]: (1) phylogenetic data showed only a single southern Africa canid-associated variant (Africa 1b) circulating among different hosts [16]; (2) transmission networks suggested that, for wildlife hosts, within-species transmission cannot be sustained [16]; and (3) statistical inference indicated that cross-species transmission events from domestic dogs resulted in only relatively short-lived chains of transmission in wildlife with no evidence for persistence [10].

domestic dogs are the sole population responsible for rabies maintenance and main source of infection for humans throughout most of Africa and Asia and therefore control of dog rabies should eliminate the disease

An outstanding question relates to southern Africa. Earlier and recent evidence indicate that jackal species (Canis mesomelas and C. adustus) and bat-eared foxes (Otocyon megalotis) may maintain the canid variant in specific geographic loci in South Africa and Zimbabwe [2], [36]–[38], [45]–[50], but it is still not clear whether these cycles can be sustained over large spatial and temporal scales in the absence of dog rabies [13],[51],[52].
423.Rationale: The passage directly provides evidence that, in the study areas (e.g., Serengeti and much of Africa and Asia), wildlife do not sustain independent maintenance cycles and domestic dogs are the maintenance population. While it notes a possible exception in parts of southern Africa (jackals/bat-eared foxes) this is presented as an unresolved local issue rather than a general rule. Therefore the option's claim that high wildlife incidence indicates the need for concurrent multi-reservoir control is not supported as the primary factor; the passage emphasizes dog-maintained transmission in most regions.

423.Option: The high proportion of unowned dogs in African communities suggests that mass vaccination campaigns are impractical, but targeted interventions in high-risk areas could still be effective
Evidence: A common claim is that the majority of dogs in Africa are unowned ‘stray’ animals, and therefore inaccessible for parenteral vaccination. It is not hard to see why this perception has arisen - unrestrained dogs, without any apparent evidence of ownership, are commonly observed. Further investigation, however, usually reveals that the vast majority are owned, and at least one household claims some responsibility, including presentation for vaccination.

Published studies in Africa, which quantify the proportion of unowned dogs, are admittedly sparse, but all support this observation [56]–[58]. Capture-mark-recapture methodologies and household questionnaires used in African settings have all found consistently low estimates (Tunisia ∼7% [57], 1%, 8% and 11% in three sites in N'Djamena, Chad [56], and 1% in a peri-urban site in Tanzania [58]).

Given that most dogs are accessible for parenteral vaccination, high coverage can be achieved with well-planned vaccination campaigns. During pilot programmes in urban and rural Africa which have not charged owners for vaccination, coverages obtained have exceeded 60% [14],[17],[56].
423.Rationale: The passage contradicts the premise of this option by citing studies that show the vast majority of dogs are owned and that the proportion of unowned dogs is low (with specific estimates). It further states that high coverage can be achieved in mass campaigns. Thus the assertion that mass campaigns are impractical due to high unowned-dog proportions is not supported by the passage.

423.Option: The low basic reproductive number (R0) of rabies in domestic dog populations indicates that herd immunity can be achieved with minimal vaccination coverage, making elimination feasible
Evidence: evaluation of historical outbreak data from around the world and recent data from Tanzania indicate that R0 in domestic dog populations is consistently low (between 1.0 and 2.0) [12], confirming the feasibility of rabies elimination through vaccination in African domestic dog populations.

An important conclusion of this study was that in populations with rapid turnover (such as those in many African countries) at least 60% of the population must be vaccinated during annual campaigns to prevent coverage falling below Pcrit between campaigns.

A critical proportion of the population must be protected (Pcrit) to eliminate infection and this threshold can be calculated from the basic reproductive number (R0, defined as the average number of secondary infections caused by an infected individual in a susceptible population) [53].
423.Rationale: While the passage states that R0 in domestic dog populations is low (1.0–2.0), it does not claim that only ‘minimal’ vaccination coverage is required. It explicitly states that at least 60% coverage is needed in populations with rapid turnover to keep coverage above Pcrit between campaigns. Therefore the option's assertion that herd immunity requires minimal coverage is inaccurate; the low R0 supports feasibility but not the misleading notion of minimal vaccination coverage.

423.Option: The effectiveness of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) in preventing human rabies cases supports the feasibility of eliminating human cases through widespread PEP distribution
Evidence: Many countries in Asia, such as Thailand, Vietnam and Sri Lanka have greatly reduced human rabies deaths through increased PEP use, but at a very high cost [83]. In Vietnam, for example, deaths fell from 285 in 1996 to 82 in 2006 with administration of >600,000 PEP courses per year at an estimated cost of ∼$27 million/year [84].

The major component of the economic burden of rabies relates to high costs of PEP, which impacts both government and household budgets. With the phasing out of NTVs, many countries spend millions of dollars importing supplies of tissue-culture vaccine (∼$196 million USD pa [3]).
In rural Tanzania, dog vaccination campaigns led to a rapid and dramatic decline in demand for costly human PEP [14].

Poor households face difficulties raising funds which results in considerable financial hardship and substantial delays in PEP delivery [11],[28]. Shortages of PEP, which are frequent in much of Africa, further increase costs as bite victims are forced to travel to multiple centres to obtain treatment, also resulting in risky delays [11].
423.Rationale: Although PEP is effective at preventing individual human rabies cases and has reduced deaths in some countries, the passage emphasizes that reliance on widespread PEP is economically burdensome, often inaccessible, and not a sustainable strategy for elimination. The authors argue that controlling rabies at its source (dog vaccination) is more feasible and cost-effective. Therefore using PEP distribution alone as the basis for elimination is not supported as the strongest factor.

423.Option: The persistence of rabies transmission depends solely on domestic dog populations, making targeted dog vaccination a viable strategy for eliminating human cases
Evidence: From the perspective of human rabies, the vast majority of human cases (>90%) result from the bites of rabid domestic dogs [2] and occur in regions where domestic dogs are the principal maintenance host [3].

evidence suggests that domestic dogs are the only population essential for maintenance [10],[13],[16]: (1) phylogenetic data showed only a single southern Africa canid-associated variant (Africa 1b) circulating among different hosts [16]; (2) transmission networks suggested that, for wildlife hosts, within-species transmission cannot be sustained [16]; and (3) statistical inference indicated that cross-species transmission events from domestic dogs resulted in only relatively short-lived chains of transmission in wildlife with no evidence for persistence [10].

domestic dogs are the sole population responsible for rabies maintenance and main source of infection for humans throughout most of Africa and Asia and therefore control of dog rabies should eliminate the disease
Vaccination has eliminated canine rabies in many countries demonstrating the success of this concept [54].

Given that most dogs are accessible for parenteral vaccination, high coverage can be achieved with well-planned vaccination campaigns. During pilot programmes in urban and rural Africa which have not charged owners for vaccination, coverages obtained have exceeded 60% [14],[17],[56].
423.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that domestic dogs are the maintenance population and the main source of human cases in most of Africa and Asia, and presents empirical and phylogenetic evidence that wildlife do not sustain long-term transmission. It also documents successful elimination of canine rabies through vaccination and achievable high coverage in campaigns. These points directly support the conclusion that targeted dog vaccination is a viable and strongly supported strategy for eliminating human rabies cases.

424.Option: The variation in STH prevalence across sub-Divisions is driven by differences in the ecological zones (Wet vs. Dry), as these zones influence environmental transmission conditions.
Evidence: There are two major ecological zones in the country: (1) Wet Zone, which receives rain > 3,000 mm/year; and (2) Dry Zone, which receives rain < 2,000 mm/year [16].
Prevalence levels for any STH differed across sub-Divisions (P = 0.0014), with the highest frequency, up to 38.2% (95% CI: 17.8–63.8), in the Nataisiri sub-Division of the Central Division (Fig 3).

it is likely that variable performance of community-based LF-MDA by locality influenced the epidemiologic situation for STH, and that there were also innate geographical differences in the underlying intensity of transmission that influenced local reinfection rates between the treatments provided by community-based LF-MDA [28].
424.Rationale: While the passage describes distinct ecological zones (Wet vs Dry) and documents statistically significant differences in prevalence across sub-Divisions, it does not present direct evidence that the Wet/Dry ecological classification is the primary driver of the observed prevalence differences. The authors instead explicitly point to variable program performance and 'innate geographical differences' in transmission intensity as likely contributors. Because the passage lacks direct linkage of Wet vs Dry zones to the observed STH burden, this option overstates the evidence and is therefore incorrect.

424.Option: The variation in STH prevalence across sub-Divisions is best explained by differences in the frequency of deworming medication use reported by parents, which reflects community-level treatment coverage.
Evidence: Most of the consecutive annual LF-MDA rounds were successful, with around 65% population coverage, and led to termination of LF-MDA decisions in three Divisions of the country [6,7]: the final LF-MDA 7th round for the Western Division took place in 2009, and the 10th for the Central and 9th for the Northern in 2012.
In one-third of the study participants, the parents/guardians answered that they could recall previous ingestions of deworming medication by their children.

Whilst some uncertainties remain, as neither pre-LF-MDA baseline data nor school-level treatment coverages are available, it is likely that variable performance of community-based LF-MDA by locality influenced the epidemiologic situation for STH, and that there were also innate geographical differences in the underlying intensity of transmission that influenced local reinfection rates between the treatments provided by community-based LF-MDA [28].
424.Rationale: The passage does indicate that community-based LF-MDA coverage varied by locality and that program performance may have influenced local epidemiology, and it reports parental recall of deworming. However, the study explicitly notes the absence of school-level treatment-coverage data and pre-MDA baselines, limiting the ability to conclude that differences in reported deworming frequency fully explain sub-Division prevalence differences. Because the data to support this claim are incomplete and the authors point to other geographic/structural influences as likely contributors, this option is not the best-supported explanation.

424.Option: The logistical constraints of survey implementation, such as limited flights to Rotuma Island, likely introduced sampling bias that explains the variation in STH prevalence across sub-Divisions.
Evidence: In the Eastern Division, 20 schools were first selected via simple random sampling from 4 sub-Divisions, and all 4 schools of the Rotuma sub-Division were added, as the team had to stay in Rotuma Island for at least a week until they returned to Suva due to the limited flights.
All class 1–8 students of the selected schools in the Eastern Division whose parents consented via signing a consent form, were enrolled.

Despite STH’s public health importance, and continued efforts to control it in Fiji, nationally representative data remain unavailable at the program level, which hampers both effective implementation and evaluation of current and future interventions [9].
424.Rationale: The passage documents logistical constraints (limited flights) and describes the sampling procedure for Rotuma, but it does not present any evidence that these operational constraints produced sampling bias sufficient to explain the observed sub-Division differences in infection burden. The authors treat the Rotuma inclusion as a practical necessity and do not attribute prevalence variation to survey logistics. Therefore, this option is not supported by the text.

424.Option: Differences in access to piped, treated water from the Fiji Water Authority across sub-Divisions likely drive the observed variation in STH prevalence, indicating a structural environmental influence
Evidence: As for the main water source at home, those having piped water from the Fiji Water Authority had lower odds for any STH than children whose home water sources were either piped water from private or local sources or other.

Another major finding in our study is that having the main water supply from the Fiji Water Authority was associated with lower odds of any STH and hookworm infection compared with having it from any other source.

Water is a critical component of WASH resources [33], and according to our observations in the field, having the main water supply from the Fiji Water Authority, which is piped and treated water, provides not only quality-controlled water but also fewer interruptions of supply than being supplied from other sources. Therefore, being supplied by the Fiji Water Authority may be a surrogate for other WASH characteristics, such as availability of functioning washing stations, rather than being causally associated with infection [28].
424.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports an association between having piped, treated water from the Fiji Water Authority and lower odds of STH (and hookworm) infection, and further interprets this as a structural/environmental factor that may reflect broader WASH infrastructure (functioning washing stations, reliable supply). These statements directly support the idea that structural and environmental conditions (water infrastructure) influence geographic differences in infection burden, making this option the best-supported choice.

424.Option: The variation in STH prevalence across sub-Divisions is primarily due to differences in the frequency of handwashing and shoe-wearing behaviors reported by caregivers, which reflect individual-level hygiene practices.
Evidence: Children who were reported to wash their hands and to wear shoes ‘usually’ had lower odds of being “any STH eggs” positive than students who were reported to wash their hands and to wear shoes less frequently such as ‘not always but sometimes’ or ‘not at all’.

A statistically significant difference existed between children regularly wearing shoes and children who did not wear shoes or did so only occasionally. The former had only half the likelihood of being infected by any STH, implying that shoe-wearing is associated with lower prevalence levels of any STH.
424.Rationale: The study does find that certain individual behaviors (handwashing, shoe-wearing) are associated with lower odds of infection, but the question asks which factor most strongly suggests that geographic differences are driven by structural/environmental conditions rather than individual behaviors alone. Because the passage presents both individual behavior associations and explicit evidence pointing to structural WASH/infrastructure (piped treated water) and geographic program differences as drivers, individual behavior explanations alone are insufficient to account for the location-linked differences. Thus this option is not the best-supported answer.

425.Option: The inclusion of studies with different study designs, such as case-control and prospective cohorts, likely contributed to heterogeneity by introducing selection bias, which may reduce the generalizability of the assay's performance in routine clinical practice
Evidence: Three studies were retrospective while the remaining were prospective.
one had a case-control design [9].
we assessed four studies as taking a high risk for bias, because they didn’t enroll participants randomly or consecutively
425.Rationale: The passage documents that included studies used different designs (retrospective, prospective, and at least one case-control) and that several studies had high risk of bias in patient selection. Variation in study design and non-random/non-consecutive enrollment can produce selection bias and systematic differences in who is sampled, which in turn contributes to between-study heterogeneity in diagnostic accuracy estimates and limits how generalizable the pooled performance is to routine clinical settings.

425.Option: Differences in the reference standard used across studies, particularly the use of PCR versus culture, likely contributed to heterogeneity by introducing misclassification bias, which may compromise the assay's validity in settings where culture is not available
Evidence: When we discuss vanA and vanB simultaneously, named vanA and vanB group, Mycobacterial culture was defined as the gold standard. When we discuss vanA or vanB separately, named vanA group and vanB group, the golden standard was defined as mycobacterial culture and PCR.
In the reference standard area, most studies had a low risk of bias, as they stated that the results of the reference standard were interpreted without knowing the index test results.
425.Rationale: Although the passage notes that different reference standards were used (culture alone versus culture plus PCR), it also states that most studies had low risk of bias in the reference standard domain. The authors did not identify reference-standard variation as a primary source of heterogeneity; instead they pointed to sample types and vanA/vanB discrepancies. Therefore, while differing reference standards could theoretically introduce misclassification, the passage does not present it as the most likely contributor to the observed heterogeneity.

425.Option: The presence of vanA and vanB resistance genes in different patient populations, particularly during outbreaks with varying genetic prevalence, likely contributed to heterogeneity by altering the assay's sensitivity and specificity, which may lead to overestimation of performance in low-prevalence settings
Evidence: Sensitivity analysis demonstrated that the study of Zabicka contributes to heterogeneity. It could be influenced by the factor that the experiment performed during a VanA E. faecium outbreak, as the report of Dekeyser et al. [24], and none of the patients was colonized with VanB enterococci.
The discrepancies between GeneXpert vanA/vanB detecting vanA and vanB might be a source of heterogeneity.

That there were more false-positive results in vanB group may be attributed to the presence of genes in several species of aerobic and anaerobic bacteria that were highly similar to the vanB sequences
425.Rationale: The authors explicitly link heterogeneity to differences in vanA/vanB prevalence across studies (noting the Zabicka study conducted during a VanA outbreak with no VanB colonization) and to discrepant performance for vanA versus vanB. Changes in the underlying genetic prevalence or outbreak context can change apparent sensitivity/specificity (and false-positive or false-negative counts), so this factor plausibly and directly contributed to heterogeneity and could cause performance estimates from high-prevalence or outbreak settings to be optimistic when applied to low-prevalence clinical settings.

425.Option: Use of a random-effects model to account for heterogeneity may have inflated the precision of the pooled estimates, leading to an overstatement of the assay's diagnostic accuracy, particularly for vanB detection, which could mislead clinical implementation
Evidence: To account for this heterogeneity, a random effects model was used to synthesis the identified studies in our analysis, which potentially increases the probability of type I error.
425.Rationale: The passage states that a random-effects model was used and that this choice "potentially increases the probability of type I error," but it does not claim the random-effects approach inflated precision. In fact, random-effects models generally produce wider CIs (less precision) than fixed-effects when heterogeneity exists. The passage identifies sample types and vanA/vanB discrepancies (not the modeling choice) as likely heterogeneity sources. Thus the specific claim that using a random-effects model inflated precision and overstated accuracy—especially for vanB—is not supported by the text or standard meta-analytic principles.

425.Option: Variation in specimen types, particularly stool and rectal swabs, likely contributed to heterogeneity by increasing false-positive vanB results due to cross-reactivity with anaerobic bacteria, which may reduce the assay's reliability in clinical settings where such specimens are common
Evidence: The result of meta regression (Table 2) indicated that sample types might be one possible source of heterogeneity.
Several FP vanB results may be attributed to the presence of genes in several species of aerobic and anaerobic bacteria that were highly similar to the vanB sequences [5,7].
stool and rectal swabs might be the harbors where anaerobic microbes were commonly checked, which increased the risks of detecting false-positive vanB results [11].
425.Rationale: The authors' meta-regression pointed to specimen type as a possible source of heterogeneity, and they specifically describe how stool and rectal swabs can harbor organisms with vanB-like sequences, increasing false positives for vanB. This mechanism directly explains between-study variation in specificity and positive predictive value, and implies that in clinical settings where these specimen types are common, the assay's reliability—particularly for vanB detection—may be lower and may require confirmatory testing.

426.Option: Compare the geometric mean levels of key cytokines (e.g., IL-10, MCP-1, IFN-α2) between vaccinated and placebo recipients at baseline and during acute breakthrough illness to infer changes in immune protection over time
Evidence: As a post-hoc exploratory analysis, we tested blood samples from selected participants at one month post-dose 3 for baseline cytokine assessment, and during the acute phase of febrile illness from participants who required hospitalization for virological confirmation of dengue infection in the active phase, and during the first and beginning of the second year of long-term follow up.

Of the 265 breakthrough cases occurring up to 28 November 2014, 207 serum samples (with sufficient volume for analysis) were available for immune profile assessment (S1 Table). An additional 40 samples were taken one month post-dose 3 (17 and 23 in the CYD-TDV and placebo groups, respectively) to assess baseline cytokine levels.

A limitation of these studies, including our present work, is the fact that samples are often collected at a single time point and at different times of the clinical course of infection (between 1 and 15 days after fever onset). This heterogeneity in sampling time could lead to misleading results due the transient expression of circulating cytokines.
Of the 15 cytokines with median levels above the LLOQ, only two, IL-1Ra and MCP-1, significantly differed between the treatment groups.
426.Rationale: While the study did compare baseline (one month post-dose 3) and acute-phase cytokine levels and reported geometric means for some markers, using only cross-sectional comparisons of cytokine geometric means between vaccinated and placebo groups at baseline and during acute illness does not directly assess waning of immune protection over time since vaccination. The passage highlights that samples were limited (40 baseline, 207 acute) and that single time-point sampling and heterogeneous timing can be misleading because cytokine levels are transient. Therefore this approach is insufficient to infer temporal decline in protection in a population with varying prior exposure histories.

426.Option: Conduct a time-stratified analysis of immune profiles in breakthrough cases, stratified by age and prior exposure indicators, to detect shifts in cytokine signatures associated with waning protection
Evidence: The comparisons between the CYD-TDV and placebo groups were stratified as follows: i) all hospitalized cases, irrespective of phase or severity; ii) severe or non-severe cases only; iii) active phase or hospital phase only; iv) cases aged <9 years or aged ≥9 years.

Partial least squares discriminant analyses (PLS-DA) were performed for cytokines that appeared to be differentially expressed between baseline and acute phase of dengue illness to analyze the overall immune profile between: i) the CYD-TDV and placebo groups, ii) CYD14 and CYD15 studies, and iii) the two age groups (<9 years or ≥9 years).
Severe dengue is most often observed in previously infected subjects undergoing secondary infection with a different DENV serotype.

Since IL-10 levels have been shown to be higher in secondary dengue infections than in primary infections or in healthy controls [31], it can be hypothesized that, in children aged <9 years, infections occurring in the CYD-TDV group are akin to secondary infections where vaccination may be considered as the primary infection/challenge and breakthrough disease as secondary infection.

A limitation of these studies, including our present work, is the fact that samples are often collected at a single time point and at different times of the clinical course of infection (between 1 and 15 days after fever onset). This heterogeneity in sampling time could lead to misleading results due the transient expression of circulating cytokines.
426.Rationale: A time-stratified analysis of immune profiles (i.e., organizing breakthrough-case samples by time since vaccination or by calendar/time-in-study) and further stratifying by age and indicators of prior dengue exposure would allow detection of changes in cytokine signatures over time while accounting for important effect modifiers. The passage documents use of stratified comparisons (age, phase, severity), use of multivariate methods (PLS-DA) to compare immune profiles, and emphasizes the importance of accounting for prior infection status (secondary infections associated with different cytokine patterns). It also notes the limitation of single time-point sampling, supporting the need for explicit time-stratified analysis. Therefore this approach best preserves validity while addressing waning in a heterogeneous-exposure population.

426.Option: Stratify breakthrough cases by time since vaccination and compare the proportion of severe versus non-severe cases across strata to identify temporal patterns in disease severity
Evidence: The comparisons between the CYD-TDV and placebo groups were stratified as follows: i) all hospitalized cases, irrespective of phase or severity; ii) severe or non-severe cases only; iii) active phase or hospital phase only; iv) cases aged <9 years or aged ≥9 years.
Severe dengue is most often observed in previously infected subjects undergoing secondary infection with a different DENV serotype.

A limitation of these studies, including our present work, is the fact that samples are often collected at a single time point and at different times of the clinical course of infection (between 1 and 15 days after fever onset). This heterogeneity in sampling time could lead to misleading results due the transient expression of circulating cytokines.
Of the 15 cytokines with median levels above the LLOQ, only two, IL-1Ra and MCP-1, significantly differed between the treatment groups.
426.Rationale: Comparing proportions of severe versus non-severe cases by time-since-vaccination strata can detect changes in clinical protection over time, which is a useful epidemiologic approach. However, in a population with varying prior exposure histories this simple stratification does not account for the strong modifying effect of prior dengue infection on disease severity and immune response. The passage emphasizes that severe dengue is most often observed in previously infected subjects and documents age- and exposure-related cytokine differences; therefore analyses that do not adjust or stratify by prior exposure indicators (serostatus/history) risk confounding and misinterpretation. Additionally, the study notes limitations of single time-point immune measures and transient cytokine expression, arguing for immune-profile-based time-stratified analyses rather than relying solely on clinical severity counts.

427.Option: The integration of multiple NTD screening into a single activity allowed for the identification of co-occurring cases, suggesting shared environmental risk factors across diseases
Evidence: Taking into account the 2013 resolution of the World Health Assembly, we set out to develop the integrated screening of BU, leprosy and yaws in the district of Lalo in southern Benin.

In the implementation of this integrated management of NTD with cutaneous manifestations, we used the approach proposed by Mitja et al [21]. We identified co-endemic communities; we trained the team; carried out social mobilization; performed mobile medical consultations; conducted the screening and management of cases; mapping and held review meetings.
In our study, we screened 1,106 people to detect only 15 BU cases (1.4%) and 3 cases of leprosy (0.3%).
This study seems to have the advantage of being efficient since, with the same resources, at least 3 types of NTDs were actively sought at the same time.
427.Rationale: The passage documents that integrated screening allowed detection of multiple NTDs and identification of co-endemic communities, which can suggest shared environmental risk factors. However, the question asks which methodological feature of the case-based observational design most directly supports tracing transmission patterns and identifying shared risk factors across cases. While integration enables simultaneous identification of co-occurring cases, it is a programmatic approach rather than the specific methodological feature (e.g., spatial analysis) that most directly supports tracing transmission patterns. Therefore this option is not the best or most direct answer to the question.

427.Option: Geospatial mapping of confirmed cases enabled visualization of clustering patterns to infer shared transmission risks and environmental exposures
Evidence: Mapping

The base maps were loaded on the DIVA-GIS program (http://www.diva-gis.org/) and completed by base maps of the “Programme National de Lutte contre la Lèpre et l’Ulcère de Buruli” (PNLLUB). The map was made using the software QGIS version 1.8.0.
The mapping of confirmed BU and leprosy cases is presented in Fig 2.
427.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes geospatial mapping of confirmed cases. Mapping is the methodological feature that most directly allows investigators to visualize spatial clustering and infer whether cases share locations or environmental exposures — key for tracing potential transmission patterns and identifying shared risk factors across cases in a case-based observational investigation.

427.Option: Standardized education and awareness sessions using WHO posters ensured consistent case definitions across all screening sites, enabling reliable comparison of clinical features between cases
Evidence: After that, the trained health workers and community health volunteers provided clear explanation on the disease by using the WHO posters on BU, leprosy and yaws.
The training of the health workers covered basic epidemiology, clinical diagnosis, differential diagnosis, complications, social consequences and treatment of these three diseases were discussed.
The training for the community health volunteers and teachers was mainly focused on clinical diagnosis to increase their capacity to suspect cases.
427.Rationale: The passage shows that education sessions and WHO posters were used and that health workers were trained in clinical diagnosis, which promotes consistent recognition of conditions. However, the option overstates by claiming these sessions 'ensured consistent case definitions' across sites; the text does not state that formal standardized case definitions were enforced by posters alone. Moreover, while such standardization aids comparability of clinical assessments, it is not the methodological feature most directly used to trace transmission patterns and shared exposures (geospatial mapping is). Thus this option is not correct as the primary answer.

427.Option: The use of trained health workers and community volunteers across multiple villages allowed for rapid identification and reporting of suspected cases, facilitating timely outbreak response
Evidence: We trained the health workers (nurses, midwives and laboratory technicians), teachers and community health volunteers in the integrated control and management of leprosy, BU and yaws.
Thirty community health volunteers are actively involved in skin NTDs control in this district.
The training for the community health volunteers and teachers was mainly focused on clinical diagnosis to increase their capacity to suspect cases.
In accordance with this schedule, the trained health workers went to villages and primary schools.

Moreover, this integrated management allowed the community-based screening team to maintain continuous monitoring; to suspect cases of yaws; to detect other NTD (lymphatic filariasis) and other dermatological conditions.
427.Rationale: The passage supports that trained health workers and volunteers increased capacity to suspect and monitor cases across villages, which facilitates identification and reporting and improves timeliness of detection. However, the question asked for the methodological feature of the case-based observational design that most directly supports tracing transmission patterns and identifying shared risk factors. Although rapid identification is important for outbreak response, it is the spatial mapping of cases that most directly supports tracing patterns and shared exposures. Therefore this option is not the best choice.

427.Option: The centralized reporting of results to the PNLLUB and district health authorities enabled real-time monitoring and immediate follow-up of suspected cases, supporting outbreak containment
Evidence: The results of this study were given to the district health authorities and the “Programme National de Lutte contre la Lèpre et l’Ulcère de Buruli” (PNLLUB) for decision-making.

The ownership and adherence of the health authorities to this approach was indicated by the active participation of the PNLLUB, the health authorities, the local elected officials and the community health volunteers.
427.Rationale: The passage indicates that study results were shared with PNLLUB and local health authorities for decision-making, which supports follow-up and programmatic response. However, it does not describe real-time monitoring or immediate follow-up functionality. More importantly, centralized reporting alone does not most directly enable tracing transmission patterns or identifying shared risk factors across cases — spatial mapping does. Thus this option is not correct as the primary methodological feature relevant to the question.

428.Option: Use of a pre-specified sample size of 300 patients to ensure sufficient power and allow for early stopping based on interim results
Evidence: Total sample sizes (across both treatments) of between 150 and 300 patients prove to be adequate in many cases, the precise value depending on both the magnitude of the treatment advantage and the nature of the ordinal scale.
Typical sample sizes when GOST is employed are in the range 150–300 (totalled over both treatment groups).
428.Rationale: While adequate sample size is necessary for statistical power and influences the ability to detect treatment effects, the question asks which feature most directly supports causal inference while maintaining efficiency. Pre-specifying a sample size (e.g., 300) pertains to power and planning but does not itself ensure comparability of baseline characteristics or eliminate confounding — those are achieved by randomisation. The passage supports that sample sizes of 150–300 are typical for efficiency, but it does not claim that choice of sample size alone is the primary feature enabling causal inference.

428.Option: Implementation of a fixed number of interim analyses (e.g., 3) to minimize the risk of false-positive findings and ensure trial integrity
Evidence: The trial will be monitored using a series of up to 20 interim analyses, equally spaced by newly accrued patient responses.

Setting 20 interim analyses for GOST is a subjective choice of the authors achieving a much quicker reaction to the message of the data than setting just 3 or 4 interim analyses while being more practical than updating the sequential plot every time a Day D report is received.
428.Rationale: The passage advocates a relatively large number of planned interim analyses (up to 20) for responsiveness; it contrasts that with setting just 3 or 4 and indicates 20 was chosen deliberately. While prespecified interim monitoring protects trial integrity and controls error rates if planned correctly, the specific suggestion of a small fixed number like '3' is not supported by the GOST description and is not the feature that most directly enables causal inference. Randomisation (and stratification) is the key feature for causal inference; interim analyses affect efficiency and error control but are secondary to random assignment for causal validity.

428.Option: Use of a binary outcome measure (e.g., alive vs. dead) to simplify data collection and reduce the number of required interim analyses
Evidence: GOST can be applied to such binary data, and equations E4 in S1 Text provide simplified versions of the test statistics.

However, binary data are less informative than the ordinal version of the data, and it will now take 520 patient responses to ensure that V eventually reaches the value where the stopping boundaries in Fig 1 meet.
The inflation in sample size due to dichotomising the ordinal scale is a factor of 1.18: an 18% increase in sample size.
428.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that binary outcomes are less informative than ordinal outcomes and increase required sample size (i.e., reduce statistical efficiency). Thus using a binary outcome does not improve efficiency and is not the feature most directly supporting causal inference. Randomisation remains the primary enabler of causal inference; dichotomising the outcome is actually detrimental to efficiency per the text.

428.Option: Randomization of patients to treatment arms with a 1:1 ratio and stratification by treatment center to ensure balance in prognostic factors
Evidence: Patients will be randomised between an experimental treatment (E) and standard care (S), stratified by treatment centre and perhaps by one or two other key prognostic factors.
Usually the allocation ratio will be set to 1:1 for simplicity and because expected sample sizes are minimised if this choice is made [3].
They also choose the allocation ratio and any stratification factors. The rest is as presented above.
428.Rationale: Randomisation is the mechanism that most directly supports causal inference by ensuring groups are comparable on both measured and unmeasured confounders. The passage specifically describes randomisation with stratification by centre (and key prognostic factors), which further ensures balance on important covariates and can improve precision. The 1:1 allocation is noted to minimise expected sample sizes (statistical efficiency). Together, randomisation, appropriate allocation ratio, and stratification most directly support drawing causal conclusions while maintaining efficiency.

428.Option: Use of a pre-specified ordinal outcome scale with fixed follow-up and sequential analysis based on actual accrued data
Evidence: The primary patient response will be the status of the patient, D days after randomization, classified into one of k outcome groups, C1, …, Ck.
Use of a response that is available after a short and fixed duration of follow-up reduces the risk of loss to follow-up and is essential if the trial is to yield an early conclusion.
GOST is presented for the case of an ordinal response because expected sample sizes will be reduced if more than two outcome categories can be reliably identified [3].
The trial will be monitored using a series of up to 20 interim analyses, equally spaced by newly accrued patient responses.
428.Rationale: A pre-specified ordinal outcome with fixed follow-up and sequential monitoring clearly enhances efficiency (more informative outcomes, reduced sample size, timely conclusions) and trial practicality. However, while this feature supports efficient estimation and early decision-making, it is not the most direct mechanism for ensuring causal inference — that role is primarily fulfilled by randomisation and stratification. Therefore, although Option 4 is important for efficiency, it is not the most direct support for causal inference as asked in the question.

429.Option: The fact that L-AmB was effective in 80% of cases due to L. infantum suggests that the drug induces a strong host immune response that is sustained over time, contributing to long-term protection
Evidence: That efficacy was high when TL was due to L. infantum (approximately 80%) and low when it was due to other identified species (around 30%) provides some substance to this assumption.
To our knowledge, no study has systematically evaluated skin penetration of L-AmB following systemic administration in human subjects.
Parasite “resistance” to L-AmB in TL may be another important parameter to explain poor efficacy in this context.
429.Rationale: The passage documents higher observed efficacy with L. infantum (~80%) but does not attribute that difference to L-AmB inducing a sustained host immune response. Instead, the authors discuss alternative explanations such as species-related differences in drug susceptibility, skin penetration, parasite resistance or dormant parasite states. There is no textual support for the claim that the drug induces a lasting immune response that explains long-term protection, so this option over-interprets the observed association.

429.Option: The high rate of relapse among patients infected with Viannia subgenus species suggests that these parasites may have a higher replication rate, leading to faster disease progression and treatment failure
Evidence: Five patients experienced a relapse after a median of 6 months [range 3–27] post treatment. Of the five patients who relapsed, four (80%) were infected by a Leishmania species belonging to the Viannia subgenus.
Parasite “resistance” to L-AmB in TL may be another important parameter to explain poor efficacy in this context.
A dormant metabolic state of parasites in skin lesions may also contribute to the suboptimal effectiveness of L-AmB in CL
429.Rationale: The passage does report that 4 of 5 relapses were caused by Viannia subgenus species, which indicates an association between relapses and that subgenus. However, inferring a higher replication rate as the mechanism is speculative and not supported by the text. The authors propose other mechanisms (parasite resistance, dormant metabolic states, species-related susceptibility differences) rather than faster replication. Therefore the specific mechanistic claim about replication rate is not supported by the passage.

429.Option: The lack of association between immunosuppression and treatment outcome suggests that host immune status does not influence the effectiveness of L-AmB, indicating that the drug's mechanism is entirely independent of host immunity
Evidence: Among immunocompromised patients (5/43; 12%), three had human immunodeficiency infection (HIV) infection and two received prolonged corticosteroid therapy and/or immunosuppressive treatment for autoimmune diseases.
No other clinical or biological factors were associated with the outcome after L-AmB therapy.
Apart from the impact of L. infantum, we found no obvious epidemiological, clinical or laboratory parameter associated with outcome after treatment with L-AmB in TL.
429.Rationale: While the study did not identify immunosuppression as a predictor of outcome, the small number of immunocompromised patients (5/43) and the observational retrospective design limit the ability to conclude that host immune status does not influence effectiveness. The passage does not claim the drug acts entirely independent of host immunity; it simply reports no association was detected in this cohort. Therefore the broad statement that host immunity does not influence L-AmB effectiveness is not supported.

429.Option: Infection with L. infantum is associated with a significantly higher likelihood of complete healing, suggesting species-specific differences in parasite susceptibility or immune evasion mechanisms
Evidence: In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).
That efficacy was high when TL was due to L. infantum (approximately 80%) and low when it was due to other identified species (around 30%) provides some substance to this assumption.
A species-related difference in drug susceptibility is a third potentially important determinant of outcome in TL and VL.
429.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that infection with L. infantum was independently associated with better outcomes (multivariable OR 5.8) and that efficacy was ~80% for L. infantum versus much lower for other species. The authors explicitly discuss species-related differences in drug susceptibility as a likely determinant of outcome. These points directly support the option that species-specific host–pathogen interactions (e.g., differing susceptibility or immune evasion) influence treatment outcomes beyond pharmacologic properties of the drug.
